title.zh: 卷四十九 王充王符仲長統列傳
title.en: Volume 49: Biographies of Wang Chong, Wang Fu, Zhongchang Tong
title.raw: 卷四十九 王充王符仲長統列傳 Volume 49: Biographies of Wang Chong, Wang Fu, Zhongchang Tong

sentences.zh: 王充字仲任，會稽上虞人也，其先自魏郡元城徙焉。

translations.literal: Wang Chong, courtesy name Zhongren, was a man of Shangyu in Kuaiji; his ancestors had moved there from Yuancheng in Wei Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chong, whose courtesy name was Zhongren, came from Shangyu in Kuaiji; his family had originally relocated there from Yuancheng in Wei Commandery.

sentences.zh: 充少孤，鄉里稱孝。

translations.literal: Chong was orphaned young, and his neighborhood praised him for filial devotion.
translations.idiomatic: He lost his father while still a boy, and the people of his district commended him as a dutiful son.

sentences.zh: 後到京師，受業太學，[一]師事扶風班彪。

translations.literal: Later he went to the capital, studied at the Imperial Academy, and took Ban Biao of Fufeng as his teacher.
translations.idiomatic: He later traveled to the capital, enrolled at the Imperial Academy, and studied under Ban Biao of Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 好博覽而不守章句。

translations.literal: He loved wide reading and did not cling to textual glosses.
translations.idiomatic: He read broadly and refused to be bound by narrow philological literalism.

sentences.zh: 家貧無書，常游洛陽市肆，閱所賣書，一見輒能誦憶，遂博通觿流百家之言。

translations.literal: His family was poor and owned no books, so he would wander the bookstalls of Luoyang, reading whatever was for sale; a single glance was enough for him to memorize it, and in this way he came to master the doctrines of the hundred schools.
translations.idiomatic: Too poor to buy books, he haunted the Luoyang market stalls and read every volume on offer; one look sufficed to fix a text in memory, until he had absorbed the teachings of every school.

sentences.zh: 後歸鄉里，屏居教授。

translations.literal: Later he returned to his home district, withdrew from public life, and taught.
translations.idiomatic: He then went home, lived in seclusion, and made his living as a teacher.

sentences.zh: 仕郡為功曹，以數諫爭不合去。

translations.literal: He served his commandery as a merit clerk but left office because he repeatedly remonstrated and his advice was not heeded.
translations.idiomatic: He held the post of merit clerk in the commandery but resigned after his repeated admonitions fell on deaf ears.

sentences.zh: 注[一]袁山松《書》：「充幼聰朗。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yuan Shansong's Book: "In youth Chong was bright and quick-witted.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] to Yuan Shansong's Book: "As a boy, Wang Chong was clever and alert.

sentences.zh: 詣太學，觀天子臨辟雍，作六儒論。」

translations.literal: He went to the Imperial Academy, watched the Son of Heaven attend the Hall of the Circular Moat, and composed the 'Treatise on the Six Scholars.'"
translations.idiomatic: He entered the Imperial Academy, witnessed the emperor's ceremony at the Hall of the Circular Moat, and wrote the 'Treatise on the Six Scholars.'"

sentences.zh: 充好論說，始若詭異，終有理實。

translations.literal: Chong loved disputation: at first his views seemed strange, but in the end they proved coherent and well grounded.
translations.idiomatic: He delighted in debate—what began sounding outlandish always resolved into sound reasoning.

sentences.zh: 以為俗儒守文，多失其真，乃閉門潛思，絕慶吊之禮，戶牖牆壁各置刀筆。

translations.literal: He believed that vulgar scholars clung to the letter of the text and often missed the truth, so he shut his doors and pondered in secret, cut himself off from congratulations and condolences, and set knife and stylus in every window, wall, and partition.
translations.idiomatic: He held that pedantic Confucians, slaves to the letter, routinely lost the genuine meaning; he therefore withdrew, reflected in solitude, forswore social calls of congratulation or condolence, and stationed brush and knife at every window and along the walls to write whenever thought struck.

sentences.zh: 箸《論衡》八十五篇，二十餘萬言，[一]釋物類同異，正時俗嫌疑。

translations.literal: He wrote the Lunheng in eighty-five chapters, more than two hundred thousand characters in all, [1] explaining similarities and differences among things and correcting the doubts and errors of his age.
translations.idiomatic: He composed the Lunheng in eighty-five pian—over two hundred thousand characters [1]—to sort out how kinds of things agree or differ and to settle the confusions of common opinion.

sentences.zh: 注[一]袁山松《書》曰：「充所作《論衡》，中土未有傳者，蔡邕入吳始得之，恆秘玩以為談助。

translations.literal: Comment [1] Yuan Shansong's Book says: "The Lunheng that Chong wrote had not yet circulated in the Central Plain; Cai Yong obtained it only after entering Wu, and he constantly kept it secret and prized it as material for conversation.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Yuan Shansong's Book records: "Wang Chong's Lunheng had not reached the heartland; when Cai Yong came south into Wu he acquired a copy and hoarded it as a private treasure to enliven his talk.

sentences.zh: 其後王朗為會稽太守，又得其書，及還許下，時人稱其才進。

translations.literal: Later, when Wang Lang became governor of Kuaiji, he too obtained the work, and when he returned to Xu below [the capital], people of the time said his literary talent had advanced.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Lang later served as governor of Kuaiji, he also got hold of the book, and after he returned to Xu people remarked that his literary powers had markedly improved.

sentences.zh: 或曰，不見異人，當得異書。

translations.literal: Some said, 'If you do not meet an extraordinary man, you should obtain an extraordinary book.'
translations.idiomatic: People said, 'Without encountering a remarkable person, one may still chance upon a remarkable book.'

sentences.zh: 問之，果以論衡之益，由是遂見傳焉。

translations.literal: When questioned, it turned out that the benefit of the Lunheng was indeed the reason, and from that time the text began to circulate.
translations.idiomatic: Asked to explain, they traced the improvement to the Lunheng—after which the work began to spread.

sentences.zh: 」《抱朴子》曰：「時人嫌蔡邕得異書，或搜求其帳中隱處，果得論衡，抱數卷持去。

translations.literal: The Baopuzi says: "People resented Cai Yong for possessing a rare book; some searched the hidden places in his curtains and actually found the Lunheng, snatched several fascicles, and carried them off.
translations.idiomatic: The Baopuzi relates: "Some begrudged Cai Yong his unusual find and rummaged through the recesses of his bed-curtains until they turned up the Lunheng, seized a few bundles, and made off with them.

sentences.zh: 邕丁寧之曰：『唯我與爾共之，勿廣也。』」

translations.literal: Yong urgently told them: 'Share this only between you and me—do not broadcast it.'"
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong urgently cautioned them, 'Let this stay between us—do not spread it abroad.'"

sentences.zh: 刺史董勤辟為從事，轉治中，自免還家。

translations.literal: Inspector Dong Qin summoned him as an attendant clerk; he was transferred to administrative supervisor, then resigned and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: The provincial inspector Dong Qin appointed him attendant clerk; he was promoted to administrative supervisor, then left office of his own accord and went home.

sentences.zh: 友人同郡謝夷吾上書薦充才學，[一]肅宗特詔公車征，病不行。

translations.literal: His fellow townsman Xie Yiwu of the same commandery submitted a memorial recommending Chong's talent and learning; [1] Emperor Zhang issued a special edict summoning him by imperial carriage, but he was ill and did not go.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Yiwu, a countryman, memorialized to recommend Chong's erudition; [1] Emperor Zhang therefore issued a special summons by the imperial carriage, but Chong pleaded illness and declined to travel.

sentences.zh: 年漸七十，志力衰耗，乃造養性書十六篇，裁節嗜欲，頤神自守。

translations.literal: As he gradually approached seventy, his ambition and strength waned, so he composed sixteen chapters on nurturing life, moderating appetites, and nourishing the spirit to preserve himself.
translations.idiomatic: When he neared seventy, vigor and resolve failed; he then wrote sixteen chapters on cultivating longevity, curbing desire, and quieting the mind to sustain himself.

sentences.zh: 永元中，病卒於家。

translations.literal: In the Yongyuan era he died of illness at home.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongyuan reign he succumbed to illness in his own house.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承《書》曰：「夷吾薦充曰：『充之天才，非學所加，雖前世孟軻、孫卿，近漢楊雄、劉向、司馬遷，不能過也。』」

translations.literal: Comment [1] Xie Cheng's Book says: "Yiwu recommended Chong, saying: 'Chong's natural genius is not something study can supply; even the Meng Ke and Xun Qing of former ages, or in recent Han times Yang Xiong, Liu Xiang, and Sima Qian, could not surpass him.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Xie Cheng's Book quotes Xie Yiwu's recommendation: 'Wang Chong's innate brilliance outstrips what mere learning can yield; not even ancient Mengzi or Xunzi, nor Han writers such as Yang Xiong, Liu Xiang, and Sima Qian, could exceed him.'"

sentences.zh: 王符字節信，安定臨涇人也。

translations.literal: Wang Fu, courtesy name Jiexin, was a man of Linjing in Anding.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu, whose courtesy name was Jiexin, hailed from Linjing in Anding Commandery.

sentences.zh: 少好學，有志操，與馬融、竇章、張衡、崔瑗等友善。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning, had integrity and resolve, and was on friendly terms with Ma Rong, Dou Zhang, Zhang Heng, Cui Yuan, and others.
translations.idiomatic: From his youth he was devoted to study, held himself to high standards, and counted Ma Rong, Dou Zhang, Zhang Heng, and Cui Yuan among his friends.

sentences.zh: 安定俗鄙庶孽，[一]而符無外家，為鄉人所賤。

translations.literal: Anding custom despised children of concubines; [1] Fu had no maternal kin, and the villagers looked down on him.
translations.idiomatic: Local custom in Anding scorned offspring of concubines; [1] because Wang Fu lacked connections on his mother's side, his neighbors held him in contempt.

sentences.zh: 自和、安之後，世務游宦，當塗者更相薦引，而符獨耿介不同於俗，以此遂不得升進。

translations.literal: From the He and An reigns onward, the world pursued office through wandering service; those in power recommended one another in turn, but Fu alone remained upright and aloof from vulgar ways, and for this reason he never advanced.
translations.idiomatic: After the He and An eras, men chased careers through itinerant posts and the powerful traded recommendations, while Wang Fu stood aloof and refused to play that game, and so he was never promoted.

sentences.zh: 志意蘊憤，乃隱居著書三十餘篇，以譏當時失得，不欲章顯其名，故號曰潛夫論。

translations.literal: Bearing his grievance in silence, he withdrew and wrote more than thirty chapters satirizing the faults of the day; unwilling to display his name, he styled the work Qianfu lun (Discourses of a Hidden Man).
translations.idiomatic: Seething with thwarted purpose, he retired and composed over thirty essays exposing contemporary wrongs; unwilling to reveal his identity, he published them under the title Discourses of a Hidden Man.

sentences.zh: 其指訐時短，討□物情，[二]足以觀見當時風政，著其五篇云爾。

translations.literal: Their purpose is to expose the shortcomings of the times and to analyze human dispositions; [2] they suffice to show the customs and government of the age—I present five chapters as examples.
translations.idiomatic: They attack the vices of the hour and probe how people feel; [2] they open a window on policy and manners—I excerpt five pieces here by way of illustration.

sentences.zh: 注[一]何休注《公羊傳》云：「孽，賤也。」

translations.literal: Comment [1] He Xiu's commentary on the Gongyang Commentary says: "Ni means 'base.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] He Xiu's gloss on the Gongyang Commentary explains: "Ni means 'low-born.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]訐，攻也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] Ji means 'to attack.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] Ji means 'to assail' or 'attack.'

sentences.zh: □，責也。

translations.literal: [Lacuna], means 'to blame.'
translations.idiomatic: The missing graph means 'to reproach' or 'blame.'

sentences.zh: 貴忠篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Exalting Loyalty' says:
translations.idiomatic: The essay "On Exalting Loyalty" begins:

sentences.zh: 夫帝王之所尊敬者天也，皇天之所愛育者人也。

translations.literal: What emperors and kings honor is Heaven; what August Heaven cherishes and nurtures is mankind.
translations.idiomatic: Sovereigns revere Heaven above all; Heaven's care rests on the human race.

sentences.zh: 今人臣受君之重位，牧天之所愛，焉可以不安而利之，養而濟之哉？

translations.literal: Now a minister receives a weighty charge from his lord and shepherds those whom Heaven loves—how can he fail to bring them peace and profit, nurture and deliver them?
translations.idiomatic: A subject who holds high office from his ruler tends the very people Heaven loves—how dare he neglect their welfare and their lives?

sentences.zh: 是以君子任職則思利人，達上則思進賢，故居上而下不怨，在前而後不恨也。

translations.literal: Therefore the noble man, when serving in office, thinks how to benefit others; when communicating upward, thinks how to advance the worthy—thus those below do not resent him when he is above, and those behind do not bear him malice when he is in front.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the gentleman in office seeks the people's good; when he addresses his superiors he seeks to elevate the worthy—so inferiors do not grudge his rank and followers do not nurse grievances.

sentences.zh: 書稱「天工人其代之」。

translations.literal: The Documents say, 'Heaven's work is to be substituted by man.'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Documents states, 'Heaven's task is discharged through human agents.'

sentences.zh: 王者法天而建官，[一]故明主不敢以私授，忠臣不敢以虛受。

translations.literal: The king models himself on Heaven in establishing offices; [1] therefore a clear-sighted ruler dares not bestow posts from private favor, and a loyal minister dares not accept empty titles.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign patterns his bureaucracy on Heaven; [1] a wise ruler therefore never hands out office from whim, and a true minister never accepts a hollow honor.

sentences.zh: 竊人之財猶謂之盜，況偷天官以私己乎！

translations.literal: To steal another man's property is still called theft—how much more to steal a Heaven-appointed office for private gain!
translations.idiomatic: Appropriating another's goods is theft—how much worse to usurp a divinely sanctioned post for selfish ends!

sentences.zh: [二]以罪犯人，必加誅罰，況乃犯天，得無咎乎？

translations.literal: [2] To punish men for their crimes one surely imposes execution or fines—how much more when one offends Heaven can one escape blame?
translations.idiomatic: [2] Men are executed for their crimes—when the offense is against Heaven, can punishment not be all the severer?

sentences.zh: 夫五*

translations.literal: As for the five*
translations.idiomatic: The phrase begins 'As for the five—'

translations.literal: (generations)
translations.idiomatic: The alternate reading '(generations)' glosses the preceding character.

sentences.zh: **[代]*之臣，以道事君，[三]澤及草木，仁被率土，是以福祚流衍，本支百世。

translations.literal: **[dynasties]* ministers who serve the ruler by the Way, [3] whose bounty reaches plants and trees and whose kindness covers all within the realm—therefore blessing spreads abroad and the trunk and branches flourish for a hundred generations.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers of the Five Dynasties who serve their lord through the moral Way [3] extend grace even to grass and trees and spread benevolence across the land—hence fortune overflows and the royal house endures for generations.

sentences.zh: [四]季世之臣，以諂媚主，不思順天，專杖殺伐。

translations.literal: [4] Ministers of a decadent age flatter their lord, do not think to obey Heaven, and rely solely on killing and war.
translations.idiomatic: [4] In a failing age, ministers toady to the throne, ignore Heaven's will, and trust in violence alone.

sentences.zh: 白起、蒙恬，秦以為功，天以為賊；

translations.literal: Bai Qi and Meng Tian were deemed meritorious by Qin but reckoned plunderers by Heaven;
translations.idiomatic: Qin praised Bai Qi and Meng Tian as heroes; Heaven counted them as bandits;

sentences.zh: [五]息夫、董賢，主以為忠，天以為盜。

translations.literal: [5] Xifu and Dong Xian were deemed loyal by their lord but reckoned thieves by Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Emperor Ai called Xifu Gong and Dong Xian loyal; Heaven deemed them robbers.

sentences.zh: [六]易曰：「德薄而位尊，智小而謀大，鮮不及矣。

translations.literal: [6] The Changes say: 'When virtue is slight yet position is exalted, when wisdom is small yet plans are great—few escape harm.
translations.idiomatic: [6] The Zhouyi warns: 'Thin virtue matched with high rank, petty wit matched with grand designs—scarcely anyone avoids disaster in such a case.

sentences.zh: 」[七]是故德不稱，其禍必酷；

translations.literal: ' [7] Therefore when virtue does not match rank, the calamity must be cruel;
translations.idiomatic: ' [7] When character and station are mismatched, the punishment is bound to be savage;

sentences.zh: 能不稱，其殃必大。

translations.literal: when ability does not match responsibility, the disaster must be great.
translations.idiomatic: when talent and office diverge, the ruin is proportionally greater.

sentences.zh: 夫竊位之人，天奪其鑒。

translations.literal: Those who usurp place—Heaven strips them of their mirror.
translations.idiomatic: Those who steal high office lose even the mirror by which they might see themselves—Heaven takes it away.

sentences.zh: [八]雖有明察之資，仁義之志，一旦富貴，則背親捐舊，喪其本心，□骨肉而親便辟，薄知友而厚犬馬，寧見朽貫千萬，而不忍貸人一錢，情知積粟腐倉，而不忍貸人一斗，骨肉怨望於家，細人謗讟於道。

translations.literal: [8] Though they may have the gift of clear discernment and the will to benevolence and right, once wealth and honor come they turn their backs on kin and cast off old friends, lose their original hearts, [lacuna] flesh-and-blood for favorites, slight true friends while pampering dogs and horses, would sooner see a million cash rot on a string than lend another a single coin, know full well that grain moulders in granaries yet grudge lending another a peck—kinfolk mutter resentment within the family, while petty folk revile them in the streets.
translations.idiomatic: [8] However keen their judgment or righteous their intent, riches and rank make them abandon family, betray old ties, and forfeit their better selves—[text defective] kin for sycophants, neglect friends while indulging horses and hounds, let millions moulder in the treasury yet refuse a single coin, watch grain rot in storehouses yet refuse a peck to the needy—so relatives curse them indoors and commoners curse them in the road.

sentences.zh: 前人以敗，後爭襲之，誠可傷也。

translations.literal: Those before them failed in this way, yet those after scramble to imitate them—truly it is pitiful.
translations.idiomatic: Their predecessors perished of it, yet successors rush to repeat the folly—how lamentable.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《尚書》咎繇謨曰：「亡曠庶官，天工人其代之。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Documents, 'Counsels of Gao Yao,' says: 'Do not leave offices empty; Heaven's work is to be substituted by man.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] The Documents, "Counsels of Gao Yao," reads: 'Do not suffer offices to stand vacant; Heaven's work is carried out through human agents.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國注云：「言人代天理官，不可以天官私非其才也。

translations.literal: ' Kong Anguo's commentary says: 'It means that men substitute for Heaven in ordering offices—one may not treat Heaven's offices as private property unfit for the talent.'
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo explains: 'People stand in for Heaven in assigning duties—celestial posts must not be treated as private sinecures.'

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「明王奉若天道，建邦設都。

translations.literal: It also says: 'The enlightened king obeys the way of Heaven in founding states and establishing capitals.
translations.idiomatic: The text continues: 'The enlightened king models himself on Heaven when founding a state and setting its capital.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國注云：「天有日、月、北斗、五星二十八宿，皆有尊卑相正之法。

translations.literal: ' Kong Anguo's commentary says: 'Heaven has sun, moon, Northern Dipper, five planets, and twenty-eight lunar lodges—each observes rules of precedence.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo adds: 'Heaven possesses sun and moon, the Northern Dipper, the five planets, and the twenty-eight lodges—each has its proper ordering.

sentences.zh: 言明王奉順此道，以立國設都也。」

translations.literal: It means that the enlightened king obeys this pattern in founding a state and setting its capital.'"
translations.idiomatic: Thus the enlightened king follows this model in establishing the realm and its seat.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]《左傳》介之推曰：「竊人之財猶謂之盜，況貪天功以為己力乎？」

translations.literal: Comment [2] Jie Zhitui in the Zuo Commentary says: 'To steal another man's goods is still called theft—how much more to seize Heaven's merit and call it one's own labor?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] Jie Zhitui in the Zuo Commentary: 'Taking another's goods is theft—how much worse to claim Heaven's achievement as one's own!'

sentences.zh: 注[三]五代謂唐、虞、夏、殷、周也。

translations.literal: Comment [3] The five generations mean Tang, Yu, Xia, Yin, and Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3] 'Five generations' denotes Tang, Yu, Xia, Shang, and Zhou.

sentences.zh: 注[四]詩大雅曰：「文王孫子，本支百世。」

translations.literal: Comment [4] The Odes, 'Major Odes,' says: 'King Wen's descendants, trunk and branches for a hundred generations.'
translations.idiomatic: Note [4] The Book of Odes, Greater Ya: 'The progeny of King Wen—root and branch for a hundred generations.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]《史記》曰，白起為秦將，與趙戰於長平，坑趙卒四十五萬人。

translations.literal: Comment [5] The Records says that Bai Qi, as a Qin general, fought Zhao at Changping and buried four hundred fifty thousand Zhao soldiers alive.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5] The Shiji records that Bai Qi, commanding Qin, defeated Zhao at Changping and slaughtered four hundred fifty thousand troops.

sentences.zh: 蒙恬為秦將，北逐戎翟，築長城，起臨洮至遼東，延袤萬餘里。

translations.literal: Meng Tian, as a Qin general, drove the Rong and Di northward, built the Long Wall from Lintao to Liaodong, more than ten thousand li in extent.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Tian, another Qin general, pursued the Rong and Di northward and ran the long wall from Lintao to Liaodong—over ten thousand li.

sentences.zh: 此為虐於人也。

translations.literal: This was cruelty toward mankind.
translations.idiomatic: These were atrocities against humanity.

sentences.zh: 注[六]息夫躬字子微，哀帝時，告東平王雲事，封宜陵侯。

translations.literal: Comment [6] Xifu Gong, courtesy name Ziwei, during Emperor Ai's reign denounced the affair of the Prince of Dongping, Wang Yun, and was enfeoffed as marquis of Yiling.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6] Xifu Gong, style Ziwei, informed on the Prince of Dongping's plot under Emperor Ai and was made marquis of Yiling.

sentences.zh: 董賢字聖卿，得幸哀帝，為賢起大第於北闕下，封為高安侯。

translations.literal: Dong Xian, courtesy name Shengqing, won the emperor's favor; for him a great mansion was built below the northern gate, and he was enfeoffed as marquis of Gao'an.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian, style Shengqing, became the emperor's favorite; Emperor Ai raised a palace for him north of the gate and invested him as marquis of Gao'an.

sentences.zh: 注[七]《易系辭》之言。

translations.literal: Comment [7] Words from the 'Appended Remarks' to the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7] A line from the Zhouyi's Xici.

sentences.zh: 注[八]《論語》孔子曰：「臧文仲其竊位者歟？

translations.literal: Comment [8] The Analects: Confucius says, 'Was not Zang Wenzhong a man who stole his office?'
translations.idiomatic: Note [8] The Analects: Confucius asks whether Zang Wenzhong 'stole his station.'

sentences.zh: 」《左傳》晉卜偃曰：「虢必亡矣，天奪之鑒而益其疾也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary: the Jin diviner Boyan said, 'Guo is sure to perish—Heaven has taken away its mirror and added to its ailments.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Commentary: Jin's diviner Boyan said, 'Guo will surely fall—Heaven has removed its mirror and deepened its malady.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云「鑒，所以自照」也。

translations.literal: ' Du Yu's commentary says: 'The mirror is what one uses to see oneself.'
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu explains: 'The mirror is the means of self-reflection.'

sentences.zh: 歷觀前政貴人之用心也，與嬰兒子其何異哉？

translations.literal: Surveying the intentions of the great men of earlier reigns—how do they differ from a babe in arms?
translations.idiomatic: Looking at how favorites of past courts set their hearts—how is it any wiser than an infant's whim?

sentences.zh: 嬰兒有常病，貴臣有常禍，父母有常失，人君有常過。

translations.literal: Babies have their chronic ailments, great ministers their chronic disasters, parents their chronic faults, and rulers their chronic errors.
translations.idiomatic: Infants have their usual illnesses, powerful ministers their usual downfalls, parents their usual missteps, sovereigns their usual blunders.

sentences.zh: 嬰兒常病，傷於飽也；

translations.literal: Babies are chronically ill from being overfed;
translations.idiomatic: Children fall ill chiefly from being overfed;

sentences.zh: 貴臣常禍，傷於寵也。

translations.literal: great ministers chronically fall from being overfavored.
translations.idiomatic: great ministers fall chiefly from excessive favor.

sentences.zh: 哺乳多則生□病，富貴盛而致驕疾。

translations.literal: Too much nursing breeds [lacuna] illness; too much wealth and honor breeds the sickness of arrogance.
translations.idiomatic: Too much coddling breeds certain ailments; too much wealth and rank breeds the disease of pride.

sentences.zh: 愛子而賊之，驕臣而滅之者，非一也。

translations.literal: Those who love a child and ruin him, or indulge a minister and destroy him—this is not a single case.
translations.idiomatic: Parents who spoil a child into ruin or rulers who pamper a minister into destruction are everywhere.

sentences.zh: 極其罰者，乃有僕死深牢，銜刀都巿，[一]豈非無功於天，有害於人者乎？

translations.literal: Those who suffer the utmost penalty end up dying in a deep prison, a knife in their teeth in the public market—[1] are they not men who have done no service to Heaven and harm to mankind?
translations.idiomatic: The worst end in fetid dungeons or bite a blade in the execution ground—[1] have they not failed Heaven and wronged humanity?

sentences.zh: 夫鳥以山為埤而增巢其上，魚以泉為淺而穿穴其中，卒所以得者餌也。

translations.literal: Birds take the hills for low and pile nests higher; fish think the spring shallow and bore tunnels there—yet in the end what catches them is bait.
translations.idiomatic: Birds deem hills too low and build higher nests; fish find springs too shallow and tunnel deeper—yet bait is what destroys them.

sentences.zh: [二]貴戚願其宅吉而制為令名，欲其門堅而造作鐵樞，卒其所以敗者，非苦禁忌少而門樞朽也，常苦崇財貨而行驕僭耳。

translations.literal: [2] Noble in-laws pray for an auspicious homestead and contrive a fine name, want their gates strong and forge iron hinges—yet what ruins them is not scanty taboos or rotten hinges but heaping up riches while acting arrogant and presumptuous.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Imperial affines want lucky sites and proud gate plaques, iron hinges for sturdy doors—yet they fall not for broken hinges or neglected omens but for piling wealth and flouting propriety.

sentences.zh: 注[一]趙將李牧為韓倉所譖，賜死。

translations.literal: Comment [1] The Zhao general Li Mu was slandered by Han Cang and ordered to die.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] Zhao's general Li Mu, framed by Han Cang, was condemned to death.

sentences.zh: 將自誅，臂短不能及，銜刀於柱以自殺。

translations.literal: About to kill himself, his arms were too short to reach; he held the blade between his teeth against a pillar and so died.
translations.idiomatic: Ordered to fall on his sword, his arms could not reach; he wedged the blade against a pillar and severed his own throat.

sentences.zh: 見《戰國策》。

translations.literal: See the Stratagems of the Warring States.
translations.idiomatic: Recorded in the Stratagems of the Warring States.

sentences.zh: 注[二]曾子之文也。

translations.literal: Comment [2] This is text by Zengzi.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] The passage comes from Zengzi.

sentences.zh: 亦見《大戴禮》。

translations.literal: It also appears in the Da Dai ritual compilation.
translations.idiomatic: It is likewise found in the Da Dai Liji.

sentences.zh: 不上順天心，下育人物，而欲任其私智，竊弄君威，反戾天地，欺誣神明。

translations.literal: They do not obey Heaven's mind above or nurture the myriad beings below, yet they would wield private cunning, usurp the ruler's awesomeness, rebel against Heaven and earth, and deceive the spirits.
translations.idiomatic: Instead of harmonizing with Heaven above and caring for the people below, they play private tricks, steal royal authority, set themselves against Heaven and earth, and lie to the gods.

sentences.zh: 居累卵之危，而圖太山之安，為朝露之行，而思傳世之功。

translations.literal: They stand on an egg's brink yet scheme for Mount Tai's security; they walk a course like morning dew yet dream of deeds that last ages.
translations.idiomatic: They perch on a pile of eggs yet plot Mount Tai's solidity; their deeds are morning dew yet they fantasize achievements for posterity.

sentences.zh: [一]豈不惑哉！

translations.literal: [1] Are they not deluded!
translations.idiomatic: [1] Can such people be anything but mad!

sentences.zh: 豈不惑哉！

translations.literal: Are they not deluded!
translations.idiomatic: Could they be anything but deluded!

sentences.zh: 注[一]朝露言易盡也。

translations.literal: Comment [1] 'Morning dew' means easily gone.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1] 'Morning dew' signifies how swiftly life passes.

sentences.zh: 蘇子曰：「人生一世，若朝露之托於桐葉耳，其與幾何！」

translations.literal: Master Su said: 'Human life in one generation is like dew resting on a paulownia leaf—how long can it last!'
translations.idiomatic: Su Shi wrote: 'A man's life is morning dew on a paulownia leaf—what span is that!'

sentences.zh: 浮侈篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Floating Extravagance' says:
translations.idiomatic: The treatise "On Floating Extravagance" says:

sentences.zh: 王者以四海為家，兆人為子。

translations.literal: The king takes the four seas as his house and the myriad people as his children.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven makes the realm his household and the million commoners his children.

sentences.zh: 一夫不耕，天下受其饑；

translations.literal: If one man does not plow, the world suffers hunger;
translations.idiomatic: When one farmer idles, the empire goes hungry;

sentences.zh: 一婦不織，天下受其寒。

translations.literal: if one woman does not weave, the world suffers cold.
translations.idiomatic: when one woman neglects her loom, the empire shivers.

sentences.zh: [一]今舉俗捨本農，趨商賈，牛馬車輿，填塞道路，游手為巧，充盈都邑，[二] 務本者少，浮食者觿。

translations.literal: [1] Now the whole custom abandons root agriculture and rushes into trade; ox-carts and carriages clog the roads, idlers ply clever tricks and fill the cities; [2] those who tend the root are few, those who eat without labor are many.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Today popular habit forsakes farming for commerce; wagons choke the highways, loafers with clever schemes pack the towns; [2] few cultivate the soil, many live on others' labor.

sentences.zh: 「商邑翼翼，四方是極。

translations.literal: 'The Shang capital stands in awesome order; the four quarters look to it as the pole.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Awesome stands the Shang capital; the regions turn to it as their pole.'

sentences.zh: 」[三]今察洛陽，資末業者什於農夫，虛偽游手什於末業。

translations.literal: ' [3] Now observe Luoyang: those who live by secondary trades outnumber farmers ten to one, and idle deceivers outnumber tradesmen ten to one.
translations.idiomatic: ' [3] Look at Luoyang—men in secondary occupations outnumber peasants tenfold, and sham idlers outnumber those tradesmen tenfold.

sentences.zh: 是則一夫耕，百人食之，一婦桑，百人衣之，以一奉百，孰能供之！

translations.literal: Thus one man plows and a hundred eat; one woman tends silkworms and a hundred clothe themselves—if one must support a hundred, who can supply it?
translations.idiomatic: One plowman feeds a hundred mouths; one silkworm-tender clothes a hundred bodies—how can a ratio of one to a hundred be sustained?

sentences.zh: 天下百郡千縣，巿邑萬數，類皆如此。

translations.literal: The empire has a hundred commanderies and a thousand counties, ten thousand market towns—almost all are like this.
translations.idiomatic: The realm's hundreds of districts and thousands of counties, its myriad towns, follow the same pattern.

sentences.zh: 本末不足相供，則民安得不饑寒？

translations.literal: When root and branch cannot supply each other, how can the people avoid hunger and cold?
translations.idiomatic: When agriculture cannot sustain the secondary occupations, how shall the people escape want and chill?

sentences.zh: 饑寒並至，則民安能無奸軌？

translations.literal: When hunger and cold come together, how can the people avoid villainy and crime?
translations.idiomatic: When hunger and cold strike together, how shall the people keep from crime?

sentences.zh: 奸軌繁多，則吏安能無嚴酷？

translations.literal: When villainy and crime abound, how can officials avoid harsh severity?
translations.idiomatic: When crime and corruption multiply, how are magistrates to keep from turning cruel?

sentences.zh: 嚴酷數加，則下安能無愁怨？

translations.literal: When harsh severity is piled on again and again, how can those below avoid sorrow and resentment?
translations.idiomatic: When cruelty is applied relentlessly, how can the people not seethe with grievance?

sentences.zh: 愁怨者多，則咎徵並臻。

translations.literal: When many harbor sorrow and resentment, omens of blame arrive together.
translations.idiomatic: Where resentment spreads widely, Heaven's reproofs descend in clusters.

sentences.zh: 下民無聊，而上天降□，則國危矣。

translations.literal: The common people have no means of livelihood, and if August Heaven sends down calamity, the state is in danger.
translations.idiomatic: When the masses are desperate and Heaven adds its stroke of disaster, the realm stands on the brink.

sentences.zh: 注[一]文子曰：「神農之法曰：『丈夫丁壯不耕，天下有受其饑者，婦人當年不織，天下有受其寒者。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Wenzi says: "The law of Shennong declares: 'If able-bodied husbands do not plow, someone under Heaven will go hungry; if women in their prime do not weave, someone under Heaven will go cold.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Wenzi quotes Shennong's rule: 'When grown men refuse the plow, hunger stalks the world; when women of age abandon the loom, cold stalks the world.

sentences.zh: 故其耕不強者，無以養生，其織不力者，無以衣形。』」

translations.literal: Therefore he whose plowing is not forceful has no way to nourish life; she whose weaving is not diligent has no way to clothe the body.'"
translations.idiomatic: Without vigorous tillage there is no sustenance; without diligent spinning there is no covering for the body.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]游手為巧謂雕鏤之屬也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 'Idlers plying clever tricks' refers to such crafts as carving and chasing.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The phrase denotes ornamental carving and similar handicrafts.

sentences.zh: 注[三]詩商頌文也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 This is language from the Shang hymns of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 The quotation comes from the Shang song in the Book of Odes.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注云：「極，中也。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Ji means 'the center.'
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses: 'Ji is the axial point.'

sentences.zh: 翼翼然可則□，乃四方之中正也。」

translations.literal: In stately awe it may serve as a model, [lacuna] and is the true pivot for the four quarters.'"
translations.idiomatic: Majestic and ordered, it sets the standard for the regions—it is the moral axis toward which the four quarters turn.'"

sentences.zh: 夫貧生於富，弱生於強，亂生於化，危生於安。

translations.literal: Poverty is born of wealth, weakness of strength, disorder of good government, peril of peace.
translations.idiomatic: Want springs from excess, feebleness from overweening power, chaos from misplaced reform, danger from complacent ease.

sentences.zh: [一]是故明王之養民，憂之勞之，教之誨之，慎微防萌，以斷其邪。

translations.literal: Therefore the enlightened king, in nurturing the people, troubles himself for them and labors for them, teaches them and instructs them, is watchful at the first hint and checks evil in the bud, so as to sever their waywardness.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the wise ruler rears the people with care and toil, with constant teaching and warning, nipping vice while it is still invisible, and so cuts off corruption at the root.

sentences.zh: 故易美節以制度，不傷財，不害民。

translations.literal: Therefore the Changes praises using moderation to set institutions, so as not to waste wealth or harm the people.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Changes commends measured institutions that spare the treasury and the common folk.

sentences.zh: [二] 七月之詩，大小教之，終而復始。

translations.literal: The poem 'Seventh Month,' by lessons great and small, instructs in cycles that end only to begin again.
translations.idiomatic: The 'Seventh Month' ode instructs high and low in a rhythm that closes one year only to open the next.

sentences.zh: 由此觀之，人固不可恣也。

translations.literal: Viewed from this, men truly must not indulge their desires.
translations.idiomatic: Seen in this light, no one may safely give free rein to appetite.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]富而不節則貧，強而驕人則弱，居理而不修德則亂，恃安而不慎微則危矣。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Wealth without restraint becomes poverty; strength that lords it over others becomes weakness; dwelling in order without cultivating virtue becomes chaos; relying on peace without heeding small signs becomes peril.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Riches without temperance turn to want; power without humility turns to weakness; stability without moral care turns to disorder; security without vigilance turns to ruin.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「節以制度」以下，並節卦彖辭也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 From 'using moderation to set institutions' downward, these are all lines from the commentary on the hexagram Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The passage from 'moderation through institutions' onward quotes the Tuan commentary on the hexagram Jie.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注云：「空府臧則傷財，力役繁則害人，二者奢泰之所致。」

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Empty treasuries injure wealth; oppressive labor injures the people; both spring from extravagance and excess.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan explains: 'Depleted storehouses ruin the economy; crushing corvée ruins lives—both are born of luxury.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]七月，詩豳風也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 'Seventh Month' is a poem in the Airs of Bin.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 'Seventh Month' belongs to the Bin section of the Odes.

sentences.zh: 大謂耕桑之法，小謂索綯之類。

translations.literal: Great means the methods of plowing and sericulture; small means such things as twisting cord.
translations.idiomatic: The 'great' lessons are farming and silk culture; the 'small' ones include chores like making rope.

sentences.zh: 自春及冬，終而復始也。

translations.literal: From spring through winter it completes a cycle and begins again.
translations.idiomatic: It runs the round of the seasons and starts the teaching anew each year.

sentences.zh: 今人奢衣服，侈飲食，事口舌而習調欺。

translations.literal: Today people lavish dress and food, devote themselves to wrangling tongues and practice deceit and fraud.
translations.idiomatic: People now squander finery and feasts, live by quarrelsome speech, and train themselves in fraud.

sentences.zh: 或以謀奸合任為業，[一]或以游博持掩為事。

translations.literal: Some take conspiracy, treachery, sworn brotherhood, and chivalric bravado as their trade, [1] or take roaming, gambling, and guessing games as their occupation.
translations.idiomatic: Some make a living of intrigue, knightly gangs, and shady alliances, while others live by wandering, dicing, and games of chance.

sentences.zh: [二]丁夫不扶廣鋤，而懷丸挾彈，攜手上山遨遊，或好取土作丸賣之，外不足禦寇盜，內不足禁鼠雀。

translations.literal: [2] Able-bodied men do not shoulder hoes or broad mattocks but carry pellets and slings and roam the hills hand in hand, or delight in gathering clay to make pellets for sale—outwardly they cannot ward off bandits, inwardly they cannot keep off rats and sparrows.
translations.idiomatic: Young fellows leave the mattock idle yet pack slingshots for idle rambles in the hills, or peddle clay pellets—useless against raiders at the gate or vermin in the granary.

sentences.zh: 或作泥車瓦狗諸戲弄之具，以巧詐小兒，此皆無益也。

translations.literal: Or they fashion clay carts, clay dogs, and other toys to cheat small children—all of this is without benefit.
translations.idiomatic: Others mold toy carts and clay hounds to wheedle pocket money from children—utterly worthless pursuits.

sentences.zh: 注[一]合任謂相合為任俠也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 'Sworn fellowship' means joining together in the manner of wandering knights.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 'He ren' describes men who band together as bravos and sworn brothers.

sentences.zh: 注[二]博謂六博，掩謂意錢也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 'Bo' means the liubo board game; 'yan' means guessing coins.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Bo is the six-token board game; yan is the coin-guessing wager.

sentences.zh: 《前書貨殖傳》曰「又況掘頤搏掩犯奸成富」也。

translations.literal: The Treatise on Money-making in the Former Han says, 'How much more those who dig out jaws and gamble, who break the law yet grow rich.'
translations.idiomatic: The Former Han 'Treatise on Money-making' adds, 'Not to mention those who gnaw fortunes by gambling and sharp practice—criminals who grow rich.'

sentences.zh: 詩刺「不績其麻，巿也婆娑」。

translations.literal: The Odes satirize 'they do not twist their hemp, yet caper in the market in their swaying dance.'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Odes mocks women who 'leave their hemp untwisted yet posture and dance in the marketplace.'

sentences.zh: [一]又婦人不修中饋，休其蠶織，[二]而起學巫祝，鼓舞事神，以欺誣細民，熒惑百姓妻女。

translations.literal: [1] Moreover, women do not tend the inner kitchen but abandon their silkworms and looms, [2] and instead study shaman invocations, drum and dance to serve the spirits, swindle the common folk, and dazzle the wives and daughters of the hundred surnames.
translations.idiomatic: Wives neglect the hearth and the loom to study spirit mediums, beat drums in trances, fleece simple neighbors, and bewitch other men's women.

sentences.zh: 羸弱疾病之家，懷憂憤憤，易為恐懼。

translations.literal: In households worn down by weakness and disease, anxiety and pent-up anger make them easy prey to fear.
translations.idiomatic: Families already broken by illness and poverty, smoldering with worry, fall quickest under fear's spell.

sentences.zh: 至使奔走便時，去離正宅，崎嶇路側，風寒所傷，奸人所利，盜賊所中。

translations.literal: They are driven to rush about at auspicious hours, leave their proper dwellings, and crouch by rough roadsides—injured by wind and cold, exploited by villains, struck by thieves and bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Victims hurry off at the diviner's hour, desert decent roofs, and huddle on broken roadsides—exposed to the elements, fleeced by scoundrels, robbed by thieves.

sentences.zh: 或增禍重祟，至於死亡，而不知巫所欺誤，反恨事神之晚，此妖妄之甚者也。

translations.literal: Some heap up calamity and double the baleful influence until death comes, yet without realizing the shaman's deception they bitterly regret that they did not serve the spirits sooner—this is the height of delusion and falsehood.
translations.idiomatic: Some die heaped with fresh disasters, yet blame themselves for not paying the medium sooner, never seeing the fraud—delusion could go no further.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩陳風也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 This is from the Airs of Chen in the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The line comes from the Chen airs in the Odes.

sentences.zh: 婆娑，舞魍。

translations.literal: Suosuo means dancing like a demon.
translations.idiomatic: Suosuo describes a whirling, spirit-like dance.

sentences.zh: 謂婦人於巿中歌舞以事神也。

translations.literal: It means women dancing in the market to serve the spirits.
translations.idiomatic: It refers to women performing ritual dance in the marketplace for the gods.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《易》家人卦六二曰：「在中饋，貞吉。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The second line of the hexagram Jiaren, six in the second place, says: 'She tends the inner kitchen—auspicious in firmness.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The Zhouyi hexagram Jiaren, second line: 'Tending the inner kitchen—firmness brings good fortune.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「中饋，酒食也。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "The inner kitchen means drink and food.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses: 'The inner kitchen is drink and food.'

sentences.zh: 」詩大雅曰：「婦無公事，休其蠶織。」

translations.literal: The Greater Odes say: 'The woman has no public duties—she rests from her silkworms and weaving.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Odes add: 'Woman has no office abroad—she may rest from silk and loom.'"

sentences.zh: 或刻畫好繒，以書祝辭；

translations.literal: Some carve fine silk and inscribe invocatory phrases upon it;
translations.idiomatic: Some scratch prayers onto costly silk;

sentences.zh: 或虛飾巧言，希致福祚；

translations.literal: some deck out clever phrases in hope of winning blessing;
translations.idiomatic: some spin ornate phrases to beg for fortune;

sentences.zh: 或糜折金彩，令廣分寸；

translations.literal: some grind down gold leaf to broaden it by a fraction;
translations.idiomatic: some hammer gold leaf thinner to stretch each inch;

sentences.zh: 或斷截觿縷，繞帶手腕；

translations.literal: some cut apart bolts of gauze to wrap them about the wrist;
translations.idiomatic: some hack good damask into strips to wind around the wrist;

sentences.zh: 或裁切綺縠，繨紩成幡。

translations.literal: some slice figured damask and stitch it into pennants.
translations.idiomatic: some slash patterned silks to sew spirit banners.

sentences.zh: 皆單費百縑，用功千倍，破牢為偽，以易就難，坐食嘉谷，消損白日。

translations.literal: Each wastes a hundred bolts of plain silk, expends a thousandfold the labor, breaks whole bolts to make sham goods, trades the easy for the hard, sits and consumes fine grain, and wastes the bright hours of the day.
translations.idiomatic: Each trick wastes a hundred rolls of plain silk and a thousandfold labor—destroying sound cloth for counterfeit charms, choosing the harder path for show, eating the best grain while idling away the daylight.

sentences.zh: [一]夫山林不能給野火，江海不能實漏琶，皆所宜禁也。

translations.literal: [1] Mountain forests cannot supply a wildfire, rivers and seas cannot fill a leaking lute—all such things ought to be forbidden.
translations.idiomatic: Forests cannot feed a bonfire, oceans cannot fill a holed jar—such waste ought to be banned outright.

sentences.zh: 注[一]損或作「捐」。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Some manuscripts read 'waste' instead of 'consume.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 An alternate reading writes 'squander' for 'consume.'

sentences.zh: 昔孝文皇帝躬衣弋綈，[一]革舄韋帶。

translations.literal: In the past Emperor Wen personally wore coarse black silk, [1] leather shoes, and a plain leather belt.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen of Han wore plain black homespun, leather slippers, and a simple leather belt.

sentences.zh: 而今京師貴戚，衣服飲食，車輿廬第，奢過王制，固亦甚矣。

translations.literal: Yet today the great kin of the capital in dress and food, carriages and houses, surpass the royal regulations in extravagance—indeed it is extreme.
translations.idiomatic: Today the capital nobility outdo the sumptuary laws in dress, diet, carriages, and mansions—the excess is shocking.

sentences.zh: 且其徒御僕妾，皆服文組彩牒，[二]錦繡綺紈，葛子升越，筩中女布。

translations.literal: Moreover their drivers, attendants, and concubines all wear figured silks and layered damask, [2] brocade and fine silk, Gezi, Sheng, and the 'women's cloth' from bamboo tubes.
translations.idiomatic: Even grooms and maids are swathed in patterned damask and layered silks—Gezi, Sheng, and the costly tube-woven cloth of Yizhou.

sentences.zh: [三]犀象珠玉，虎魄輂瑁，石山隱飾，金銀錯鏤，[四]窮極麗靡，轉相誇宛。

translations.literal: [3] Rhinoceros horn, ivory, pearls and jade, amber, tortoise shell, relief carving like mountain rock, gold and silver inlay, [4] exhaust every degree of splendor and vie in mutual boasting.
translations.idiomatic: They flaunt rhino horn, ivory, pearls, jade, amber, hawksbill, relief work like stone cliffs, and gold-inlaid metal—each trying to outshine the next in wasteful display.

sentences.zh: [五]其嫁娶者，車軿數里，緹帷竟道，[六]騎奴侍童，夾轂並引。

translations.literal: [5] At weddings and funerals their carriages stretch for miles, crimson curtains shade the whole road, [6] mounted slaves and boy attendants flank the wheel hubs and lead the teams in parallel.
translations.idiomatic: Wedding and funeral trains clog the road for li on end, with crimson canopies meeting overhead and outriders flanking every axle.

sentences.zh: 富者競欲相過，貧者恥其不逮，一饗之所費，破終身之業。

translations.literal: The rich strive to outdo one another, the poor are ashamed not to keep up—a single feast can ruin a lifetime's accumulation.
translations.idiomatic: The wealthy compete to spend more; the humble are shamed if they cannot match them—one banquet can bankrupt a family forever.

sentences.zh: 古者必有命然後乃得衣繒絲而乘車馬，[七]今雖不能復古，宜令細民略用孝文之制。

translations.literal: In antiquity one needed a royal grant before one might wear figured silk or ride horses and carriages; [7] though we cannot fully restore antiquity now, the common people should at least approximate Emperor Wen's standard.
translations.idiomatic: Anciently no one wore silk or rode carriages without a royal patent; we cannot revive every old rule, yet commoners might still be held to something like Wen's frugality.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書音義曰：「弋，皁也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Former Han glossary says: "Yi means black.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The Former Han sound gloss: 'Yi is black cloth.

sentences.zh: 綈，繒也。」

translations.literal: Ti means silk.'"
translations.idiomatic: Ti is a kind of silk.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]牒即今疊布也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Die here is the same as today's folded cloth.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Die is what we now call layered bolt cloth.

sentences.zh: 注[三]《說文》曰：「綺，文繒也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 The Shuowen says: "Qi is figured silk.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 The Shuowen defines qi as patterned silk.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰：「齊俗作冰紈。

translations.literal: The Former Han says: "Qi custom produced ice-white silk.
translations.idiomatic: The Former Han adds: 'Qi folk wove ice-white gauze.

sentences.zh: 」子，細稱也。

translations.literal: Zi means fine weave.
translations.idiomatic: Zi denotes the finest grade.

sentences.zh: 沈懷遠《南越志》曰：「蕉布之品有三，有蕉布，有竹子布，又有葛焉。

translations.literal: Shen Huaiyuan's Record of Southern Yue says: "There are three grades of plantain cloth: plantain cloth, bamboo cloth, and also ge among them.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Huaiyuan's Southern Yue treatise lists three plantain fabrics—pure plantain, bamboo fiber, and ge.

sentences.zh: 雖精麤之殊，皆同出而異名。

translations.literal: Though fine and coarse differ, all come from one source under different names.
translations.idiomatic: Coarse or fine, they share one origin and bear different labels.

sentences.zh: 」楊雄《蜀都賦》曰：「布則蜘蛛作絲，不可見風，筩中黃潤，一端數金。

translations.literal: Yang Xiong's Rhapsody on Shu says: "Cloth—spiders spin thread that must not meet the wind; the yellow lustrous cloth from the tube, one bolt costs several in gold.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong's Shu rhapsody boasts cloth so fine a spider's thread must not feel the breeze, and tube-woven yellow silk worth its weight in gold.

sentences.zh: 」盛弘之《荊州記》曰：「秭歸縣室多幽閒，其女盡織布至數十升。

translations.literal: Sheng Hongzhi's Record of Jingzhou says: "In Zigui County the chambers are many and secluded; its women all weave cloth to several dozen sheng in fineness.
translations.idiomatic: Sheng Hongzhi's Jingzhou gazetteer says Zigui women weave cloth of extraordinary tightness.

sentences.zh: 」今永州俗猶呼貢布為女子布也。

translations.literal: Today Yongzhou custom still calls tribute cloth 'women's cloth.'"
translations.idiomatic: Even now Yongzhou folk still call tribute-grade cloth 'women's cloth.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]《廣雅》曰：「虎魄，珠也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 The Guangya says: "Amber is a pearl.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 The Guangya classes amber as a gem.

sentences.zh: 生地中，其上及旁不生草，深者八九尺。

translations.literal: It grows in the earth; on the surface and around it no grass grows; the deep ones are eight or nine chi.
translations.idiomatic: It forms underground; no grass grows on the spot; the deepest deposits lie eight or nine feet down.

sentences.zh: 初時如桃膠，凝堅乃成。

translations.literal: At first it is like peach gum; when congealed and hard it is complete.
translations.idiomatic: At first it resembles peach resin; hardened, it becomes true amber.

sentences.zh: 其方人以為枕。

translations.literal: The people of that region use it for pillows.
translations.idiomatic: Locals carve it into pillows.

sentences.zh: 出罽賓及大秦國。

translations.literal: It comes from Jibin and Daqin.
translations.idiomatic: Sources lie in Jibin and the Roman East.

sentences.zh: 」吳錄曰：「輂瑁似龜而大，出南海。

translations.literal: The Wu lu says: "The dai mao tortoise resembles a turtle but is larger and comes from the South Sea.
translations.idiomatic: The Wu lu describes the hawksbill as a giant sea turtle from the southern ocean.

sentences.zh: 」山石謂隱起為山石之文也。

translations.literal: Mountain rock means relief carving in the pattern of mountain and stone.
translations.idiomatic: Relief 'mountain-stone' pattern means raised landscape motifs.

sentences.zh: 注[五]郭景純注《子虛賦》曰：「詫，誇也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 Guo Jingchun's commentary on the Rhapsody on the Void says: "Tuo means to boast.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Guo Pu on the Rhapsody on the Void glosses tuo as boasting.

sentences.zh: 」宛與詫通也。

translations.literal: Yuan is interchangeable with tuo.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan here reads the same as tuo.

sentences.zh: 注[六]蒼頡篇曰：「軿，衣車。

translations.literal: Comment 6 The Cangjie pian says: "Ping means a curtained carriage.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 The Cangjie wordbook defines ping as a veiled women's carriage.

sentences.zh: 」軿音薄丁反，又步田反。

translations.literal: Ping is read either as boding fan or as butian fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph is read boding or butian.

sentences.zh: 注[七]《尚書大傳》曰：「古之帝王者必有命。

translations.literal: Comment 7 The Great Tradition on the Documents says: "The ancient emperors and kings always had their grants.
translations.idiomatic: Note 7 The Documents Great Tradition says ancient kings issued sumptuary patents.

sentences.zh: 人能敬長矜孤，取捨好讓者，命於其君，得乘飾車軿馬，衣文錦。

translations.literal: Those who can revere elders and pity orphans, who in taking and giving prefer yielding, receive a grant from their lord and may ride ornamented carriages and horses and wear figured brocade.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever honors age, pities orphans, and yields in giving and taking receives a patent to ride fine carriages and wear patterned brocade.

sentences.zh: 未有命者，不得衣，不得乘，乘衣者有罰。」

translations.literal: Those without a grant may not wear silk or ride; those who ride or wear silk without a grant are punished.'"
translations.idiomatic: Without a patent one may not wear silk or ride; violators are punished.'"

sentences.zh: 古之葬者，厚衣之以薪，葬之中野，不封不樹，喪期無數。

translations.literal: In antiquity the dead were thickly wrapped in firewood and buried in the open field without mound or tree, and the mourning period had no fixed length.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient burials wrapped the corpse in fuel and laid it in the wild without mound or marker, with no set mourning span.

sentences.zh: 後世聖人易之以棺槨，[一]桐木為棺，葛采為緘，[二]下不及泉，上不洩臭。

translations.literal: Later sages replaced this with inner and outer coffins, [1] paulownia for the inner coffin, kudzu cord for the seams, [2] so that below they did not reach the springs and above they did not leak the stench.
translations.idiomatic: Later ritualists introduced inner and outer coffins—paulownia shells, kudzu stitching—sealed so neither groundwater nor odor escaped.

sentences.zh: 中世以後，轉用楸梓槐柏杶樗之屬，各因方土，裁用膠漆，使其堅足恃，其用足任，如此而已。

translations.literal: From the middle age onward they turned to catalpa, cedar, locust, cypress, chinquapin, and ailanthus, according to each region's timber, using glue and lacquer so the coffin was firm enough to rely on and serviceable enough to bear the burden—no more than this.
translations.idiomatic: Middle-period practice used local hardwoods—catalpa, cedar, locust, cypress, chinquapin, ailanthus—glued and lacquered until the box was sound and serviceable, nothing more.

sentences.zh: 今者京師貴戚，必欲江南檽梓豫章之木。

translations.literal: Today the great kin of the capital insist on catalpa, camphor, and Zhang wood from the south.
translations.idiomatic: Capital grandees demand southern camphor, nanmu, and Zhang timber.

sentences.zh: [三]邊遠下土，亦競相放□。

translations.literal: [3] In remote borderlands and poor districts they vie to imitate the fashion as well.
translations.idiomatic: Even distant commanderies scramble to copy the style.

sentences.zh: 夫檽樟豫章，所出殊遠，伐之高山，引之窮谷，入海乘淮，逆河泝洛，工匠雕刻，連累日月，會觿而後動，多牛而後致，重且千斤，功將萬夫，而東至樂浪，西達敦煌，費力傷農於萬里之地。

translations.literal: For catalpa, camphor, and Zhang timber come from regions far away—they are felled on high mountains, dragged through deep valleys, shipped out to sea, floated up the Huai, borne against the current of the Yellow River and the Luo, carved and chased by craftsmen for months on end, assembled only after long preparation, hauled by many oxen before they arrive—each load weighing a thousand jin and the labor of ten thousand men, carried east to Lelang and west to Dunhuang, wasting strength and harming agriculture across ten thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Those timbers grow far away—cut on mountain heights, snaked through ravines, rafted on the Huai, poled up the Yellow River and Luo, worked for months, joined with iron and glue, dragged by whole herds of oxen—loads of a thousand jin apiece, the toil of myriad men, shipped from Lelang to Dunhuang, bleeding the farmer dry across the realm.

sentences.zh: 古者墓而不墳，中世墳而不崇。

translations.literal: In antiquity there were burials without mounds; in the middle period mounds but no lofty height.
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity buried flat; the middle ages added low mounds, never high tumuli.

sentences.zh: 仲尼喪母，頤高四尺，遇雨而崩，弟子請修之，夫子泣曰：「古不修墓。

translations.literal: When Confucius buried his mother, the mound was four chi high; rain caused it to collapse, and the disciples asked to repair it; the Master wept and said, 'In antiquity there was no repairing of graves.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius gave his mother a barrow only four chi high; rain washed it flat and the disciples wished to rebuild it, but he wept, 'The ancients did not repair graves.

sentences.zh: 」[四]及鯉也死，有棺無幟。

translations.literal: ' [4] When his son Li died, there was a coffin but no outer shell.
translations.idiomatic: When his son Li died, he used an inner coffin without an outer casing.

sentences.zh: 文帝葬芷陽，[五]明帝葬洛南，皆不臧珠寶，不起山陵，墓雖卑而德最高。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen was buried at Zhiyang, [5] Emperor Ming south of the Luo—neither hid pearls or jade, neither raised great mountain tombs; though low, their virtue was the highest.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen lay at Zhiyang, Ming south of the Luo—no jade shrouds, no artificial mountains; modest graves, towering virtue.

sentences.zh: 今京師貴戚，郡縣豪家，生不極養，死乃崇喪。

translations.literal: Today the great kin of the capital and the powerful houses of the commanderies give no utmost care to the living but vie in lavish funerals.
translations.idiomatic: Capital nobles and rural magnates stint the parents while alive yet bankrupt themselves for the dead.

sentences.zh: 或至金縷玉匣，檽梓楩□，多埋珍寶偶人車馬，造起大頤，廣種松柏，廬舍祠堂，務崇華侈。

translations.literal: Some go so far as gold thread and jade suits, catalpa and nanmu coffins, burying treasures in profusion, clay figurines, chariots and horses, raising huge mounds, planting pines and cypress in wide groves, and building halls and shrines in quest of the utmost splendor.
translations.idiomatic: They sew gold into shrouds, line tombs with rare wood, pack grave goods, clay armies, paper chariots, heap artificial hills, blanket acres in pine, and raise ancestral halls of pointless grandeur.

sentences.zh: 案鄗畢之陵，南城之頤，[六]周公非不忠，曾子非不孝，以為□君愛父，不在於聚財，揚名顯親，無取於車馬。

translations.literal: Consider the tombs at Hao and Bi, the mounds south of the city—[6] the Duke of Zhou was not disloyal, Zengzi was not unfilial, yet they held that to honor ruler or father did not lie in hoarding wealth, and to make a name and glorify kin did not require chariots and horses.
translations.idiomatic: Look at the Zhou tombs at Hao and Bi, the southern barrows—the Duke of Zhou was hardly faithless, Zengzi hardly undutiful, yet both knew that honoring lord or parent is not a matter of piling treasure, and that fame does not ride on funeral display.

sentences.zh: 昔晉靈公多賦以雕牆，春秋以為*

translations.literal: In the past Duke Ling of Jin levied heavy taxes to carve his walls—the Spring and Autumn regarded this as *
translations.idiomatic: Duke Ling of Jin taxed the people to carve his palace walls—the Spring and Autumn annals branded such conduct as *

translations.literal: (wrong)
translations.idiomatic: (wrong)

translations.literal: **[unworthy]* of a ruler;
translations.idiomatic: **[unworthy]* in a ruler;

sentences.zh: [七]華元、樂舉厚葬文公，君子以為不臣。

translations.literal: [7] Hua Yuan and Yue Ju gave Minister Wen a sumptuous burial, and the gentlemen deemed them disloyal subjects.
translations.idiomatic: Hua Yuan and Yue Ju buried Duke Wen with wasteful pomp, and critics judged them disloyal ministers.

sentences.zh: [八]況於腢司士庶，乃可僭侈主上，過天道乎？

translations.literal: [8] How much more officials and commoners—may they outdo their sovereign and transgress Heaven's way?
translations.idiomatic: How much worse when petty officials and commoners outspend the throne and violate cosmic order?
The ninth note stands without appended text in the received edition.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《易系辭》之言也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 These are words from the 'Appended Remarks' to the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The passage quotes the Zhouyi Xici.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《屍子》曰：「禹之喪法，死於陵者葬於陵，死於澤者葬於澤，桐棺三寸，制喪三日。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The Master Shi says: "Yu prescribed burial: those who died on the hill were buried on the hill, those who died in the marsh were buried in the marsh; the paulownia coffin was three inches thick, and mourning was fixed at three days.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Master Shi records Yu's law: bury highlanders on the heights, lowlanders in the wetlands; use a three-inch paulownia shell and mourn three days.

sentences.zh: 」《墨子》曰：「舜西教乎七戎，道死，葬南巴之中，衣衾三領，款木之棺，葛以緘之。

translations.literal: The Mozi says: "Shun went west to instruct the seven Rong tribes, died on the road, and was buried amid Southern Ba; he had three layers of shroud, a coffin of kuanshu wood, and kudzu cord to bind the seams.
translations.idiomatic: The Mozi records that Shun traveled west to teach the seven Rong, died en route, and was interred in the Southern Ba region with only three winding-sheets, a plain kuanshu coffin, and kudzu lashing.

sentences.zh: 」采猶蔓也。

translations.literal: Cai here means 'to spread' like a vine.
translations.idiomatic: Here cai means 'to spread' or 'run along' like a creeping vine.

sentences.zh: 緘，束也。

translations.literal: Jian means 'to bind.'
translations.idiomatic: Jian means to bind or lash shut.

sentences.zh: 注[三]檽音乃豆反，見埤蒼。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Nai is read naidou fan; see the Bicang.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 The tree-name graph here is read naidou; see the Bicang lexicon.

sentences.zh: 《爾雅》曰：「栵檽。

translations.literal: The Erya says: "Lie and nai.
translations.idiomatic: The Erya pairs lie with nai (oak species).

sentences.zh: 」音而。

translations.literal: It is pronounced er.
translations.idiomatic: The reading is er.

sentences.zh: 注云「檽似槲□而痺小」，恐非棺槨之用。

translations.literal: The commentary says 'nai resembles oak [lacuna] but is smaller'—probably not timber for inner and outer coffins.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss compares nai to a dwarf oak—hardly suitable timber for state coffins.

sentences.zh: 豫章即樟木也。

translations.literal: Zhang of Yuzhang is camphor wood.
translations.idiomatic: The 'Zhang' of Yuzhang commandery is camphorwood.

sentences.zh: 注[四]孔子合葬母於防，曰：「吾聞之，古也墓而不墳。

translations.literal: Comment 4 Confucius buried his mother with his father at Fang, saying: 'I have heard that in antiquity there was burial but no mound.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Confucius reinterred his parents at Fang, remarking, 'The ancients raised barrows but not high tumuli.

sentences.zh: 」於是封之崇四尺。

translations.literal: Therefore he piled the mound four chi high.
translations.idiomatic: He therefore heaped earth only four chi high.

sentences.zh: 孔子先反，門人後，雨甚至。

translations.literal: Confucius returned first; the disciples came after, and the rain became very heavy.
translations.idiomatic: The Master went ahead while the disciples lagged, and a downpour struck.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「爾來何遲也？

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Why have you come so late?'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius asked, 'Why are you so late?'

sentences.zh: 」曰：「防墓崩。

translations.literal: They said: 'The tomb at Fang has collapsed.
translations.idiomatic: They answered, 'The barrow at Fang has washed out.

sentences.zh: 」孔子泫然流涕曰：「吾聞之，古不修墓。

translations.literal: Confucius, tears streaming, said: 'I have heard that in antiquity they did not repair graves.
translations.idiomatic: Weeping, he said, 'I was taught that the ancients never rebuilt tombs.

sentences.zh: 」見《禮記》也。

translations.literal: This is seen in the Record of Rites.'"
translations.idiomatic: The episode appears in the Liji.'"

sentences.zh: 注[五]縣名，屬京兆，文帝后改曰霸陵。

translations.literal: Comment 5 It is a county name belonging to Jingzhao; after Emperor Wen's death it was renamed Baling.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Zhiyang was a Jingzhao county later renamed Baling for Emperor Wen's mausoleum.

sentences.zh: 注[六]畢，周文王、武王葬地也。

translations.literal: Comment 6 Bi is where Kings Wen and Wu of Zhou were buried.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 Bi is the Zhou royal burial ground of Wen and Wu.

sentences.zh: 司馬遷云「在鄗東南杜中」，無墳隴，在今咸陽縣西北。

translations.literal: Sima Qian says 'southeast of Hao in Du,' with no mound or ridge; today it lies northwest of Xianyang County.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Qian places it southeast of Hao in Du, flat and unmarked—northwest of present Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 孔安國注《尚書》雲在長安西北。

translations.literal: Kong Anguo's commentary on the Documents places it northwest of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo's Shangshu gloss puts the site northwest of Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 南城山，曾子父所葬，在今沂州費縣西南也。

translations.literal: Mount Nancheng, where Zengzi's father was buried, lies southwest of Fei County in Yi Prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: The southern-city hill where Zengzi's father lies is southwest of Fei in modern Yizhou.

sentences.zh: 注[七]《左傳》：「晉靈公不君，厚斂以雕牆。

translations.literal: Comment 7 The Zuo Commentary: 'Duke Ling of Jin did not behave as a ruler should; he levied heavy taxes to carve his walls.
translations.idiomatic: Note 7 The Zuo: 'Duke Ling of Jin failed as sovereign, taxing the people to ornament his walls.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「不君，失君道也。

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary says: 'Not behaving as ruler means he lost the way of a lord.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu explains: 'Not ruling' means abandoning the ruler's path.

sentences.zh: 雕，畫也。」

translations.literal: Diao means to paint.'"
translations.idiomatic: Diao here means painted relief.'"

sentences.zh: 注[八]左傳：「宋文公卒，始厚葬，用蜃炭，益車馬，始用殉，幟有四阿，棺有翰檜。

translations.literal: Comment 8 The Zuo Commentary: 'When Duke Wen of Song died, they first practiced lavish burial—using oyster ash and charcoal, adding chariots and horses, beginning human sacrifice, canopies with four gables, coffins with han and gui struts.
translations.idiomatic: Note 8 The Zuo records Duke Wen of Song's first extravagant funeral—oyster lime, extra chariots, human victims, four-eaved shades, coffins with han and gui timbers.

sentences.zh: 君子謂華元、樂舉於是不臣，是□君於惡也。」

translations.literal: The gentlemen said that Hua Yuan and Yue Ju in this were not loyal ministers—they [lacuna] their ruler toward evil.'"
translations.idiomatic: Critics said Hua Yuan and Yue Ju were no true ministers—they led their lord into vice.'"

sentences.zh: 注[九]前書貢禹曰：「今大夫僭諸侯，諸侯僭天子，天子過天道，其日久矣。」

translations.literal: Comment 9 Gong Yu in the Former Han said: 'Today grandees usurp the prerogatives of marquises, marquises those of the Son of Heaven, and the Son of Heaven exceeds Heaven's way—this has gone on long.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note 9 Gong Yu warned that ministers ape marquises, marquises ape the throne, and the throne outruns cosmic order—a long-standing evil.'"

sentences.zh: 實貢篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Substantive Recommendation' says:
translations.idiomatic: The treatise "On Substantive Recommendation" declares:

sentences.zh: 國以賢興，以諂衰；

translations.literal: The state rises through worthies and declines through flattery;
translations.idiomatic: States flourish when the worthy advance and wither when sycophants rule;

sentences.zh: 君以忠安，以佞危。

translations.literal: the ruler is secure through loyalty and endangered through craftiness.
translations.idiomatic: sovereigns stand firm on loyal counsel and totter on deceit.

sentences.zh: 此古今之常論，而時所共知也。

translations.literal: This is the constant judgment of past and present and what every age professes to know.
translations.idiomatic: So runs the perennial verdict of history, acknowledged in every generation.

sentences.zh: 然衰國危君，繼踵不絕者，豈時無忠信正直之士哉，誠苦其道不得行耳。

translations.literal: Yet failing states and endangered lords follow one another without cease—is it truly that no loyal, trustworthy, upright men exist? The bitter truth is that their path cannot be practiced.
translations.idiomatic: Still, ruined realms and imperiled thrones succeed without end—not because integrity is extinct, but because honest men cannot get a hearing.

sentences.zh: 夫十步之閒，必有茂草；

translations.literal: Within ten paces there must be lush grass;
translations.idiomatic: As the proverb says, rank grass grows within ten paces;

sentences.zh: 十室之邑，必有忠信。

translations.literal: in a hamlet of ten houses there must be men of loyalty and good faith.
translations.idiomatic: in a village of ten doors you will find loyalty and faith.

sentences.zh: [一]是故亂殷有三仁，小□多君子。

translations.literal: Therefore troubled Yin still had the Three Worthies, and small [lacuna] had many gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Thus even decadent Yin kept the Three Worthies, and minor states could field gentlemen aplenty.

sentences.zh: [二] 今以大漢之廣土，士民之繁庶，朝廷之清明，上下之修正，而官無善吏，位無良臣。

translations.literal: Now, given the vast territory of Great Han, the multitude of scholars and commoners, the clarity at court, and the rectification above and below, yet offices have no good clerks and posts no fine ministers—
translations.idiomatic: Yet under the Han's wide domain, teeming population, lucid court, and ordered hierarchy, good clerks are scarce and able ministers fewer still—

sentences.zh: 此豈時之無賢，諒由取之乖實。

translations.literal: can this mean the age lacks worthies? Assuredly it is because selection strays from reality.
translations.idiomatic: surely the fault is not missing talent but recruitment divorced from fact.

sentences.zh: 夫志道者少與，逐俗者多疇，是以朋黨用私，背實趨華。

translations.literal: Those who set their hearts on the Way win few allies; those who chase fashion find many companions—hence factions act from private interest and turn their backs on substance to pursue show.
translations.idiomatic: Few follow the Way, many chase fashion—so cliques serve private ends, abandon the real, and chase glitter.

sentences.zh: 其貢士者，不復依其質干，准其才行，但虛造聲譽，妄生羽毛。

translations.literal: Those who recommend candidates no longer test intrinsic substance or match ability to office but invent reputations and falsely grow plumes.
translations.idiomatic: Patrons no longer weigh character and capacity but fabricate fame and pin borrowed feathers on mediocrity.

sentences.zh: 略計所舉，歲且二百。

translations.literal: Rough reckoning of those nominated each year approaches two hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Tallies suggest nearly two hundred men are pushed forward yearly.

sentences.zh: 覽察其狀，則德侔顏、冉，詳核厥能，則鮮及中人，皆總務陞官，自相推達。

translations.literal: Read their dossiers and they rival Yan Hui and Ran Boniu; scrutinize their deeds and few reach middling talent—yet all are rushed up the ladder through mutual puffery.
translations.idiomatic: On paper they equal Yan and Ran; judged by performance scarcely one reaches average—yet all climb by logrolling.

sentences.zh: 夫士者貴其用也，不必求備。

translations.literal: For a scholar what matters is usefulness, not completeness in every art.
translations.idiomatic: What counts in a scholar is what he can do, not encyclopedic perfection.

sentences.zh: 故四友雖美，能不相兼；

translations.literal: Therefore though the Four Friends were splendid, their abilities did not duplicate one another;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius' four disciples excelled, yet each had a different gift;

sentences.zh: [三]三仁齊致，事不一節。

translations.literal: the Three Worthies of Yin rose together yet served in different capacities.
translations.idiomatic: Yin's three loyalists rose side by side yet filled unlike roles.

sentences.zh: 高祖佐命，出自亡秦；

translations.literal: The founders who aided Emperor Gao came forth from perishing Qin;
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang's helpers emerged from the wreck of Qin;

sentences.zh: 光武得士，亦資暴莽。

translations.literal: the men who served Emperor Guangwu likewise drew on the violence of Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu's talent pool likewise fed on Wang Mang's tyranny.

sentences.zh: 況太平之時，而雲無士乎！

translations.literal: How much more, in an age of general peace, can one say there are no gentlemen!
translations.idiomatic: In a peaceful age, then, who dare claim the realm lacks able men?

sentences.zh: 注[一]《說苑》曰：「十步之澤，必有芳草。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Shuoyuan says: 'Within ten paces of marsh there must be fragrant grass.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Shuoyuan: 'Even by a ten-pace marsh sweet herbs grow.

sentences.zh: 」《論語》曰「十室之邑，必有忠信」也。

translations.literal: The Analects says, 'In a hamlet of ten houses there must be those who are loyal and of good faith.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Analects adds, 'Ten doors will still yield loyalty and faith.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]亂殷謂紂時也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 'Troubled Yin' means the age of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 'Troubled Yin' denotes King Zhou's reign.

sentences.zh: 三仁，箕子、微子、比干也。

translations.literal: The Three Worthies are Jizi, Weizi, and Bigan.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Worthies are Jizi, Weizi, and Bigan.

sentences.zh: 《左傳》，吳季札適□，悅蘧瑗、史狗、史□、公子荊、公叔發、公子朝，曰：「□多君子，未有患也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary: Wu prince Jizha visited [lacuna], admired Qu Yuan, Shi Gou, Shi [lacuna], Prince Jing of Wei, Gongshu Fa, and Prince Zhao of Wei, and said, '[lacuna] has many gentlemen; I see no cause for worry.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jizha of Wu toured Wei, praised Qu Yuan, Shi Gou, Shi [lacuna], Prince Jing, Gongshu Fa, and Prince Zhao, and declared, 'Wei teems with gentlemen—no peril there.

sentences.zh: 」又臧宣叔曰：「□之於晉，不得為次國。

translations.literal: Zang Xuanshu also said: '[lacuna] toward Jin cannot count even as a middle state.
translations.idiomatic: Zang Xuanshu observed that Wei beside Jin barely ranks as a middling power.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「春秋之時，以強弱為大小，□雖侯爵，猶為小國。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary says: 'In Spring and Autumn times strength and weakness determined rank; though [lacuna] held a marquis's title, it remained a small state.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu notes that in the Chunqiu era size followed power; though Wei bore a marquisal rank, it was still counted small.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]《尚書大傳》孔子曰：「文王得四臣，丘亦得四友。

translations.literal: Comment 3 The Great Tradition on the Documents: Confucius said, 'King Wen gained four ministers, and I too have four friends.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 The Shangshu dazhuan quotes Confucius: 'King Wen had four helpers, and I have four companions.

sentences.zh: 」謂回也為胥附，賜也為奔走，師也為先後，由也為禦侮，其能各不同也。

translations.literal: He named Hui as 'support at the side,' Ci as 'runner on errands,' Shi as 'vanguard and rear,' and You as 'shield against insult'—their capacities were not the same.'"
translations.idiomatic: He called Hui counselor at his flank, Ci his swift messenger, Shi his advance and rear guard, You his champion against affront—each with a distinct role.'"

sentences.zh: 夫明君之詔也若聲，忠臣之和也如響。

translations.literal: The enlightened ruler's edict is like sound; the loyal minister's accord is like an echo.
translations.idiomatic: A wise sovereign's command is voice; the faithful minister's answer is echo.

sentences.zh: 長短大小，清濁疾徐，必相應也。

translations.literal: Whether long or short, large or small, clear or turbid, fast or slow, they must answer each other.
translations.idiomatic: Length, size, tone, and pace must match as in call and response.

sentences.zh: 且攻玉以石，洗金以鹽，[一]濯錦以魚，浣布以灰。

translations.literal: Moreover one polishes jade with stone, washes gold with salt, [1] rinses brocade with fish glue, and scours cloth with ashes.
translations.idiomatic: Jade is cut with common stone, gold brightened in brine, brocade washed with isinglass, cloth bleached with lye—

sentences.zh: 夫物固有以賤理貴，以醜化好者矣。

translations.literal: For things there are indeed cases where the base refines the noble and the ugly transforms into the beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: low means can perfect high ends, homely agents make beauty shine.

sentences.zh: 智者□短取長，以致其功。

translations.literal: The wise man [lacuna] the short and takes the long, thereby accomplishing his task.
translations.idiomatic: The wise accept flaws and exploit strengths, and so achieve their aim.

sentences.zh: 今使貢士必核以實，其有小疵，勿強衣飾，[二] 出處默語，各因其方，則蕭、曹、周、韓之倫，何足不致，吳、鄧、梁、竇之屬，企踵可待。

translations.literal: Now if recommended men were verified against fact, their small blemishes not forcibly dressed up, [2] whether retiring or outspoken, each matched to his bent—then could men like Xiao He, Cao Shen, Zhou Bo, and Han Xin fail to appear? Men of the caliber of Wu, Deng, Liang, and Dou could be had standing on tiptoe.
translations.idiomatic: Were nominees tested for real substance, petty faults left unvarnished, and temperaments matched to roles—Xiao He, Cao Shen, Zhou Bo, and Han Xin would crowd the gates; even Wu, Deng, Liang, and Dou would arrive at a beckon.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「未之思也，夫何遠之有？

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'They have not reflected on it—how could it be far?'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, 'They have not thought it through—how then could it be distant?

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]詩小雅曰：「它山之石，可以攻玉。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Odes, Lesser Ya, says: 'Stones from another hill may serve to polish jade.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The Lesser Ya: 'Alien stone may grind this jade.

sentences.zh: 」今之金工發金色者，皆淬之於鹽水焉。

translations.literal: Today's goldsmiths who bring out gold color all quench it in brine.
translations.idiomatic: Modern smiths dip gold in salt water to draw its luster.

sentences.zh: 注[二]衣飾謂裝飾以成其過也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 'Dressing up' means ornamenting to complete a fault.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 'Dressing' here means papering over defects with ornament.

sentences.zh: 衣音於氣反。

translations.literal: Yi is read yuqi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Yi is read yuqi fan.

sentences.zh: 愛日篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'Cherishing the Days' says:
translations.idiomatic: The essay "Cherishing the Days" says:

sentences.zh: 國之所以為國者，以有民也。

translations.literal: That by which a state is a state is that it has a people.
translations.idiomatic: A realm is a realm because it has a people;

sentences.zh: 民之所以為民者，以有谷也。

translations.literal: That by which the people are the people is that they have grain.
translations.idiomatic: the people are a people because they have grain;

sentences.zh: 谷之所以豐殖者，以有民功也。

translations.literal: That by which grain grows abundant is that the people have labor to devote to it.
translations.idiomatic: grain multiplies because the people can work the soil;

sentences.zh: 功之所以能建者，以日力也。

translations.literal: That by which labor can be accomplished is that they have the days for it.
