translations.idiomatic: work succeeds because days are not stolen from them.

sentences.zh: 化國之日舒以長，故其民閒暇而力有餘；

translations.literal: In a well-governed state the days are long and leisurely, so the people have ease and strength to spare;
translations.idiomatic: Under good rule the daylight feels long—people have leisure and surplus strength;

sentences.zh: 亂國之日促以短，故其民困務而力不足。

translations.literal: in a chaotic state the days are hurried and short, so the people are harried in their tasks and lack strength.
translations.idiomatic: under bad rule the hours race—people are driven to exhaustion.

sentences.zh: 舒長者，非謂羲和安行，[一] 乃君明民靜而力有餘也。

translations.literal: Long and leisurely does not mean Xi and He move the sun slowly; [1] it means the ruler is enlightened, the people quiet, and strength abounds.
translations.idiomatic: Leisurely length is not slow suns; it means clear sovereigns and calm subjects with energy to spare.

sentences.zh: 促短者，非謂分度損減，[二]乃上闇下亂，力不足也。

translations.literal: Hurried and short does not mean the degrees of the calendar are reduced; [2] it means the ruler is dark, those below in turmoil, and strength fails.
translations.idiomatic: Short hurry is not a shrunken calendar; it means benighted thrones, riot below, and spent strength.

sentences.zh: 孔子稱「既庶則富之，既富乃教之」。

translations.literal: Confucius said, 'When they are numerous, enrich them; when they are enriched, instruct them.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius taught, 'Make them numerous, then enrich them; enrich them, then teach them.'

sentences.zh: 是故禮義生於富足，盜竊起於貧窮；

translations.literal: Therefore ritual and right arise from plenty, theft and robbery from want;
translations.idiomatic: Hence rites and justice grow from abundance, theft and banditry from privation;

sentences.zh: 富足生於寬暇，貧窮起於無日。

translations.literal: plenty arises from ample time, want from having no days left.
translations.idiomatic: abundance comes from unharried days, want from days devoured by the state.

sentences.zh: 聖人深知力者民之本，國之基也，故務省徭役，使之愛日。

translations.literal: The sage deeply knew that labor is the root of the people and the foundation of the state, therefore he labored to lighten corvée and tax so they might cherish their days.
translations.idiomatic: Sages understood labor as the people's root and the state's base, and strove to ease corvée so folk could husband their hours.

sentences.zh: 是以堯□羲和，欽若昊天，敬授民時。

translations.literal: Therefore Yao [lacuna] Xi and He, reverently obeyed August Heaven, and respectfully imparted the seasons to the people.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Yao charged Xi and He to revere Heaven and hand down the farming seasons.

sentences.zh: 明帝時，公車以反支日不受章奏，[三]帝聞而怪曰：「民廢農桑，遠來詣闕，而復拘以禁忌，豈為政之意乎！

translations.literal: In Emperor Ming's time the public carriage office refused memorials on fanzhi days; [3] when the emperor heard of it he wondered, 'The people neglect farming and silkworms to come far to the palace gates, yet you bind them with taboos—can this be the intent of government!'
translations.idiomatic: Under Mingdi the capital coach office rejected petitions on unlucky days; the emperor exclaimed, 'Men abandon field and loom to reach our gates, and clerks block them with superstition—is that governance?

sentences.zh: 」於是遂蠲其制。

translations.literal: Thereupon he abolished that regulation.
translations.idiomatic: He therefore scrapped the rule.

translations.literal: The manuscript carries an asterisk marking textual uncertainty.
translations.idiomatic: An asterisk in the edition flags damaged text.

translations.literal: (Decree)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss '(order)' supplies a missing graph.

sentences.zh: **[今]*冤民仰希申訴，而令長以神自畜，[四]百姓廢農桑而趨府廷者，相續道路，非朝餔不得通，非意氣不得見。

translations.literal: **[Now]* wronged people look up hoping to lodge complaints, yet magistrates hoard themselves like spirits; [4] commoners abandon farming and silkworms to throng yamen gates in an unbroken line on the roads—they cannot get through except at noon or dusk, cannot be seen except when the official is pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Today the injured petition heaven for redress while district magistrates hide like gods; peasants desert plow and loom to pack the courthouse lane, admitted only at the noon or dusk audiences, granted audience only if the clerk is in humor.

sentences.zh: [五]或連日累月，更相瞻視；

translations.literal: [5] Some wait days on end, months in succession, staring at one another;
translations.idiomatic: Some camp for days and weeks, exchanging helpless glances;

sentences.zh: 或轉請鄰里，饋糧應對。

translations.literal: others importune neighbors and must bring grain to sustain the waiting and answers.
translations.idiomatic: others beg neighbors for food to endure the wait and the questioning.

sentences.zh: 歲功既虧，天下豈無受其饑者乎？

translations.literal: If the year's harvest is thus ruined, can the world avoid hunger?
translations.idiomatic: When the year's tillage is lost in queues, can the empire escape famine?

sentences.zh: 注[一]羲和，日也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Xi and He here mean the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Here Xi and He stand for the solar course.

sentences.zh: 《山海經》曰：「東南海之外，甘水之閒，有羲和之國。

translations.literal: The Guideways Through Mountains and Seas says: 'Beyond the southeast sea, amid the sweet waters, lies the land of Xi and He.
translations.idiomatic: The Shanhaijing: 'South-east of the sea, between sweet rivers, lies Xi-He's country.

sentences.zh: 有女子曰羲和，方浴日於甘泉。

translations.literal: There is a woman named Xi and He who is bathing the sun in a sweet spring.
translations.idiomatic: A woman named Xi He bathes the sun in a sweet spring.

sentences.zh: 羲和者，帝俊之妻，是生十日。

translations.literal: Xi and He is the wife of Emperor Jun; she gave birth to the ten suns.
translations.idiomatic: She is Di Jun's wife and mother of the ten suns.

sentences.zh: 」郭璞注曰：「羲和蓋天地始生日月者也。」

translations.literal: Guo Pu's commentary says: 'Xi and He are those who first gave birth to sun and moon for heaven and earth.'"
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu glosses them as primordial parents of the luminaries.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]洛書甄耀度曰「凡周天三百六十五度四分度之一，一度為千九百三十二里。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The Luoshu Zhenyao du says: 'A full revolution is three hundred sixty-five and one-quarter degrees; one degree is one thousand nine hundred thirty-two li.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The Apocryphal Luoshu: 'The circuit is 365¼ degrees; each degree spans 1,932 li.

sentences.zh: 日一日行一度，月一日行十三度十九分度之一」也。

translations.literal: The sun travels one degree a day, the moon thirteen and nineteen thirty-seconds of a degree per day.'"
translations.idiomatic: The sun moves one degree daily, the moon thirteen and nineteen thirty-seconds.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]凡反支日，用月朔為正。

translations.literal: Comment 3 In general fanzhi days take the new moon as pivot.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Fanzhi inauspicious days are reckoned from each month's first day.

sentences.zh: 戌﹑亥朔一日反支，申﹑酉朔二日反支，午﹑未朔三日反支，辰﹑巳朔四日反支，寅﹑卯朔五日反支，子﹑丑朔六日反支。

translations.literal: On a new moon in xu or hai, the first day is fanzhi; on shen or you, the second; on wu or wei, the third; on chen or si, the fourth; on yin or mao, the fifth; on zi or chou, the sixth.
translations.idiomatic: Xu/hai first days make day one fanzhi; shen/you make day two; wu/wei day three; chen/si day four; yin/mao day five; zi/chou day six.

sentences.zh: 見《陰陽書》也。

translations.literal: This is seen in the Yin-yang treatises.
translations.idiomatic: See the yin-yang manuals.

sentences.zh: 注[四]難見如神也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 They are as hard to meet as spirits.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 'Like spirits' means as hard to approach as gods.

sentences.zh: 注[五]《說文》曰：「餔謂日加申時也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 The Shuowen says: 'Bu is the time when the sun has reached the shen double-hour.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 The Shuowen defines bu as late afternoon when the sun stands at shen.

sentences.zh: 」今為「晡」字也。

translations.literal: Today this is written with the character for 'bu' (late afternoon).
translations.idiomatic: The graph is now written as bu, the late-afternoon designation.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「聽訟吾猶人也。

translations.literal: Confucius said, 'In hearing lawsuits I am like other men.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, 'When I sit in judgment I am no wiser than anyone else.'

sentences.zh: 」從此言之，中才以上，足議曲直，鄉亭部吏，亦有任決斷者，而類多枉曲，蓋有故焉。

translations.literal: From this it follows that men of middling talent and above are fully able to debate right and wrong, and clerks of township, post station, and department include some competent to decide—yet most bend the case awry, and there are reasons.
translations.idiomatic: From this we see that anyone of average ability can weigh justice, and local clerks sometimes can judge—yet verdicts are routinely crooked, and the causes lie deeper.

sentences.zh: 夫理直則恃正而不橈，事曲則諂意以行賕。

translations.literal: When the principle is straight one relies on rectitude and does not cringe; when the affair is crooked one fawns and offers bribes.
translations.idiomatic: The innocent stand on truth and refuse to grovel; the guilty flatter and buy favor.

sentences.zh: 不橈故無恩於吏，行賕故見私於法。

translations.literal: Not cringing therefore wins no favor from the clerk; offering bribes therefore finds private favor in the law.
translations.idiomatic: Uprightness earns no debt from the magistrate's clerk; bribes buy private mercy from the code.

sentences.zh: 若事有反覆，吏應坐之，吏以應坐之故，不得不枉之於庭。

translations.literal: If the case is reversed, the clerk ought to bear guilt for it; because he would bear guilt, he has no choice but to twist the outcome in court.
translations.idiomatic: When a verdict flips, the clerk should be punished—so to save himself he warps the hearing.

sentences.zh: 以羸民之少黨，而與豪吏對訟，其埶得無屈乎？

translations.literal: With the weak commoner lacking allies, matched against a powerful clerk in suit, how can the balance not tilt?
translations.idiomatic: How can a lone peasant, friendless, face a venal clerk and not be crushed?

sentences.zh: 縣承吏言，故與之同。

translations.literal: The county, accepting the clerk's word, therefore sides with him.
translations.idiomatic: The county magistrate trusts the clerk and follows his lead.

sentences.zh: 若事有反覆，縣亦應坐之，縣以應坐之故，而排之於郡。

translations.literal: If the case is reversed, the county too ought to bear guilt; because it would bear guilt, it shunts the matter up to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: If judgment flips, the county should answer for it—so it kicks the file upward to spare itself.

sentences.zh: 以一民之輕，而與一縣為訟，其理豈得申乎？

translations.literal: With one light-weight commoner suing an entire county, how can his grievance be heard?
translations.idiomatic: One private citizen against a whole district—how shall his cause prevail?

sentences.zh: 事有反覆，郡亦坐之，郡以共坐之故，而排之於州。

translations.literal: If the affair is reversed, the commandery too bears guilt; because of shared liability it pushes the case to the province.
translations.idiomatic: When blame would fall on the commandery, it passes the buck to the provincial office.

sentences.zh: 以一民之輕，與一郡為訟，其事豈獲勝乎？

translations.literal: With one light-weight commoner suing an entire commandery, how can he win?
translations.idiomatic: A single plaintiff against a commandery—what chance has he?

sentences.zh: 既不肯理，故乃遠詣公府。

translations.literal: When they refuse to take it up, he must journey far to the high ministries.
translations.idiomatic: Refused locally, he trudges to the capital tribunals.

sentences.zh: 公府復不能察，而當延以日月。

translations.literal: The high ministries again cannot investigate but stall him month after month.
translations.idiomatic: Even there the boards drag out the calendar and never decide.

sentences.zh: 貧弱者無以曠旬，強富者可盈千日。

translations.literal: The poor and weak cannot idle away ten-day spans; the rich and strong can fill a thousand days.
translations.idiomatic: Paupers cannot wait out endless weeks; magnates can spin a case for years.

sentences.zh: 理訟若此，何枉之能理乎？

translations.literal: If lawsuits are handled thus, how can any wrong be set right?
translations.idiomatic: Under such procedure, what injustice is ever redressed?

sentences.zh: 正士懷怨結而不見信，[一]猾吏崇奸軌而不被坐，此小民所以易侵苦，而天下所以多困窮也。

translations.literal: Upright men nurse sealed resentment and are not believed; [1] crafty clerks exalt villainy and escape punishment—this is why the little people are easily oppressed and the world is full of destitution.
translations.idiomatic: Honest men choke on unvented rage while [1] slick clerics prosper crime unpunished—hence the weak are trampled and want spreads across the realm.

sentences.zh: 注[一]信讀曰伸。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Xin is to be read as shen 'to extend, to have heard.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Xin here reads as shen, meaning to get a full hearing.

sentences.zh: 且除上天感痛致□，但以人功見事言之。

translations.literal: Moreover, setting aside whether High Heaven sends down sympathetic pain and reproof, take only what human effort and visible affairs show.
translations.idiomatic: Leaving aside Heaven's own grief, consider only what human agency reveals.

sentences.zh: 自三府州郡，至於鄉縣典司之吏，辭訟之民，官事相連，更相檢對者，日可有十萬人。

translations.literal: From the Three Offices and commanderies down to township and county clerks who manage lawsuits, the litigating people, and official business intertwined in mutual checks—each day there may be a hundred thousand persons involved.
translations.idiomatic: From capital boards to village yamens, clerks, suitors, and cross-filed cases may involve a hundred thousand people a day.

sentences.zh: 一人有事，二人經營，是為日三十萬人廢其業也。

translations.literal: When one man has a suit, two others run his errands—that makes three hundred thousand men a day who abandon their proper work.
translations.idiomatic: One lawsuit ties up three lives—three hundred thousand daily who forsake their trade.

sentences.zh: 以中農率之，則是歲三百萬人受其饑者也。

translations.literal: Figuring by the income of a middle farmer, that means in a year three million men suffer hunger from it.
translations.idiomatic: At a middling farmer's yield, that wastes enough labor to leave three million hungry each year.

sentences.zh: 然則盜賊何從而銷，太平何由而作乎？

translations.literal: In that case how shall banditry be quelled, and by what path shall great peace come?
translations.idiomatic: How then shall theft cease or the Great Peace arrive?

sentences.zh: 詩云：「莫肯念亂，誰無父母？

translations.literal: The Odes say, 'None will think of the disorder—who is without father and mother?'
translations.idiomatic: The Classic warns, 'No one heeds the chaos—yet who lacks parents to mourn?'

sentences.zh: 」[一]百姓不足，君誰與足？

translations.literal: ' [1] When the hundred surnames are not enough, with whom shall the lord be enough?
translations.idiomatic: ' [1] If the people lack means, what can sustain the throne?

sentences.zh: 可無思哉！

translations.literal: Can one fail to ponder this!
translations.idiomatic: Can rulers ignore this!

sentences.zh: 可無思哉！

translations.literal: Can one fail to ponder this!
translations.idiomatic: They must think on it again and again!

sentences.zh: 注[一]詩小雅也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 This is from the Lesser Ya of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The lines come from the Lesser Ya.

sentences.zh: 述赦篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Describing Amnesties' says:
translations.idiomatic: The essay "On Amnesties" declares:

sentences.zh: 凡療病者，必知脈之虛實，氣之所結，然後為之方，故疾可愈而壽可長也。

translations.literal: In general, to cure illness one must know whether the pulse is empty or full and where the vital breath is knotted, and only then prescribe—thus the disease can be healed and life prolonged.
translations.idiomatic: A physician must read pulse and blockage before dosing—so the state must diagnose popular woes before legislating.

sentences.zh: 為國者，必先知民之所苦，禍之所起，然後為之禁，故奸可塞而國可安也。

translations.literal: He who governs a state must first know what the people suffer and whence calamity arises, and only then impose prohibitions—thus villainy can be blocked and the state secured.
translations.idiomatic: Rulers must learn where harm begins before they ban it—then crime is choked off and the realm rests easy.

sentences.zh: 今日賊良民之甚者，莫大於數赦贖。

translations.literal: Today nothing injures good subjects more than repeated amnesties and ransom payments.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing harms the law-abiding like constant general pardons and paid commutations.

sentences.zh: 赦贖數，則惡人昌而善人傷矣。

translations.literal: When amnesties and ransoms are frequent, the wicked flourish and the good are wounded.
translations.idiomatic: Frequent mercy makes rogues bold and honest men bleed.

sentences.zh: 何以明之哉？

translations.literal: How may this be demonstrated?
translations.idiomatic: How is this shown?

sentences.zh: 夫謹□之人，身不蹈非，又有為吏正直，不避強禦，而奸猾之黨橫加誣言者，皆知赦之不久故也。

translations.literal: As for cautious [lacuna] men who never tread in wrong, or honest clerks who stand straight and do not flinch before the powerful—yet villainous packs slander them with lying accusations—they all know an amnesty is never far off.
translations.idiomatic: Steadfast men and upright clerks who never bend still get framed, because rogues bank on the next blanket pardon.

sentences.zh: 善人君子，被侵怨而能至闕庭自明者，萬無數人；

translations.literal: Of good men and gentlemen who suffer invasion and resentment yet can reach the palace gates to clear themselves, not one in ten thousand succeeds;
translations.idiomatic: Of worthy men wronged who reach the capital to plead, fewer than one in ten thousand arrives;

sentences.zh: 數人之中得省問者，百不過一；

translations.literal: of those few, not one in a hundred receives an imperial inquiry;
translations.idiomatic: of those, fewer than one in a hundred win an audience;

sentences.zh: 既對尚書而空遣去者，復什六七矣。

translations.literal: after facing the secretariat and being sent away empty, six or seven tenths more are gone.
translations.idiomatic: and six or seven in ten of the survivors leave with no result.

sentences.zh: 其輕薄奸軌，既陷罪法，怨毒之家冀其辜戮，以解畜憤，而反一□悉蒙赦釋，令惡人高會而誇宛，老盜服臧而過門，孝子見讎而不得討，遭盜者鶯物而不敢取，痛莫甚焉！

translations.literal: The frivolous villains, once trapped by the law, are hated by families who hope for their execution to vent stored fury—yet all at once [lacuna] they receive blanket pardon; the wicked hold high revel and boast, old thieves don stolen goods and stroll past the gate, filial sons see their enemies yet cannot avenge them, robbery victims spy their goods yet dare not seize them—no grief is greater!
translations.idiomatic: Families of victims long for executions to cool their rage—then a general amnesty lets every knave swagger home in stolen silks while sons may not avenge fathers and plundered men may not reclaim a bolt—what pain could exceed that?

sentences.zh: 夫養稂莠者傷禾稼，惠奸軌者賊良民。

translations.literal: To nurture darnel injures the grain; to favor villains injures good subjects.
translations.idiomatic: Coddling weeds starves the crop; coddling criminals starves the people.

sentences.zh: [一]書曰：「文王作罰，刑茲無赦。

translations.literal: The Documents say, 'King Wen made punishments; for such as these there was no pardon.'
translations.idiomatic: The Documents say King Wen fixed penalties and granted no indulgence to these crimes.

sentences.zh: 」[二] 先王之制刑法也，非好傷人肌膚，斷人壽命也；

translations.literal: ' [2] The punishments and laws of the former kings did not take pleasure in wounding flesh or cutting short lives; they valued awe for the villainous and feared the evil, removing harm from mankind.
translations.idiomatic: ' [2] The ancient kings framed penal law not to relish blood but to overawe the wicked and rid the world of harm.

sentences.zh: 貴威奸懲惡，除人害也。

translations.literal: They esteemed awe-inspiring authority to punish villainy and remove harm from the people.
translations.idiomatic: They meant to terrify the wicked, crush crime, and lift harm from the common people.

sentences.zh: 故經稱「天命有德，五服五章哉，天討有罪，五刑五用哉」；

translations.literal: Therefore the classic says, 'Heaven charged the virtuous with the five grades of dress; Heaven punished the guilty with the five employments of punishment';
translations.idiomatic: Hence the canon pairs 'Heaven invested the worthy with five insignia' with 'Heaven struck the guilty with five punishments';

sentences.zh: 詩刺「彼宜有罪，汝反脫之」。

translations.literal: the Odes satirize 'those who deserved guilt—you instead released them.'
translations.idiomatic: and the Odes mock rulers who 'free the guilty while binding the innocent.'

sentences.zh: [三]古者唯始受命之君，承大亂之極，寇賊奸軌，難為法禁，故不得不有一赦，與之更新，頤育萬民，以成大化。

translations.literal: [3] In antiquity only the first ruler who received the mandate, inheriting the utmost of great chaos when bandits and villains were hard to bind by law, was compelled to issue a single amnesty to renew the age, nurture the myriad people, and complete the great transformation.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Only a founding sovereign, inheriting total chaos when law could not yet bite, might issue one great amnesty to reset the era and rear the people.

sentences.zh: 非以養奸活罪，放縱天賊也。

translations.literal: It was not to nurture villainy, spare the guilty, and unleash Heaven's robbers.
translations.idiomatic: That was never meant to fatten crime or loose Heaven's thieves.

sentences.zh: 夫性惡之民，民之豺狼，雖得放宥之澤，終無改悔之心。

translations.literal: Men of vicious nature are wolves among the people; though they taste the dew of mercy, in the end they have no mind to reform.
translations.idiomatic: Innately evil men are human jackals; pardon only hardens them.

sentences.zh: 旦脫重梏，夕還囹圄，嚴明令尹，不能使其斷絕。

translations.literal: Morning freed from heavy fetters, evening they are back in prison—though the magistrate be stern, he cannot cut them off forever.
translations.idiomatic: They leave the stocks at dawn and re-enter the jail by dusk—no strict prefect can sever the chain.

sentences.zh: 何也？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why is this?

sentences.zh: 凡敢為大奸者，才必有過於觿，而能自媚於上者也。

translations.literal: Whoever dares the greatest villainy must have talent surpassing the common run and know how to flatter those above.
translations.idiomatic: Every arch-criminal combines unusual gifts with a gift for flattering superiors.

sentences.zh: 多散誕得之財，奉以諂諛之辭，以轉相驅，[四] 非有第五公之廉直，孰不為顧哉？

translations.literal: They scatter wealth gained by reckless boasts and offer sycophantic phrases, driving one another on in relays—[4] without the integrity of a Fifth Gong, who would not bend?
translations.idiomatic: They shower stolen riches and honeyed words in relays—[4] and without a Fifth Lun's probity, who stays straight?

sentences.zh: [五]論者多曰：「久不赦則奸軌熾而吏不制，宜數肆眚以解散之。

translations.literal: [5] Many debaters say: 'If long without an amnesty, villainy blazes and clerks cannot control it—one should often grant indulgence to scatter it.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Critics urge frequent clemency lest crime rage beyond control.

sentences.zh: 」此未昭政亂之本源，不察禍福之所生也。

translations.literal: ' This does not clarify the root of governmental disorder or examine whence fortune and misfortune arise.'
translations.idiomatic: They mistake symptom for source and never trace blessing or bane to its root.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]《爾雅》曰：「稂，童徹。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Erya says: 'Lang is tongche.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Erya: lang is tongche (false grain).

sentences.zh: 」郭璞注云：「莠類也。

translations.literal: Guo Pu's commentary says: 'It is a kind of weed.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu calls it a tare resembling grain.

sentences.zh: 」詩曰：「不稂不莠。

translations.literal: The Odes say, 'Neither darnel nor false grain.'
translations.idiomatic: The Odes cry, 'No darnel, no tares among our fields.'

sentences.zh: 」稂音郎。

translations.literal: Lang is read lang.
translations.idiomatic: The reading is lang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]康誥之言也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 These are words from the Kanggao.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The quotation comes from the Kanggao.

sentences.zh: 注[三]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 This is from the Greater Ya of the Odes.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 The lines belong to the Greater Ya.

sentences.zh: 「此宜無罪，汝反收之；

translations.literal: 'This one surely had no crime, yet you seized him;
translations.idiomatic: 'The innocent you bound;

sentences.zh: 彼宜有罪，汝反脫之」。

translations.literal: that one surely had guilt, yet you released him.'"
translations.idiomatic: the guilty you let slip.'"

sentences.zh: 毛萇注云：「脫，赦也。」

translations.literal: Mao Chang's commentary says: 'Tuo means to pardon.'"
translations.idiomatic: Mao's gloss: tuo means amnesty.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]誕猶虛也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 Dan here means empty or hollow.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Dan means hollow boasting.

sentences.zh: 注[五]謂第五倫也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 This means Fifth Lun.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Fifth Lun is meant.

sentences.zh: 為司空，性廉直也。

translations.literal: He served as minister of works and was by nature honest and upright.
translations.idiomatic: As minister of works he was famed for blunt integrity.

sentences.zh: 後度遼將軍皇甫規解官歸安定，鄉人有以貨得鴈門太守者，亦去職還家，書刺謁規。

translations.literal: Later, when the Liao River general Huangfu Gui resigned office and returned to Anding, a fellow townsman who had bought the governorship of Yanmen also left office and came home; he sent in his visiting card to pay respects to Gui.
translations.idiomatic: When Huangfu Gui retired to Anding, a neighbor who had purchased the Yanmen governorship also came home and called on him with a formal card.

sentences.zh: 規臥不迎，既入而問：「卿前在郡食鴈美乎？

translations.literal: Gui lay down and would not go out to greet him; after the man entered he asked, 'Did the wild goose taste good when you fed in that commandery?'
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu feigned illness, then asked pointedly whether 'wild goose' had tasted well in his lucrative post.

sentences.zh: 」有頃，又白王符在門。

translations.literal: After a while someone announced that Wang Fu was at the door.
translations.idiomatic: Soon an attendant reported Wang Fu outside.

sentences.zh: 規素聞符名，乃驚遽而起，衣不及帶，屣履出迎，援符手而還，與同坐，極歡。

translations.literal: Gui had long heard Fu's name; startled, he scrambled up, his sash not tied, and in slippers hurried out, took Fu by the hand, and led him in to sit together in the utmost delight.
translations.idiomatic: At Wang Fu's name Huangfu leaped up half-dressed, shuffled out in slippers, seized his hand, and ushered him in as an honored guest.

sentences.zh: 時人為之語曰：「徒見二千石，不如一縫掖。

translations.literal: People of the time made a saying: 'It is better to see one scholar in a large-sleeved robe than a two-thousand-bushel official.
translations.idiomatic: Wits said meeting a poor scholar beats dining with a two-thousand-bushel grandee.

sentences.zh: 」[一]言書生道義之為貴也。

translations.literal: ' [1] It means the moral weight of the bookish class is prized.
translations.idiomatic: ' [1] The jest praises learning over rank.

sentences.zh: 符竟不仕，終於家。

translations.literal: Fu never took office and ended his days at home.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Fu died a private gentleman, never serving.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《禮記·儒行》孔子曰：「丘少居魯，衣逢掖之衣。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Record of Rites, 'Conduct of the Scholar,' Confucius says: 'In youth I dwelt in Lu and wore the large-sleeved robe of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Liji, 'Conduct of the Scholar': Confucius wore Lu's broad-sleeved scholar's coat.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「逢猶大也。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says: 'Feng means large.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses feng as 'ample.'

sentences.zh: 大掖之衣，大袂單衣也。」

translations.literal: The large-sleeved robe is a single garment with wide sleeves.'"
translations.idiomatic: The fengye robe is a loose gown with wide sleeves.'"

sentences.zh: 仲長統

translations.literal: Zhongchang Tong
translations.idiomatic: Section heading: Zhongchang Tong.

sentences.zh: 仲長統字公理，山陽高平人也。

translations.literal: Zhongchang Tong, courtesy name Gongli, was a man of Gaoping in Shanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongchang Tong, style Gongli, came from Gaoping in Shanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: 少好學，博涉書記，贍於文辭。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning, read widely in documents and annals, and was rich in literary expression.
translations.idiomatic: He studied voraciously, mastered many texts, and wrote with uncommon fluency.

sentences.zh: 年二十餘，遊學青﹑徐﹑並﹑冀之閒，與交友者多異之。

translations.literal: In his twenties he traveled for study among Qing, Xu, Bing, and Ji, and most of those who befriended him thought him extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Before thirty he wandered the northern provinces; companions found him remarkable.

sentences.zh: 并州刺史高干，袁紹甥也。

translations.literal: The inspector of Bing Province, Gao Gan, was Yuan Shao's nephew.
translations.idiomatic: Bing inspector Gao Gan was Yuan Shao's nephew.

sentences.zh: 素貴有名，招致四方游士，士多歸附。

translations.literal: He had long been noble and famous and summoned wandering scholars from the four quarters; many men attached themselves to him.
translations.idiomatic: Proud of his pedigree, he gathered itinerant scholars in crowds.

sentences.zh: 統過干，干善待遇，訪以當時之事。

translations.literal: Tong visited Gan, who treated him well and asked his opinion on affairs of the day.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongchang called on him; Gan was courteous and pressed him for policy.

sentences.zh: 統謂干曰：「君有雄志而無雄才，好士而不能擇人，所以為君深戒也。

translations.literal: Tong said to Gan: 'You harbor heroic ambition but lack heroic talent; you love scholars yet cannot pick men—this is what I would urgently warn you against.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongchang warned him: 'You aim high but judge poorly; you patronize talent yet cannot sort the worthy from the worthless—take that as grave counsel.

sentences.zh: 」干雅自多，不納其言，統遂去之。

translations.literal: Gan prided himself on his superiority and would not heed the words, so Tong left him.
translations.idiomatic: Gan, vain as ever, ignored him, and Zhongchang departed.

sentences.zh: 無幾，干以并州叛，卒至於敗。

translations.literal: Before long Gan rebelled in Bing Province and in the end was ruined.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after, Gan revolted in Bing and came to ruin.

sentences.zh: [一]並冀之士皆以是異統。

translations.literal: [1] Scholars of Bing and Ji therefore all the more thought Tong unusual.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Northern literati then esteemed Zhongchang's foresight.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]魏志曰：「高干叛，欲*[南]*奔*

translations.literal: [2] Comment 1 The Wei zhi says: 'When Gao Gan rebelled he wished *[south]* to flee *
translations.idiomatic: [2] Note 1 The Wei chronicle says Gao Gan, in revolt, tried to flee south—

translations.literal: (south)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss '(south)' marks an editorial restoration,

sentences.zh: *荊州，上洛都尉王琰捕斬之」也。

translations.literal: *to Jing Province; the Luoyang commandant Wang Yan captured and beheaded him.'
translations.idiomatic: toward Jing Province—until Wang Yan of Shangluo cut him down.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]異其有知人之鑒也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 They marveled that he could read men.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 They praised his discernment of character.

sentences.zh: 統性俶儻，敢直言，不矜小節，默語無常，時人或謂之狂生。

translations.literal: Tong's nature was forthright and daring; he spoke bluntly without fussing over small points; whether silent or speaking he was unpredictable, and some of his contemporaries called him a madcap scholar.
translations.idiomatic: He was blunt, erratic, careless of decorum—some called him a wild scholar.

sentences.zh: 每州郡命召，輒稱疾不就。

translations.literal: Whenever a province or commandery summoned him, he always pleaded illness and would not go.
translations.idiomatic: Every official summons he declined on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 常以為凡游帝王者，欲以立身揚名耳，而名不常存，人生易滅，優遊偃仰，可以自娛，欲卜居清曠，以樂其志，論之曰：「使居有良田廣宅，背山臨流，溝池環□，竹木周布，場圃築前，果園樹後。

translations.literal: He used to think that those who wander in attendance on emperors and kings only wish to establish themselves and spread their names, yet fame is not constant and human life is quickly snuffed out—better to roam at ease and amuse oneself; he wished to choose a clear and spacious dwelling to delight his aims, and he discussed it, saying: 'Suppose one's dwelling had good fields and a broad house, backed by hills and facing a stream, with ditches and pools encircling [lacuna], bamboo and trees all around, vegetable plots before the gate and an orchard behind.
translations.idiomatic: He argued that courting power wins only fleeting name while life is brief—far better to retire; he sketched an ideal estate: fertile acres, a broad hall against hills above a river, ditches and groves ringing the wall, kitchen gardens forward and fruit trees behind.

sentences.zh: 舟車足以代步涉之艱，使令足以息四體之役。

translations.literal: Boats and carts enough to spare the hardship of walking; servants enough to spare the toil of the four limbs.
translations.idiomatic: Boats and wagons remove the ache of travel; a few retainers spare one's limbs.

sentences.zh: 養親有兼珍之膳，妻孥無苦身之勞。

translations.literal: To nurture parents there are dishes of doubled delicacies; wife and children know no bitter labor of the body.
translations.idiomatic: Parents dine on doubled delicacies; wife and children never know crushing toil.

sentences.zh: [一]良朋萃止，則陳酒餚以娛之；

translations.literal: [1] When good friends gather, spread wine and viands to delight them;
translations.idiomatic: [1] When friends visit, lay out wine and savories;

sentences.zh: 嘉時吉日，則亨羔豚以奉之。

translations.literal: on fine seasons and auspicious days, boil lamb and suckling pig to offer them.
translations.idiomatic: on festival days roast lamb and pork in their honor.

sentences.zh: 木躇畦苑，遊戲平林，[二]濯清水，追涼風，釣遊鯉，弋高鴻。

translations.literal: Stroll the nurseries and gardens, sport in the level woods, [2] bathe in clear water, chase the cool breeze, angle for swimming carp, shoot wild geese on the wing.
translations.idiomatic: Ramble the nursery slopes, idle in open groves, bathe in clear pools, ride the summer wind, hook carp, wing-shoot wild geese.

sentences.zh: 諷於舞雩之下，詠歸高堂之上。

translations.literal: Hum poetry beneath the Rain Dance altar, chant homeward songs in the high hall.
translations.idiomatic: Chant verses below the Wu rain altar, sing homecoming songs in the upper hall.

sentences.zh: [三]安神閨房，思老氏之玄虛；

translations.literal: [3] Calm the spirit in the women's quarters, pondering Laozi's mystery and emptiness;
translations.idiomatic: [3] You quiet the mind in private chambers, mulling Laozi's teaching of dark emptiness;

sentences.zh: 呼吸精和，求至人之彷彿。

translations.literal: breathe in essential harmony, seeking to resemble the perfected man.
translations.idiomatic: you draw in vital breath, straining after the sage who has left self behind.

sentences.zh: [四]與達者數子，論道講書，俯仰二儀，錯綜人物。

translations.literal: [4] With several companions who have attained insight, debate the Way and expound the texts, looking up and down on the two poles, weaving together the patterns of mankind.
translations.idiomatic: [4] With a few enlightened friends you dispute doctrine and parse the classics, scanning heaven and earth and threading human affairs into sense.

sentences.zh: 彈南風之雅操，發清商之妙曲。

translations.literal: Strum the noble airs of the 'South Wind,' unleash the subtle melodies of the clear shang mode.
translations.idiomatic: You pluck Shun's southern breeze on the zither and lift the pure shang tunes.

sentences.zh: [五]消搖一世之上，睥睨天地之閒。

translations.literal: [5] Roaming free above the generation, squinting sidelong between heaven and earth,
translations.idiomatic: [5] You float above the age, glancing proudly between sky and soil,

sentences.zh: 不受當時之責，永保性命之期。

translations.literal: not bearing the blame of the moment, forever preserving the term of your life.
translations.idiomatic: free of the era's reproaches, guarding the full span of life.

sentences.zh: 如是，則可以陵霄漢，出宇宙之外矣。

translations.literal: In such a case one may vault the Milky Way and pass beyond the rim of the cosmos.
translations.idiomatic: Then you may cross the River of Stars and step outside the shell of space.

sentences.zh: 豈羨夫入帝王之門哉！」

translations.literal: Would one envy entering the gates of emperors and kings!'
translations.idiomatic: Why should one envy the antechambers of kings?'

sentences.zh: 又作詩二篇，以見其志。

translations.literal: He also composed two poems to reveal his intent.
translations.idiomatic: He added two poems to spell out his mind.

sentences.zh: 辭曰：

translations.literal: The text runs:
translations.idiomatic: The verses read:

sentences.zh: 注[一]孥讀曰奴。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Nu is to be read as nu 'slave.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Nu here means children or household dependents.

sentences.zh: 注[二]木躇猶踟木也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Mu chu means pacing in the woods.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 'Wood-strolling' is wandering among trees.

sentences.zh: 注[三]雩，祭旱之名也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Yu is the name of the sacrifice for drought.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Yu names the rain-prayer rite for drought.

sentences.zh: 為壇而□其上，以祈雨焉。

translations.literal: They build an altar and [lacuna] above it to pray for rain.
translations.idiomatic: They raise a platform and stack the offering there to plead for showers.

sentences.zh: 《論語》曾點曰：「春服既成，冠者五六人，童子六七人，浴乎沂，風乎舞雩，詠而歸。」

translations.literal: Zeng Dian in the Analects said: 'When spring garments are ready, with five or six capped youths and six or seven boys, bathe in the Yi, take the wind at the Rain Dance altar, and chant our way home.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Analects quotes Zeng Dian bathing in the Yi and singing home from the Wu rain altar.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]《老子》曰：「玄之又玄，虛其心，實其腹。

translations.literal: Comment 4 The Laozi says: 'Mystery of mysteries—empty the mind, fill the belly.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Laozi: 'Deeper than deep—empty the heart, fill the belly.

sentences.zh: 」呼吸謂嚥氣養生也。

translations.literal: Breathing means swallowing breath to nurture life.
translations.idiomatic: Controlled breathing is the art of ingesting qi for longevity.

sentences.zh: 《莊子》曰：「吹煦呼吸，吐故納新。

translations.literal: The Zhuangzi says: 'Blow and breathe, expel the old, draw in the new.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi teaches 'puffing and sipping, casting out stale breath, drawing fresh.'

sentences.zh: 」又曰「至人無己」也。

translations.literal: It also says, 'The perfected man has no self.'"
translations.idiomatic: It adds that the perfected man 'has no separate ego.'"

sentences.zh: 注[五]家語曰：「舜彈五弦之琴，造南風之詩曰：『南風之熏兮，可以解吾人之慍兮。

translations.literal: Comment 5 The Family Sayings records: 'Shun strummed the five-stringed zither and made the poem to the South Wind: "How gentle the south wind—it can unbind the people's fret.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 The Kongzi jiayu: Shun played five strings and sang, 'How soft the south wind—it lifts the people's gloom.

sentences.zh: 南風之時兮，可以阜吾人之財兮。

translations.literal: How timely the south wind—it can enrich the people's wealth."
translations.idiomatic: How timely that breeze—it swells the people's wealth.'"

sentences.zh: 』」三禮圖曰：「琴本五弦，曰宮﹑商﹑角﹑征﹑羽，文王增二，曰少宮﹑少商，弦最清也。」

translations.literal: The Three Rites Illustrations says: 'The zither originally had five strings—gong, shang, jue, zhi, yu; King Wen added two, shao gong and shao shang, the clearest strings.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Sanli tu adds that Wen added two higher strings for clarity.'"

sentences.zh: 飛鳥遺跡，蟬蛻亡殼。

translations.literal: The flying bird leaves its tracks; the cicada casts off its shell.
translations.idiomatic: Birds vanish leaving only tracks; cicadas slip the husk and fly.

sentences.zh: 騰蛇□鱗，神龍喪角。

translations.literal: The soaring serpent [lacuna] its scales; the divine dragon sheds its horns.
translations.idiomatic: The winged serpent strips its scales; the dragon casts its horn-crown.

sentences.zh: [一]至人能變，達士拔俗。

translations.literal: [1] The perfected man can transform; the enlightened scholar rises above the vulgar.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Sages transfigure themselves; wise men shake off the dust.

sentences.zh: 乘雲無轡，騁風無足。

translations.literal: Riding the clouds he needs no reins; galloping the wind he needs no feet.
translations.idiomatic: Cloud-chariots need no bridles; wind-steeds need no hooves.

sentences.zh: 垂露成幃，張霄成幄。

translations.literal: Dew hanging forms a curtain; stretched aurora forms a canopy.
translations.idiomatic: Dripping dew weaves curtains; stretched rainbows roof the sky.

sentences.zh: 沆瀣當餐，九陽代燭。

translations.literal: Mist-dew serves for breakfast; the nine suns replace candles.
translations.idiomatic: Night vapor becomes your meal; nine suns replace lamp-flame.

sentences.zh: [二]恆星艷珠，朝霞潤玉。

translations.literal: [2] Fixed stars gleam like pearls; morning rosy clouds moisten like jade.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Constellations shine pearl-bright; dawn clouds wash you like cool jade.

sentences.zh: 六合之內，恣心所欲。

translations.literal: Within the six directions the heart may roam as it will.
translations.idiomatic: Inside the six reaches you follow every whim.

sentences.zh: 人事可遺，何為侷促？

translations.literal: Human affairs may be cast aside—why stay cramped and small?
translations.idiomatic: Why cling to mortal bother when you could be vast?

sentences.zh: 注[一]王充《論衡》曰：「蠐螬化為復育，復育轉為蟬。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Wang Chong's Lunheng says: 'The grub becomes the pupa, the pupa turns into the cicada.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Wang Chong compares metamorphosis: grub to pupa to cicada.

sentences.zh: 蟬之去復育，龜之解甲，蛇之脫皮，可謂屍解矣。

translations.literal: The cicada leaving the pupa, the tortoise shedding its shell, the snake sloughing its skin—these may be called liberation of the corpse.
translations.idiomatic: Leaving the pupa, like tortoise shell or serpent skin, is the Daoist 'release from the corpse.'

sentences.zh: 」蛻音式銳反。

translations.literal: Tui is read shirui fan.
translations.idiomatic: Tui is read shirui.

sentences.zh: 《爾雅》曰：「騰蛇有鱗。

translations.literal: The Erya says: 'The soaring serpent has scales.
translations.idiomatic: Erya: the flying serpent bears scales.

sentences.zh: 」《廣雅》曰：「有角曰龍。

translations.literal: The Guangya says: 'What has horns is called a dragon.
translations.idiomatic: Guangya: horned dragons are true dragons.

sentences.zh: 」喪角，解角也。

translations.literal: Losing horns means shedding the horns.
translations.idiomatic: To lose horns is to slough them like skin.

sentences.zh: 注[二]霄，摩天赤氣也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Xiao is the crimson vapor that brushes heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Xiao is the red mist scraping the sky.

sentences.zh: 在旁曰幃，在上曰幄。

translations.literal: At the side it is called wei; above it is called wo.
translations.idiomatic: Side-hangings are wei; overhead hangings are wo.

sentences.zh: 陵陽子明經曰：「沆瀣者，北方夜半氣也。

translations.literal: Master Lingyang's scripture says: 'Hangxie is the midnight vapor of the north.
translations.idiomatic: Lingyang Ziming's scripture defines hangxie as northern midnight breath.

sentences.zh: 」九陽謂日也。

translations.literal: Nine yang means the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Nine yangs stand for the sun.

sentences.zh: 《山海經》曰「陽谷上有扶木，九日居下枝，一日居上枝」也。

translations.literal: The Guideways Through Mountains and Seas says, 'On the Yang Valley mulberry the nine suns dwell on the lower boughs, one sun on the upper bough.'"
translations.idiomatic: Shanhaijing places nine suns on Fusang's lower branches, one above.'"

sentences.zh: 大道雖夷，見幾者寡。

translations.literal: Though the great Way is level, few see the subtle turning.
translations.idiomatic: The Way lies plain, yet few spy the hinge of change.

sentences.zh: 任意無非，適物無可。

translations.literal: Do as you will and nothing is wrong; suit things and nothing is blocked.
translations.idiomatic: Act without forcing and blame fades; fit the moment and obstruction ends.

sentences.zh: 古來繞繞，委曲如瑣。

translations.literal: From of old the world has wound and tangled, crooked as chain links.
translations.idiomatic: Since antiquity the world has coiled like linked rings.

sentences.zh: 百慮何為，至要在我。

translations.literal: Why pile a hundred cares—the crux lies in myself.
translations.idiomatic: Why multiply anxieties? The pivot is in me.

sentences.zh: 寄愁天上，埋憂地下。

translations.literal: Send sorrow up to heaven, bury grief beneath the earth.
translations.idiomatic: Hang grief in the sky, inter worry underground.

sentences.zh: 叛散五經，滅□風﹑雅。

translations.literal: Rebel against the Five Classics, blot out [lacuna] the Feng and Ya.
translations.idiomatic: Cast off the Five Classics, erase the Airs and Elegants,

sentences.zh: 百家雜碎，請用從火。

translations.literal: the hundred schools' clutter—please commit them to the fire.
translations.idiomatic: and toss the hundred schools' scraps to the flames.

sentences.zh: 抗志山棲，游心海左。

translations.literal: Set your will on mountain seclusion, let your heart roam the eastern sea.
translations.idiomatic: Plant your purpose in high hills, sail your mind beyond the eastern sea.

sentences.zh: 元氣為舟，微風為柂。

translations.literal: Primordial vapor becomes your boat, the light breeze your rudder.
translations.idiomatic: Chaos-qi builds your hull, soft wind trims the stern.

sentences.zh: [一]敖翔太清，縱意容冶。

translations.literal: [1] Soar in the Grand Clarity, give rein to ease and grace.
translations.idiomatic: [1] You wheel in the empyrean, indulging poise and polish.

sentences.zh: 注[一]柂，船尾也，音徒可反。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Tuo is the stern of the boat, read tuo fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Tuo names the steering oar at the stern.

sentences.zh: 尚書令荀彧聞統名，奇之，舉為尚書郎。

translations.literal: Director of the Secretariat Xun Yu heard Tong's name, marveled at him, and recommended him as a gentleman of the Secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Yu, head of the secretariat, heard of Zhongchang, admired him, and nominated him as a secretary.

sentences.zh: 後參丞相曹操軍事。

translations.literal: Later he served as adviser on the army staff of Chancellor Cao Cao.
translations.idiomatic: He later joined Cao Cao's military staff as strategist.

sentences.zh: 每論說古今及時俗行事，恆發憤歎息。

translations.literal: Whenever he discussed past and present or current practice, he always burst into indignant sighs.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever he spoke of history or policy he broke into angry laments.

sentences.zh: 因著論名曰昌言，[一]凡三十四篇，十餘萬言。

translations.literal: Therefore he wrote a treatise entitled Changyan; [1] in all thirty-four chapters, more than a hundred thousand characters.
translations.idiomatic: He therefore composed the Changyan in thirty-four pian—over a hundred thousand graphs. Note 1: chang means 'straight.'

sentences.zh: 注[一]昌，當也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Chang means 'appropriate.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Chang carries the sense of 'fitting speech.'

sentences.zh: 尚書曰：「汝亦昌言。」

translations.literal: The Documents says, 'You too should speak fitting words.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Shangshu bids ministers to 'speak straight.'"

sentences.zh: 獻帝遜位之歲，統卒，時年四十一。

translations.literal: In the year the Emperor abdicated, Tong died, aged forty-one.
translations.idiomatic: The year the emperor yielded the throne, Zhongchang died at forty-one.

sentences.zh: 友人東海繆襲常稱統才章足繼西京董﹑賈﹑劉﹑楊。

translations.literal: His friend Miao Xi of Donghai often said Tong's literary talent could follow Dong Zhongshu, Jia Yi, Liu Xiang, and Yang Xiong of Western Capitals fame.
translations.idiomatic: His friend Miao Xi ranked his prose with the great Western Han masters Dong, Jia, Liu, and Yang.

sentences.zh: [一]今簡撮其書有益政者，略載之雲。

translations.literal: [1] Now I select and excerpt passages from his book that benefit government, recording them briefly below.
translations.idiomatic: What follows excerpts the politically useful parts of that work.

sentences.zh: 注[一]董仲舒﹑賈誼﹑劉向﹑楊雄也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Dong Zhongshu, Jia Yi, Liu Xiang, and Yang Xiong.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The four Han writers named above.

sentences.zh: 襲字熙伯，辟御史府，後至尚書﹑光祿勳。

translations.literal: Xi, courtesy name Xibo, was summoned to the censorate and later rose to director of the secretariat and superintendent of the imperial household.
translations.idiomatic: Miao Xi, style Xibo, entered the censorate and ended as secretariat director and chamberlain for the palace.

sentences.zh: 理亂篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Order and Disorder' says:
translations.idiomatic: The essay "On Order and Disorder" says:

sentences.zh: 豪傑之當天命者，未始有天下之分者也。

translations.literal: Heroes who answer Heaven's mandate have never yet held a predetermined share of the realm.
translations.idiomatic: No warlord who seizes Heaven's nod begins with a guaranteed empire.

sentences.zh: 無天下之分，故戰爭者競起焉。

translations.literal: Without a predetermined share, therefore wars of competition arise.
translations.idiomatic: Without fixed shares, contention explodes.

sentences.zh: 於斯之時，並偽假天威，矯據方國，擁甲兵與我角才智，程勇力與我競雌雄，不知去就，疑誤天下，蓋不可數也。

translations.literal: At such a time each falsely borrows Heaven's awesomeness, arrogantly occupies a region, arms soldiers to match wits with me, measures courage against mine to decide mastery, ignorant of advance or retreat, misleading the world—such men cannot be counted.
translations.idiomatic: Each pretends to divine sanction, seizes a corner, marshals arms to rival my wit and nerve, confuses the age—legion are their names.

sentences.zh: 角知者皆窮，角力者皆負，形不堪復伉，埶不足復校，乃始羈首繫頸，就我之銜紲耳。

translations.literal: When those who matched wits are exhausted and those who matched strength are defeated, their forms can no longer stand as equals, their power no longer bears comparison—they begin to halter their necks and accept my leading rein.
translations.idiomatic: When brains and brawn fail, they can neither fight nor flee—they slip halters on their necks and take my bridle.

sentences.zh: [一]夫或曾為我之尊長矣，或曾與我為等儕矣，或曾臣虜我矣，或曾執囚我矣。

translations.literal: [1] Some were once my elders, some once my peers, some once enslaved me, some once held me prisoner.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Yesterday's captor may be today's captive; yesterday's jailer, jailed.

sentences.zh: 彼之蔚蔚，皆匈詈腹詛，幸我之不成，[二]而以奮其前志，詎肯用此為終死之分邪？

translations.literal: Their flourishing rage—all curses in breast and belly—hoped I would fail so they might revive their old ambitions; how could they willingly take this as their lot unto death?
translations.idiomatic: They nursed secret oaths, praying I would stumble so old scores might be paid—would they truly accept lifelong servitude?

sentences.zh: 注[一]銜，勒也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Xian means the bit; xie means the reins.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Xian is the cheek-piece, xie the leather reins.

sentences.zh: 紲，韁也。

translations.literal: Xie means halter.
translations.idiomatic: Xie is the head-stall.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蔚與郁古字通。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Wei is an ancient graph interchangeable with yu 'luxuriant.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Wei reads as yu, 'dense' or 'sullen.'

sentences.zh: 及繼體之時，民心定矣。

translations.literal: When the successor reign arrives, the people's hearts are settled.
translations.idiomatic: Once the heir's reign opens, hearts stabilize.

sentences.zh: 普天之下，賴我而得生育，由我而得富貴，安居樂業，長養子孫，天下晏然，皆歸心於我矣。

translations.literal: Under all Heaven they depend on me for life and nurture, through me for wealth and honor; they dwell in peace, delight in their work, rear sons and grandsons in quiet—the world is calm and all hearts turn to me.
translations.idiomatic: The realm lives by my care, grows rich at my hand, raises children in peace—all hearts pivot to the throne.

sentences.zh: 豪傑之心既絕，士民之志已定，貴有常家，尊在一人。

translations.literal: The heroes' ambitions are ended, scholars and commoners have fixed their wills; nobility stays in established houses, supremacy rests in one man.
translations.idiomatic: Rivals are broken; ranks are fixed; honor belongs to old families; awe to one man.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，雖下愚之才居之，猶能使恩同天地，威侔鬼神。

translations.literal: At such a moment even a man of the meanest wit occupying the throne could make his kindness match heaven and earth, his majesty rival ghosts and spirits.
translations.idiomatic: Then even a dullard on the throne seems kind as cosmos, terrible as gods.

sentences.zh: 暴風疾霆，不足以方其怒；

translations.literal: Sudden gale and swift thunder are not enough to compare to his wrath;
translations.idiomatic: His rage outstorms thunder;

sentences.zh: 陽春時雨，不足以喻其澤；

translations.literal: warm spring and timely rain are not enough to liken to his bounty.
translations.idiomatic: his bounty outsoftens spring rain.

sentences.zh: 周﹑孔數千，無所復角其聖；

translations.literal: Though there were thousands of a Zhou or a Confucius, none could again vie with him in sagehood;
translations.idiomatic: A thousand Confuciuses could not outshine him;

sentences.zh: 賁﹑育百萬，無所復奮其勇矣。

translations.literal: though there were millions of a Ben or Yu, none could again exert their courage.
translations.idiomatic: a million Bens or Yus could not outbrave him.

sentences.zh: 彼後嗣之愚主，見天下莫敢與之違，自謂若天地之不可亡也，乃奔其私嗜，騁其邪欲，君臣宣淫，上下同惡。

translations.literal: Yet those foolish later lords, seeing that none under Heaven dares oppose them, fancy themselves as indestructible as heaven and earth; they rush after private appetites, give rein to depraved desires, ruler and minister flaunt lewdness together, high and low share the same vices.
translations.idiomatic: Yet feckless sons of fortune, sure none dare resist, think themselves eternal; they chase lust, wallow in vice, court and harem rot together.

sentences.zh: [一]目極角抵之觀，耳窮鄭□之聲。

translations.literal: [1] The eye exhausts itself on wrestling shows, the ear on the sounds of Zheng and [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: [1] Eyes gorge on acrobats, ears on the lewd strains of Zheng and Wei.

sentences.zh: [二]入則耽於婦人，出則馳於田獵。

translations.literal: [2] Within the palace they dote on women; without they gallop in the hunt.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Indoors they drown in concubines; outdoors they race hounds and chariots.

sentences.zh: 荒廢庶政，□亡人物，澶漫彌流，無所底極。

translations.literal: They neglect the myriad tasks of government, [lacuna] waste human lives, flood and spread without limit or shore.
translations.idiomatic: State business moulders, lives are squandered, excess swells without bound.

sentences.zh: [三] 信任親愛者，盡佞諂容說之人也；

translations.literal: [3] Those they trust and love are all flattering sycophants;
translations.idiomatic: [3] Every favorite is a smiling parasite;

sentences.zh: 寵貴隆豐者，盡后妃姬妾之家也。

translations.literal: those they honor and enrich are all households of empresses and concubines.
translations.idiomatic: every enriched clan is kin to the harem.

sentences.zh: 使餓狼守庖廚，饑虎牧牢豚，遂至熬天下之脂膏，斲生人之骨髓。

translations.literal: They set starving wolves to guard the kitchen, famished tigers to herd the sheep—until they boil away the fat of the realm and chop the marrow from the living.
translations.idiomatic: They hire wolves to mind the larder and tigers to herd lambs—until the empire's grease is rendered and the people's marrow carved out.

sentences.zh: 怨毒無聊，禍亂並起，中國擾攘，四夷侵叛，土崩瓦解，一朝而去。

translations.literal: Resentment and poison mount with no outlet; calamity and rebellion break out together; the Central States seethe, the four barbarians invade and revolt; it crumbles like clods and melts like tiles, gone in a morning.
translations.idiomatic: Hatred chokes with no vent; revolt and invasion follow; China boils, border tribes rise; the realm shatters overnight.

sentences.zh: 昔之為我哺乳之子孫者，今儘是我飲血之寇讎也。

translations.literal: The grandsons I once suckled are now the blood-drinking foes who feed on me.
translations.idiomatic: The babes I nursed return as vampires at my throat.

sentences.zh: 至於運徙埶去，猶不覺悟者，豈非富貴生不仁，沉溺致愚疾邪？

translations.literal: As for those who even when fortune's tide has turned still do not wake—does not wealth and honor beget inhumanity, and drowning in pleasure breed a sickness of folly?
translations.idiomatic: Those who still sleep as power ebbs—has not ease bred cruelty and indulgence bred stupidity?

sentences.zh: 存亡以之迭代，政亂從此周復，天道常然之大數也。

translations.literal: Survival and perdition thus alternate, order and chaos revolve in cycles—this is the great constant number of Heaven's way.
translations.idiomatic: Rise and fall rotate; order and chaos return—such is Heaven's long rhythm.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]《左傳》洩冶諫陳靈公曰：「公卿宣淫，人無□焉。

translations.literal: [4] Comment 1 The Zuo Commentary: Xie Ye remonstrated Duke Ling of Chen: 'When high ministers flaunt lewdness, the people have no [lacuna].'
translations.idiomatic: [4] Note 1 Xie Ye warned Chen Ling that public debauchery in high places leaves the people no model.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「宣，示也。」

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary says: 'Xuan means to display.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses xuan as 'parade' or 'display.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]武帝元封三年，作角抵戲。

translations.literal: Comment 2 In the third year of Yuanfeng, Emperor Wu staged horn-butting games.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 In 108 BCE Wu Di introduced horn-wrestling spectacles.

sentences.zh: 音義云：「兩兩相當角力，角伎蓺射御，故名角抵，蓋雜伎樂*

translations.literal: The sound gloss says: 'Pairs match strength in wrestling; horn skills include archery and driving—hence the name horn-butting; it embraces miscellaneous entertainments *
translations.idiomatic: The commentary explains paired combat and acrobatics under the label 'horn-butting,' a medley of acts *

translations.literal: (with)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss '(with)' supplies a missing particle,

sentences.zh: **[也]*，巴俞戲魚龍蔓延之屬也。

translations.literal: **[also]*, Ba and Yu dances, fish-dragon, and spreading vine routines.
translations.idiomatic: **including Ba-Yu dances, fish-dragon illusions, and the spreading-vine show.

sentences.zh: 後更名平樂觀。

translations.literal: Later it was renamed the Pingle Watchtower.
translations.idiomatic: The arena was later renamed the Pingle viewing stand.

sentences.zh: 」《禮記》曰「鄭音好濫淫志，宋音宴安溺志」也。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says, 'The music of Zheng tends to dissipation and corrupts the mind; the music of Song tends to ease and drowns the mind.'"
translations.idiomatic: The Liji warns that Zheng tunes dissolve the will and Song airs lull the spirit into sloth.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]澶漫猶縱逸也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Dan man means reckless dissipation.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Dan man denotes wanton excess.

sentences.zh: 澶音徒旦反。

translations.literal: Dan is read tudan fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph dan is read tudan.

sentences.zh: 《莊子外篇》曰「澶漫為樂」也。

translations.literal: The Outer Chapters of Zhuangzi say, 'Dan man is their pleasure.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi's outer books use dan man for heedless revelry.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]《左傳》曰「美惡周必復，天之道也。」

translations.literal: Comment 4 The Zuo Commentary says, 'Good and evil complete a cycle—that is Heaven's way.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 The Zuo: 'Beauty and ugliness turn full circle—such is the Dao of Heaven.'"

sentences.zh: 又政之為理者，取一切而已，非能斟酌賢愚之分，以開盛衰之數也。

translations.literal: Moreover, when government 'orders' things it grabs everything in one net; it cannot weigh worthy against fool to open the calculus of rise and fall.
translations.idiomatic: Policy that seizes the whole in one sweep cannot sort sage from mediocrity or chart fortune's tide.

sentences.zh: 日不如古，彌以遠甚，豈不然邪？

translations.literal: Each day it is less like antiquity, and the gap widens—is it not so?
translations.idiomatic: With every generation the model of the past recedes—can anyone deny it?

sentences.zh: 漢興以來，相與同為編戶齊民，而以財力相君長者，世無數焉。

translations.literal: Since Han arose, those who were fellow registered households and commoners yet became one another's overlords by wealth and force have been beyond counting, generation after generation.
translations.idiomatic: For two centuries men equal on the tax rolls have bullied one another with money and muscle without number.

sentences.zh: 而清絜之士，徒自苦於茨棘之閒，無所益損於風俗也。

translations.literal: Yet pure scholars only torment themselves among thorns and brambles, doing neither good nor harm to the prevailing custom.
translations.idiomatic: The fastidious recluse starves in weeds yet shifts the age not a hair.

sentences.zh: 豪人之室，連棟數百，膏田滿野，奴婢千腢，徒附萬計。

translations.literal: The magnate's mansions run to hundreds of linked bays; fat fields fill the wilderness; bond servants number in thousands; clients and retainers in tens of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: A great house strings a hundred gables, owns valleys of grain, keeps a thousand slaves and ten thousand dependents.

sentences.zh: [一]船車賈販，周於四方；

translations.literal: [1] Barges and wagons peddle goods to the four quarters;
translations.idiomatic: [1] His fleets and caravans huckster the realm;

sentences.zh: 廢居積貯，滿於都城。

translations.literal: idle hoards and stacked stores cram the capital.
translations.idiomatic: speculative granaries choke the metropolis.

sentences.zh: [二]琦賂寶貨，巨室不能容；

translations.literal: [2] Rare gifts and treasures his halls cannot hold;
translations.idiomatic: [2] Curios overflow rooms no vault can fit;

sentences.zh: [三]馬牛羊豕，山谷不能受。

translations.literal: [3] horses, cattle, sheep, and swine more than hills and dales can pasture.
translations.idiomatic: [3] His herds outgrow every pasture.

sentences.zh: 妖童美妾，填乎綺室；

translations.literal: Bewitching boys and lovely concubines pack his silk chambers;
translations.idiomatic: Catamites and concubines crowd the inner chambers;

sentences.zh: 倡謳*

translations.literal: singing girls*
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript reads 'singing girls' with an asterisk,

translations.literal: (courtesans)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss '(courtesans)' supplies the missing word,

sentences.zh: *[伎]樂，列乎深堂。

translations.literal: * entertainers and musicians line his deep halls.
translations.idiomatic: and the line continues with musicians filling the inner hall.

sentences.zh: 賓客待見而不敢去，車騎交錯而不敢進。

translations.literal: Guests await audience and dare not leave; carriages and riders crowd the gate yet dare not enter.
translations.idiomatic: Clients cool their heels in the courtyard; equipage blocks the lane yet none may pass.

sentences.zh: 三牲之肉，臭而不可食；

translations.literal: Flesh of the three sacrifices rots stinking, uneatable;
translations.idiomatic: Offered meat moulders untouched;

sentences.zh: 清醇之酎，敗而不可飲。

translations.literal: fine strong ale sours, undrinkable.
translations.idiomatic: vintage mash turns vinegary in the jars.

sentences.zh: 睇盼則人從其目之所視，喜怒則人隨其心之所慮。

translations.literal: A sidelong glance and men follow his eyes; joy or anger and men track his mood.
translations.idiomatic: One look binds retainers to his glance; whim becomes their law.

sentences.zh: 此皆公侯之廣樂，君長之厚實也。

translations.literal: All this is the lavish music of dukes and marquises, the thick substance of lords and chiefs.
translations.idiomatic: Such display belongs to princes of blood, not commoners.

sentences.zh: 苟能運智詐者，則得之焉；

translations.literal: If one can wield cunning, one seizes it;
translations.idiomatic: Cunning wins the pile;

sentences.zh: 苟能得之者，人不以為罪焉。

translations.literal: once seized, the world no longer calls it crime.
translations.idiomatic: once won, the world forgives the theft.

sentences.zh: 源發而橫流，路開而四通矣。

translations.literal: The source bursts sideways, the road opens four ways.
translations.idiomatic: Wealth floods every outlet; every path runs to his door.

sentences.zh: 求士之捨榮樂而居窮苦，[四]□放逸而赴束縛，夫誰肯為之者邪！

translations.literal: To seek gentlemen who would leave glory and ease for poverty and hardship, [four] [lacuna] abandon wandering and accept bondage—who would consent to that!
translations.idiomatic: Who would trade soft honors for thorns, [lacuna] quit leisure for fetters—who signs for such a bargain!

sentences.zh: [五]夫亂世長而化世短。

translations.literal: [five] Chaotic ages are long; ages of transformation are short.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Disorder outlasts order in the ledger of time.

sentences.zh: 亂世則小人貴寵，君子困賤。

translations.literal: In chaotic ages petty men are honored while gentlemen are trapped in want.
translations.idiomatic: When the realm tilts, knaves rise and the worthy sink.

sentences.zh: 當君子困賤之時，局高天，蹐厚地，猶恐有鎮厭之禍也。

translations.literal: When gentlemen are trapped, they crouch under high heaven and tread lightly on thick earth, still fearing the disaster of suppression.
translations.idiomatic: Even crouching under sky and earth they dread the crush of power.

sentences.zh: [六]逮至清世，則復入於矯枉過正之檢。

translations.literal: [six] Only when a clear age arrives do they again enter the reckoning of overcorrecting the straight.
translations.idiomatic: [6] In a lucid reign men swing to the opposite excess.

sentences.zh: 老者耄矣，不能及寬饒之俗；

translations.literal: The old are senescent and cannot catch up with the ease of wide tolerance.
translations.idiomatic: Gray heads cannot live to see generous rule return.

sentences.zh: 少者方壯，將復困於衰亂之時。

translations.literal: The young are in their prime yet will again be trapped in decline and chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Youth will meet another round of rot before their hair whitens.

sentences.zh: 是使奸人擅無窮之福利，而善士掛不赦之罪辜。

translations.literal: Thus villains monopolize endless blessings while good men hang unpardonable guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Rogues harvest every boon; the righteous wear unpardonable blame.

sentences.zh: 苟目能辯色，耳能辯聲，口能辯味，體能辯寒溫者，將皆以修絜為諱惡，設智巧以避之焉，況肯有安而樂之者邪？

translations.literal: If eyes can tell color, ears sound, mouth taste, body heat and cold—all will treat purity as a shameful vice and deploy wit to evade it; how much less will they willingly dwell in ease and delight in it!
translations.idiomatic: Anyone sharp enough to sense the world brands integrity a bore and schemes to dodge it—who then chooses the hard clean path for joy?

sentences.zh: 斯下世人主一切之愆也。

translations.literal: This is the blanket fault of the rulers of later ages.
translations.idiomatic: Such is the wholesale error of late sovereigns.

sentences.zh: 注[一]徒，觿也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Tu means 'multitude.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Tu means throng or mass.

sentences.zh: 附，親也。

translations.literal: Fu means 'attached' or 'kin.'
translations.idiomatic: Fu denotes hangers-on bound to a patron.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《史記》曰：「轉轂百數，廢居蓄邑。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The Records say, 'Wheels by the hundred; idling and hoarding in cities.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 The Shiji speaks of caravans numbering hundreds, speculating in urban markets.

sentences.zh: 」注云：「有所廢，有所蓄，言其乘時射利也。」

translations.literal: The commentary says, 'Sometimes idling stock, sometimes hoarding—riding the moment to shoot for profit.'"
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: buy low, sell high—timing the market.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]琦，瑋也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Qi means rare or marvelous.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Qi means precious oddity.

sentences.zh: 《抱朴子》曰「片玉可以琦，奚必俟盈尺」也。

translations.literal: The Baopozi says, 'A flake of jade may be treasured—why wait for a full foot?'"
translations.idiomatic: Ge Hong: even a splinter of jade counts as treasure.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]捨音式者反。

translations.literal: Comment 4 She is read shizhe fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 She is read shizhe.

sentences.zh: 注[五]束縛謂自潔清如拘執也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 'Bondage' here means holding oneself pure as though under arrest.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 'Bondage' describes self-imposed austerity like a prisoner's discipline.

sentences.zh: 注[六]詩小雅曰：「謂天蓋高，不敢不局；

translations.literal: The Odes, Lesser Ya, says, 'They say Heaven is high—yet I dare not straighten;
translations.idiomatic: Lesser Ya: 'Heaven is called high, yet I dare not stand upright;

sentences.zh: 謂地蓋厚，不敢不蹐。

translations.literal: they say Earth is thick—yet I dare not stride freely.
translations.idiomatic: earth is called thick, yet I dare not stride freely.'

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「局，曲也。

translations.literal: Mao Chang's commentary says, 'Ju means to curl up;
translations.idiomatic: Mao's gloss: ju means to cower;

sentences.zh: 蹐，累足也。」

translations.literal: ji means to tread with stacked feet.'"
translations.idiomatic: ji means to tread with mincing, stacked steps.'"

sentences.zh: 昔春秋之時，周氏之亂世也。

translations.literal: In former times the Spring and Autumn era was the chaos of the Zhou house.
translations.idiomatic: The Chunqiu age was already Zhou's disorder.

sentences.zh: 逮乎戰國，則又甚矣。

translations.literal: By the Warring States it was worse still.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhanguo decades deepened the rot.

sentences.zh: 秦政乘並兼之埶，放虎狼之心，[一]屠裂天下，吞食生人，暴虐不已，以招楚漢用兵之苦，甚於戰國之時也。

translations.literal: Qin Shi Huang rode the momentum of annexation, unleashed a tiger-and-wolf heart, [one] carved up the realm and devoured the living; his cruelty knew no end, until the agony of Chu and Han at arms surpassed even the Warring States.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor used conquest to loose wolfish greed, butchered the world, and outdid Warring States carnage until the Chu-Han war topped every prior horror.

sentences.zh: 漢二百年而遭王莽之亂，[二]計其殘夷滅亡之數，又復倍乎秦﹑項矣。

translations.literal: Han endured two hundred years and then met Wang Mang's rebellion; [two] counting the slaughtered and extinguished, the toll doubled Qin and Xiang Yu combined.
translations.idiomatic: Two centuries of Han ended in Mang's coup; the body count doubled Qin's tyranny and Xiang Yu's fury.

sentences.zh: 以及今日，名都空而不居，百里絕而無民者，不可勝數。

translations.literal: Down to our own day famous cities stand empty, districts of a hundred li lack inhabitants—beyond counting.
translations.idiomatic: Today ghost cities and depopulated cantons are too many to list.

sentences.zh: [三]此則又甚於亡新之時也。

translations.literal: [three] This is worse still than the fall of Wang Mang's Xin.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The waste exceeds Xin dynasty's collapse.

sentences.zh: 悲夫！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: How pitiful!

sentences.zh: 不及五百年，大難三起，[四]中閒之亂，尚不數焉。

translations.literal: Within five hundred years great calamities have thrice arisen; [four] the turmoil between them is not even reckoned.
translations.idiomatic: Under five hundred years heaven sent three great convulsions, not counting the lesser shocks.

sentences.zh: 變而彌猜，下而加酷，[五]推此以往，可及於盡矣。

translations.literal: Change grows ever more suspicious, the later ever more cruel; [five] push this trend forward and it may end in total extinction.
translations.idiomatic: Each cycle breeds sharper fear and harsher rule—carry the line to its end and nothing survives.

sentences.zh: 嗟乎！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas again!

sentences.zh: 不知來世聖人救此之道，將何用也？

translations.literal: I know not by what path later sages will rescue this.
translations.idiomatic: What remedy later sages may find is beyond my sight.

sentences.zh: 又不知天若窮此之數，欲何至邪？

translations.literal: Nor do I know, if Heaven exhausts this number, where it intends to stop.
translations.idiomatic: Nor whether Heaven, exhausting this span, aims at annihilation or renewal.

sentences.zh: 注[一]政，始皇名也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Zheng is the personal name of the First Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Zheng names the First Emperor of Qin.

sentences.zh: 注[二]漢至王莽篡位二百一十四年。

translations.literal: Comment 2 From Han's rise to Wang Mang's usurpation is two hundred fourteen years.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Han ruled 214 years before Mang seized the throne.

sentences.zh: 雲二百者，舉全數。

translations.literal: Saying 'two hundred' states a round figure.
translations.idiomatic: The text rounds to two hundred for simplicity.

sentences.zh: 注[三]孝平帝時，凡郡國一百三，縣邑一千三百一十四，道三十四，侯國二百四十一。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Under Emperor Ping there were 103 commanderies, 1,314 counties and towns, thirty-four protectorates, and 241 marquisates.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Ping's register: 103 commanderies, 1,314 county seats, 34 routes, 241 noble fiefs.

sentences.zh: 地東西九千三百二里，南北一萬三百六十八里。

translations.literal: The territory measured 9,302 li from east to west and 10,368 li from north to south.
translations.idiomatic: The realm spanned 9,302 li east-west and 10,368 li north-south.

sentences.zh: 人戶一千二百二十三萬三千六十二，口五千九百五十九萬四千九百七十八。

translations.literal: Households numbered 12,233,062; mouths 59,594,978.
translations.idiomatic: Households totaled over 12.2 million; population nearly 60 million.

sentences.zh: 此漢家極盛之時。

translations.literal: This was Han at its zenith.
translations.idiomatic: That was the Han peak.

sentences.zh: 遭王莽喪亂，暨光武中興，海內人戶，准之於前，十裁二三，邊方蕭條，略無孑遺。

translations.literal: After Wang Mang's devastation and Guangwu's revival, households within the seas compared to earlier figures amounted to only two or three tenths; the frontiers were desolate, almost without survivors.
translations.idiomatic: Mang's wars cut the census to a fifth or third of the peak; the marches were stripped bare.

sentences.zh: 孝靈遭黃巾之寇，獻帝嬰董卓之禍，英雄潟峙，白骨膏野，兵亂相尋三十餘年，三方既寧，萬不存一也。

translations.literal: Lingdi suffered the Yellow Turban raid; Xiandi as a child met Dong Zhuo's disaster; warlords faced off, white bones greased the fields; for more than thirty years arms never rested; though the three corners were pacified, not one in ten thousand survived.
translations.idiomatic: Ling fell to turbans, Xian to Dong Zhuo; warlords fed crows for thirty years; when the tripartite peace came, perhaps one soul in ten thousand remained.

sentences.zh: 注[四]秦三王二帝通在位四十九年，前漢二百三十年，後漢百九十五年，凡四百七十四年，故云不及五百年也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 The three Qin kings and two Qin emperors together held the throne forty-nine years; Former Han two hundred thirty years; Later Han one hundred ninety-five years—474 years in all—hence the text says less than five hundred years.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Qin's kings and First Emperor total 49 years; Former Han 230; Later Han 195—474—hence 'under five hundred years.'

sentences.zh: 三起謂秦末及王莽並獻帝時也。

translations.literal: The three great upheavals mean the end of Qin, Wang Mang, and Emperor Xian's era.
translations.idiomatic: The three cataclysms are Qin's fall, Mang's coup, and Han's end under Xian.

sentences.zh: 注[五]下猶後也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 Xia here means 'later.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Xia means 'afterward' or 'later.'

sentences.zh: 損益篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Profit and Loss' says:
translations.idiomatic: The treatise "On Profit and Loss" says:

sentences.zh: 作有利於時，制有便於物者，可為也。

translations.literal: What is created to profit the times, what is instituted to suit things—these may be done.
translations.idiomatic: Institutions that serve the age and tools that fit reality may stand.

sentences.zh: 事有乖於數，法有翫於時者，可改也。

translations.literal: When affairs run counter to the pattern, when laws grow stale for the time—these may be changed.
translations.idiomatic: When policy fights the trend and statutes rot, change is owed.

sentences.zh: 故行於古有其多，用於今無其功者，不可不變。

translations.literal: Therefore what flourished in antiquity yet fails when used today must be altered.
translations.idiomatic: What worked for the ancients but fails now must yield.

sentences.zh: 變而不如前，易有多所敗者，亦不可不復也。

translations.literal: If change proves worse than what went before, and change brings widespread ruin, one must not refuse to restore the old.
translations.idiomatic: If reform only deepens the wound, revert without shame.

sentences.zh: 漢之初興，分王子弟，委之以士民之命，假之以殺生之權。

translations.literal: At Han's first rise it enfeoffed imperial sons, entrusting to them the life of the people and lending them the power of life and death.
translations.idiomatic: Founding Han parcelled kingdoms to princes, handing them the people and the sword of justice.

sentences.zh: 於是驕逸自恣，志意無厭。

translations.literal: Thereupon arrogance and dissipation followed their own whim without satiety.
translations.idiomatic: The princes then rode whim and excess without limit.

sentences.zh: 魚肉百姓，以盈其欲；

translations.literal: They fed on the commoners to glut their desires;
translations.idiomatic: They devoured the peasantry to fill private want;

sentences.zh: 報蒸骨血，以快其情。

translations.literal: they even boiled the marrow of kin to pleasure their moods.
translations.idiomatic: they even tortured kin for sport.

sentences.zh: 上有篡叛不軌之奸，下有暴亂殘賊之害。

translations.literal: Above there was treason and usurpation; below tyranny and the harm of bandits.
translations.idiomatic: Rebellion festered above; slaughter spread below.

sentences.zh: 雖藉親屬之恩，蓋源流形埶使之然也。

translations.literal: Though kinship grace existed, the springhead and the situation made it so.
translations.idiomatic: Blood ties could not check the structural rot.

sentences.zh: 降爵削土，稍稍割奪，卒至於坐食奉祿而已。

translations.literal: Ranks were lowered, lands pared away bit by bit, until at last they merely sat eating stipends.
translations.idiomatic: Titles shrank, fiefs were shaved, until nobles lived on salaries alone.

sentences.zh: 然其洿穢之行，淫昏之罪，猶尚多焉。

translations.literal: Yet their foul conduct and wanton crimes still abounded.
translations.idiomatic: Even stripped of power they wallowed in vice.

sentences.zh: 故淺其根本，輕其恩義，猶尚假一日之尊，收士民之用。

translations.literal: Therefore roots were deliberately shallow and kindness lightened, yet they were still lent one day's supremacy to command the use of scholars and commoners.
translations.idiomatic: The court still lent them a day's prestige to command the people.

sentences.zh: 況專之於國，擅之於嗣，豈可鞭笞叱宛，而使唯我所為者乎？

translations.literal: How much more if they monopolized a whole state and its succession—could you whip and shout to make all bend to your will?
translations.idiomatic: Had they kept whole kingdoms, could any whip have bent them to one will?

sentences.zh: 時政雕敝，風俗移易，純樸已去，智惠已來。

translations.literal: The policy of the moment is carved and broken; customs have shifted; pure simplicity is gone and cleverness has arrived.
translations.idiomatic: Government is fractured; manners have turned; simplicity is lost to cunning.

sentences.zh: [一]出於禮制之防，放於嗜欲之域久矣，固不可授之以柄，假之以資者也。

translations.literal: [1] Long having slipped the dikes of ritual and wandered in the realm of appetite, one surely cannot hand them the handle or lend them capital.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Men long outside ritual's banks and sunk in appetite must not be given levers or loans of authority.

sentences.zh: 是故收其奕世之權，校其從橫之埶，善者早登，否者早去，[二]故下土無壅滯之士，國朝無專貴之人。

translations.literal: Therefore gather in their hereditary power, weigh their crosswise situation, promote the good early and dismiss the bad early—[two] thus the lower soil has no blocked scholars and the court no men who monopolize honor.
translations.idiomatic: Recall hereditary clout, read the chessboard, elevate the worthy early and purge the vicious—then the provinces stay fluid and no clan owns the court.

sentences.zh: 此變之善，可遂行者也。

translations.literal: This is a good change that can be carried through.
translations.idiomatic: That reform is sound and practicable.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《老子》曰「智惠出，有大偽」也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Laozi says, 'When cleverness appears, great hypocrisy arises.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Laozi ties wit to wholesale deceit.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]去音袪莒反。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Qu is read quju fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Qu is read quju.

sentences.zh: 井田之變，豪人貨殖，館舍佈於州郡，田畝連於方國。

translations.literal: With the well-field system changed, magnates speculate; inns spread through commanderies; fields link across regional states.
translations.idiomatic: Once equal-field rule vanished, merchants built hostels in every commandery and stitched manors across state borders.

sentences.zh: 身無半通青綸之命，而竊三辰龍章之服；

translations.literal: Though they hold no half-tally green-ribbon appointment, they usurp robes emblazoned with the three luminaries and the dragon.
translations.idiomatic: Without a petty clerk's warrant they wear insignia reserved for the throne.

sentences.zh: [一]不為編戶一伍之長，而有千室名邑之役。
