translations.literal: [1] Though they are not heads of a single hamlet of five households, they bear corvée burdens fit for towns of a thousand gates.
translations.idiomatic: [1] They shirk the five-house headship yet impose labor worthy of a county seat.

sentences.zh: [二]榮樂過於封君，埶力侔於守令。

translations.literal: [two] Their glory and ease exceed enfeoffed lords; their power matches prefects and magistrates.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Their splendor outshines titled lords; their muscle rivals governors.

sentences.zh: 財賂自營，犯法不坐。

translations.literal: They run their own bribes and break the law without sitting for it.
translations.idiomatic: They buy impunity and laugh at the code.

sentences.zh: 刺客死士，為之投命。

translations.literal: Assassins and devoted bravos cast away their lives at their bidding.
translations.idiomatic: Bravos and killers pledge lives to their purse.

sentences.zh: 至使弱力少智之子，被穿帷敗，寄死不斂，冤枉窮困，不敢自理。

translations.literal: So that weak sons of scant wit have their curtains torn, die abroad unburied, wronged and destitute yet dare not plead their case.
translations.idiomatic: Weaklings end stripped, dying on the road unshrouded, too crushed to sue.

sentences.zh: 雖亦由網禁□闊，蓋分田無限使之然也。

translations.literal: Though that too proceeds from loose laws and wide prohibitions, fundamentally it was unlimited land division that made it so.
translations.idiomatic: Partly the law was slack—but above all, abolishing caps on landholding caused it.

sentences.zh: 今欲張太平之紀綱，立至化之基趾，齊民財之豐寡，正風俗之奢儉，非井田實莫由也。

translations.literal: Now if we wish to extend the cords of great peace, lay the footing of perfect transformation, level the people's wealth between plenty and want, and correct luxury and thrift in custom—without restoring the well-field there is truly no way.
translations.idiomatic: To set lasting peace, true reform, fair shares, and sound customs, you cannot bypass the well-field system.

sentences.zh: 此變有所敗，而宜復者也。

translations.literal: This is a change that failed in part and ought to be restored.
translations.idiomatic: That reform stumbled; the old system deserves another hearing.

sentences.zh: 注[一]十三州志曰：「有秩、嗇夫，得假半章印。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Treatise on the Thirteen Provinces says: 'Ranked clerks and village chiefs may borrow the half-seal.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The provincial gazetteer: petty officials may use the half-size seal.

sentences.zh: 」續漢輿服志曰：「百石，青紺綸，一采，宛轉繆織，長丈二尺。

translations.literal: The Continued Han Treatise on Carriages and Robes says: 'For one hundred bushels, green-dark silk ribbon, one color, twisted weave, one zhang two chi in length.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Han yufu zhi: hundred-bushel rank wears a green twisted ribbon twelve chi long.

sentences.zh: 」《說文》：「綸，青絲綬也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: 'Lun is a green silk sash.
translations.idiomatic: Shuowen defines lun as a green silk cord.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注《禮記》曰：「綸，今有秩、嗇夫所佩也。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary on the Record of Rites says: 'Lun is what ranked clerks and village chiefs wear today.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan: lun is the sash worn by low clerks today.

sentences.zh: 」三辰，日、月、星也。

translations.literal: The three luminaries are sun, moon, and stars.
translations.idiomatic: The three celestial emblems are sun, moon, and stars.

sentences.zh: 龍章謂山龍之章。

translations.literal: Dragon insignia means the mountain-dragon pattern.
translations.idiomatic: 'Dragon chapter' denotes the mountain-dragon motif on court dress.

sentences.zh: 皆畫於衣也。

translations.literal: All are depicted on the robe.
translations.idiomatic: Those designs are woven or painted on the garments.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《周禮》小司徒職：「五人為伍。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The Zhou li, office of junior steward: 'Five men make a squad.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Zhou li junior steward: five households form a squad.

sentences.zh: 」《前書》曰：「五家為伍，伍有長。

translations.literal: The Former Han says: 'Five households make a squad; each squad has a head.
translations.idiomatic: Former Han law: five doors to a squad, each with a leader.

sentences.zh: 」《論語》孔子曰：「千室之邑，百乘之家。

translations.literal: The Analects, Confucius says: 'A town of a thousand households, a house with a hundred chariots.
translations.idiomatic: The Analects speaks of a thousand-gate town and a hundred-chariot house.

sentences.zh: 」言豪強之家，身無品秩，而強富比於公侯也。

translations.literal: It means powerful houses that hold no official rank yet rival marquises in wealth.
translations.idiomatic: That is magnates without rank whose riches match princes of blood.

sentences.zh: 肉刑之廢，輕重無品，下死則得髡鉗，下髡鉗則得鞭笞。

translations.literal: With mutilating punishments abolished, heavy and light lack proper grades; one step below death yields only shaving and shackles, one step below that yields only scourging.
translations.idiomatic: Ending corporal punishment erased proportion: just short of death you shave and collar; short of that you only flog.

sentences.zh: [一]死者不可復生，而髡者無傷於人。

translations.literal: [1] The dead cannot live again, yet shaving does no physical harm to others.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Dead men stay dead, while shaving wounds no third party.

sentences.zh: 髡笞不足以懲中罪，安得不至於死哉！

translations.literal: Shaving and flogging are not enough to punish middling crimes—how can matters not drift to execution!
translations.idiomatic: When flogging cannot check middling guilt, cases slide inexorably toward the headsman's block.

sentences.zh: [二]夫雞狗之攘竊，男女之淫奔，酒醴之賂遺，謬誤之傷害，皆非值於死者也。

translations.literal: [2] Petty theft of fowl or dogs, illicit elopements, gifts of ale, harm from honest mistake—none of these deserve death.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Chicken-thieving, elopement, jugs of wine, accidental injury—none merit capital punishment.

sentences.zh: 殺之則甚重，髡之則甚輕。

translations.literal: To kill them is excessive; to shave them is too light.
translations.idiomatic: Execution is disproportionate; shaving alone is far too mild.

sentences.zh: 不制中刑以稱其罪，則法令安得不參差，殺生安得不過謬乎？

translations.literal: If you do not institute intermediate punishments to fit the crime, how can statutes avoid inconsistency and life-and-death verdicts avoid gross error?
translations.idiomatic: Without middle penalties matched to guilt, law becomes a lottery and killing a blunder.

sentences.zh: 今患刑輕之不足以懲惡，則假臧貨以成罪，托疾病以諱殺。

translations.literal: Now the worry that light punishments fail to check evil leads clerks to pad stolen goods to fabricate guilt or plead illness to hide murder.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing leniency emboldens knaves, yamen men inflate stolen sums or forge sickbed deaths to kill by stealth.

sentences.zh: [三]科條無所准，名實不相應，恐非帝王之通法，聖人之良制也。

translations.literal: [3] Statutes lack clear standards; name and fact no longer match—surely this is not the universal law of emperors nor the good system of sages.
translations.idiomatic: [3] When codes lack anchors and charges lie, you have neither royal precedent nor sage-like justice.

sentences.zh: 或曰：過刑惡人，可也；

translations.literal: Someone says: Excessive punishment of the wicked may pass;
translations.idiomatic: Critics say: crushing villains might be tolerable;

sentences.zh: 過刑善人，豈可復哉？

translations.literal: but excessive punishment of the good—can that ever be right?
translations.idiomatic: but crushing the innocent—can that stand?

sentences.zh: 曰：若前政以來，未曾枉害善人者，則有罪不死也，[四]是為忍於殺人*

translations.literal: The answer: If since prior reigns no good man was ever wrongly harmed, then the guilty would never die—[four] that would mean sparing the murderer*
translations.idiomatic: The reply: if no innocent ever died, no criminal would either—[4] that would pity the killer*

translations.literal: (indeed)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss '(indeed)' marks the particle,

sentences.zh: *，而不忍於刑人也。

translations.literal: *yet being ruthless toward corporal punishment.
translations.idiomatic: yet shrink from mutilating the living.

sentences.zh: 今令五刑有品，輕重有數，科條有序，名實有正，非殺人逆亂鳥獸之行甚重者，皆勿殺。

translations.literal: Now let the five punishments have fixed grades, weight and lightness numbered scales, statutes ordered, name and fact aligned—except for killing, rebellion, or conduct viler than birds and beasts, none shall be put to death.
translations.idiomatic: Set clear degrees for the five punishments, ranked statutes, honest charges—spare the axe except for murder, revolt, or bestial crimes.

sentences.zh: [五]嗣周氏之秘典，續呂侯之祥刑，此又宜復之善者也。

translations.literal: [five] Inherit the Zhou house's secret canons, continue the Marquis of Lü's merciful code—this too is a restoration worth making.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Revive Zhou's penal canons and Lü's balanced code—another worthy return.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]下猶減也。

translations.literal: [six] Comment 1 Xia here means 'to reduce.'
translations.idiomatic: [6] Note 1 Xia means 'reduce' or 'step down.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]言髡笞太輕，不足畏懼，而奸人冒罪，以陷於死。

translations.literal: Comment 2 It means shaving and flogging are too light to inspire fear, so villains risk crime and fall into death.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Light flogging fails to deter, so rogues gamble and end on the scaffold.

sentences.zh: 明復古肉刑，則人不陷於死也。

translations.literal: Clarifying restoration of ancient corporal punishment would keep men from falling into execution.
translations.idiomatic: Restoring graded mutilation would pull many back from the death pit.

sentences.zh: 注[三]假增臧貨，以益其罪。

translations.literal: Comment 3 They forge increased stolen goods to magnify the charge.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Clerks inflate theft tallies to deepen guilt.

sentences.zh: 托稱疾病，令死於獄也。

translations.literal: They pretend sickness to let the man die in prison.
translations.idiomatic: They feign illness so the prisoner dies in custody.

sentences.zh: 注[四]言善人有罪，亦當殺之也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 It means that when good men are guilty, they too should be executed.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Even worthy offenders must die when law demands.

sentences.zh: 注[五]鳥獸之行謂蒸報也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 'Bird-and-beast conduct' means incestuous violation of kin.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 'Bestial conduct' denotes incestuous outrage.

sentences.zh: 注[六]《周禮》大司寇職：「掌邦之三典，以佐王刑邦國，詰四方，一曰刑新國用輕典，二曰刑平國用中典，三曰刑亂國用重典。

translations.literal: Comment 6 The Zhou li, great minister of justice: 'He grasps the state's three canons to aid the king in punishing the regions and correcting the four quarters—first, use light codes in a new state; second, middle codes in a settled state; third, heavy codes in a chaotic state.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 Zhou li great minister of justice lists light, middle, and heavy codes for new, stable, and chaotic realms.

sentences.zh: 」祥，善也。

translations.literal: Xiang means good.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang means 'good' or 'auspicious.'

sentences.zh: 《尚書》曰：「教爾祥刑。」

translations.literal: The Documents says, 'Teach you a merciful punishment.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shangshu bids rulers teach lenient justice.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：「陽一君二臣，君子之道也；

translations.literal: The Changes says: 'In yang, one lord and two ministers—this is the way of the noble man;
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi: one ruler, two ministers in a yang hexagram marks the gentleman's path;

sentences.zh: 陰二君一臣，小人之道也。

translations.literal: in yin, two lords and one minister—this is the way of the petty man.
translations.idiomatic: two rulers, one minister in yin marks the small man's path.

sentences.zh: 」[一]然則寡者，為人上者也；

translations.literal: ' [1] Thus the few are those who stand above others;
translations.idiomatic: ' [1] The few sit atop the many;

sentences.zh: 觿者，為人下者也。

translations.literal: the many are those who stand below others.
translations.idiomatic: the many serve beneath.

sentences.zh: 一伍之長，才足以長一伍者也；

translations.literal: The head of one squad has talent enough to lead one squad;
translations.idiomatic: A squad leader needs only squad-scale talent;

sentences.zh: 一國之君，才足以君一國者也；

translations.literal: the lord of one state has talent enough to rule one state;
translations.idiomatic: a king needs only state-scale talent;

sentences.zh: 天下之王，才足以王天下者也。

translations.literal: the king of all under Heaven has talent enough to reign over all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: the Son of Heaven needs empire-spanning gifts alone.

sentences.zh: 愚役於智，猶枝之附干，此理天下之常法也。

translations.literal: The foolish serve the wise as branches cling to the trunk—this is the constant law of ordering the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Stupid obeys clever as twigs cling to bough—Heaven's rule for governing.

sentences.zh: 制國以分人，立政以分事，人遠則難綏，事總則難了。

translations.literal: Order the state by dividing responsibility among men; establish government by dividing tasks among offices—when men are remote they are hard to pacify, when tasks are piled in one place they are hard to finish.
translations.idiomatic: Rule splits labor among men and offices—distance breeds neglect, central overload chokes action.

sentences.zh: 今遠州之縣，或相去數百千里，雖多山陵洿澤，猶有可居人種穀者焉。

translations.literal: Now in distant counties of far commanderies, places may lie several hundred or even a thousand li apart; though mountains, marshes, and wastes intervene, there are still lands where people can dwell and grain be sown.
translations.idiomatic: Frontier counties sprawl hundreds of li apart, yet even amid waste there is arable ground.

sentences.zh: 當更制其境界，使遠者不過二百里。

translations.literal: The boundaries ought to be redrawn so that no jurisdiction exceeds two hundred li at the farthest.
translations.idiomatic: Redraw borders so no magistrate sits farther than two hundred li from his farthest hamlet.

sentences.zh: 明版籍以相數閱，審什伍以相連持，[二]限夫田以斷並兼，定五刑以救死亡，[三]益君長以興政理，急農桑以豐委積，去末作以一本業，敦教學以移情性，表德行以厲風俗，核才蓺以□官宜，簡精悍以習師田，[四]修武器以存守戰，嚴禁令以防僭差，信實罰以驗懲勸，糾游戲以杜奸邪，察苛刻以絕煩暴。

translations.literal: Clarify household registers for mutual inspection, tighten squads of five and ten for mutual guarantee, [two] cap peasant holdings to stop annexation, fix the five punishments to spare wrongful death, [three] add magistrates to invigorate administration, press farming and sericulture to fill granaries, cut secondary trades to root the people in primary work, promote schools to shift temperament, display virtue to steel custom, test talent and craft to match office, pick stalwart fighters to drill for camp and field, [four] maintain arms for defense and war, tighten bans against usurpation, keep rewards and penalties honest to prove encouragement and warning, curb idle games to block villainy, watch harsh clerks to end bullying.
translations.idiomatic: Brighten registers and mutual surveillance, bind squads of five and ten, limit acreage against land-grabs, grade punishments to spare the wrongly killed, add honest magistrates, push tillage and silk to fill bins, choke off huckstering to fix the root, thicken schooling to reshape hearts, honor virtue to stiffen manners, audit skill to fit office, drill picked troops, keep weapons ready, seal sumptuary law, make rewards and fines credible, ban gambling dens, purge cruel petty clerks.

sentences.zh: 審此十六者以為政務，操之有常，課之有限，安寧勿懈墯，有事不迫遽，聖人復起，不能易也。

translations.literal: Take these sixteen measures as the substance of government; hold them steady, assess them on a schedule, keep quiet times from slackening, urgent times from panic—even if a sage returned he could not improve on them.
translations.idiomatic: Make these sixteen policies the backbone of rule, hold the course, grade performance on a calendar—neither slack in peace nor frantic in crisis—and no sage could better the scheme.

sentences.zh: 注[一]系詞之文也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 This is language from the 'Appended Remarks.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 The lines come from the Xici.

sentences.zh: 陽卦一陽而二陰，陰卦一陰而二陽。

translations.literal: In a yang hexagram there is one yang line and two yin; in a yin hexagram one yin and two yang.
translations.idiomatic: Yang trigrams show one firm line among two yielding; yin the reverse.

sentences.zh: 陽為君，陰為臣。

translations.literal: Yang stands for the ruler, yin for the minister.
translations.idiomatic: Yang images the throne, yin the subject.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《周禮》曰：「凡在版者。

translations.literal: Comment 2 The Zhou li says, 'All who are on the register.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Zhou li: everyone on the board.

sentences.zh: 」注云：「版，名籍也，以版為之也。」

translations.literal: The commentary says, 'The board is the name-list, made of wooden tablets.'"
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: wooden tablets list households.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]《司馬法》曰：「步百為畝，畝百為夫，夫三為屋，屋三為井。

translations.literal: Comment 3 The Sima fa says, 'A hundred paces square make a mu; a hundred mu make a husbandman; three husbandmen make a house; three houses make a well.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Sima fa lays out the well-field chain from pace to well.

sentences.zh: 」並兼謂豪富之家以財埶並取貧人之田而兼有之。

translations.literal: Annexation means powerful houses using wealth and influence to swallow poor men's fields and hold them all.
translations.idiomatic: Land-grabbers use money and muscle to swallow smallholders whole.

sentences.zh: 注四《周禮》曰：「凡師田斬牲以左右徇陳。

translations.literal: Comment four The Zhou li says, 'Whenever drilling camp or field, behead the victim beast to pace the lines left and right.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Zhou li: military drill begins with a sacrificial beast run down the ranks.

sentences.zh: 」注云：「示犯誓必殺也。」

translations.literal: The commentary says, 'It shows that breaking the oath means death.'"
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: oath-breakers die like the victim.'"

sentences.zh: 向者，天下戶過千萬，除其老弱，但戶一丁壯，則千萬人也。

translations.literal: Formerly, households under Heaven passed ten million; deducting old and weak, if each household supplied one able-bodied man, that would be ten million men.
translations.idiomatic: At Han's height the census passed ten million doors—one able man each yields ten million soldiers on paper.

sentences.zh: 遺漏既多，又蠻夷戎狄居漢地者尚不在焉。

translations.literal: Omissions were already many, and this does not include barbarians settled within Han territory.
translations.idiomatic: Undercounting was huge, and border tribes inside the wall are not even tallied.

sentences.zh: 丁壯十人之中，必有堪為其什伍之長，推什長已上，則百萬人也。

translations.literal: Among ten able men one can surely find a head for squad or platoon; promoting from platoon head upward yields a million men.
translations.idiomatic: One leader in ten can captain squads—stack promotions and you have a million officers.

sentences.zh: 又十取之，則佐史之才已上十萬人也。

translations.literal: Taking one in ten again yields more than a hundred thousand fit for clerkships.
translations.idiomatic: Another decimation yields a hundred thousand clerk-grade talents.

sentences.zh: 又十取之，則可使在政理之位者萬人也。

translations.literal: Taking one in ten again yields ten thousand fit for seats of governance.
translations.idiomatic: One more pass yields ten thousand capable of high office.

sentences.zh: 以筋力用者謂之人，人求丁壯；

translations.literal: Those employed for muscle are called commoners—the commoner supply wants young brawn;
translations.idiomatic: Laborers are 'people'—you draft youth;

sentences.zh: 以才智用者謂之士，士貴耆老。

translations.literal: those employed for wit are called gentlemen—the gentleman supply honors age.
translations.idiomatic: thinkers are 'gentlemen'—you prize gray hair.

sentences.zh: 充此制以用天下之人，猶將有儲，何嫌乎不足也？

translations.literal: Fill offices by this measure and the realm still has surplus talent—why fear shortage?
translations.idiomatic: Staff the empire this way and talent still pools unused—what shortage could you fear?

sentences.zh: 故物有不求，未有無物之歲也；

translations.literal: Therefore goods may go un-sought, but there is no year without goods;
translations.idiomatic: Products may lie idle, yet no age lacks goods;

sentences.zh: 士有不用，未有少士之世也。

translations.literal: gentlemen may go unused, but there is no age short of gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: scholars may sit idle, yet no era truly lacks scholars.

sentences.zh: 夫如此，然後可以用天性，究人理，興頓廢，屬斷絕，[一]網羅遺漏，拱柙天人矣。

translations.literal: Only then can you employ Heaven's nature, exhaust human principle, raise what has fallen, continue what was cut off—[one] net the overlooked and hold Heaven and man in one grip.
translations.idiomatic: Then you align human nature with cosmic pattern, revive the fallen, mend broken lines—[1] catch every stray talent and join Heaven with man.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]屬猶續也。

translations.literal: [two] Comment 1 Shu means to continue.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Note 1 Shu means 'continue' or 'reconnect.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]拱，執也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Gong means to grasp.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Gong means to hold fast.

sentences.zh: 柙，檻也。

translations.literal: Xia means cage.
translations.idiomatic: Xia means a wooden cage or pen.

sentences.zh: 柙，音下甲反。

translations.literal: Xia is read xiajia fan.
translations.idiomatic: Xia is read xiajia.

sentences.zh: 或曰：善為政者，欲除煩去苛，並官省職，為之以無為，事之以無事，何子言之云云也？

translations.literal: Someone says: Good government means cutting tangle and harshness, merging offices and trimming posts, acting through non-action, governing through non-striving—why all your elaborate talk?
translations.idiomatic: Critics quote Laozi: rule by doing nothing—why your sixteen-point plan?

sentences.zh: [一]曰：若是，三代不足摹，聖人未可師也。

translations.literal: [one] The answer: If so, then the Three Dynasties are not worth imitating and sages are not worth taking as models.
translations.idiomatic: [1] If that were enough, the Three Ages need not be copied nor sages heeded.

sentences.zh: [二]君子用法制而至於化，小人用法制而至於亂。

translations.literal: [two] When gentlemen wield law and institutions, transformation follows; when petty men wield them, chaos follows.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Good men with codes bring order; knaves with the same codes bring riot.

sentences.zh: 均是一法制也，或以之化，或以之亂，行之不同也。

translations.literal: It is the same code—one use brings order, another chaos—because the practice differs.
translations.idiomatic: Identical statutes succeed or fail with the hand that wields them.

sentences.zh: 苟使豺狼牧羊豚，盜跖主徵稅，國家昏亂，吏人放肆，則惡復論損益之閒哉！

translations.literal: If you set wolves to herd sheep or appoint Robber Zhi to collect taxes, the state goes dark, clerks run wild—then what is the point of debating profit and loss?
translations.idiomatic: Wolves guarding lambs or bandits collecting tax ends debate about 'streamlining.'

sentences.zh: [三]夫人待君子然後化理，國待蓄積乃無憂患。

translations.literal: [three] The people await gentlemen before they are transformed; the state awaits stored grain before it is free of peril.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Folk need leaders to change; treasuries need grain before crisis.

sentences.zh: 君子非自農桑以求衣食者也，蓄積非橫賦斂以取優饒者也。

translations.literal: Gentlemen do not beg clothes and food from farming and sericulture; stored grain is not seized by ruthless extra levies for surplus ease.
translations.idiomatic: Officials are not peasants; granaries are not built on predatory surtaxes.

sentences.zh: 奉祿誠厚，則割剝貿易之罪乃可絕也；

translations.literal: When salaries are truly ample, the crimes of slicing and trading on the people can be ended;
translations.idiomatic: Pay magistrates enough and extortion ends;

sentences.zh: 蓄積誠多，則兵寇水旱之□不足苦也。

translations.literal: when stores are truly full, the harm of war, banditry, flood, and drought [lacuna] will not distress.
translations.idiomatic: fill bins and flood, drought, and raid lose their sting.

sentences.zh: 故由其道而得之，民不以為奢；

translations.literal: Therefore when goods are gained by the right Way, the people do not call it extravagance;
translations.idiomatic: Wealth taken by honest rule does not read as excess;

sentences.zh: 由其道而取之，民不以為勞。

translations.literal: when goods are taken by the right Way, the people do not call it toil.
translations.idiomatic: taxes gathered fairly do not feel like crushing toil.

sentences.zh: 天□流行，開倉庫以稟貸，不亦仁乎？

translations.literal: When Heaven's [lacuna] flows abroad, opening granaries to lend grain—is that not benevolence?
translations.idiomatic: When Heaven sends dearth, open the bins—is that not kindness?

sentences.zh: 衣食有餘，損靡麗以散施，不亦義乎？

translations.literal: When clothes and food are abundant, cutting splendor to give alms—is that not righteousness?
translations.idiomatic: When surplus fills lofts, trim finery to feed the needy—is that not justice?

sentences.zh: 彼君子居位為士民之長，固宜重肉累帛，朱輪四馬。

translations.literal: Those gentlemen who sit in office as shepherds of the people should rightly eat layered meat and pile silks, ride vermillion wheels with four horses.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrates who lead the million should dine well and ride high—that is fitting.

sentences.zh: 今反謂薄屋者為高，藿食者為清，既失天地之性，又開虛偽之名，使小智居大位，庶績不咸熙，未必不由此也。

translations.literal: Now to call a mean roof noble and a diet of beans pure loses Heaven's true measure and opens the door to hollow fame, letting petty wit hold great office so that no task shines—perhaps that is the root.
translations.idiomatic: To praise hovels and coarse fare as 'integrity' perverts nature, invites poseurs, and fills high seats with small minds—source of every failure.

sentences.zh: 得拘絜而失才能，非立功之實也。

translations.literal: To gain punctilious austerity while losing real capacity is not the substance of achievement.
translations.idiomatic: Clutching cheap virtue while losing talent wins no real merit.

sentences.zh: [四]以廉舉而以貪去，非士君子之志也。

translations.literal: [four] To promote for probity yet dismiss for greed is not the intent of gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Elevating the honest yet firing the grasping twists the gentleman's purpose.

sentences.zh: [五]夫選用必取善士。

translations.literal: [five] Selection must always take good men.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Appointments must seek the worthy.

sentences.zh: 善士富者少而貧者多，祿不足以供養，安能不少營私門乎？

translations.literal: Among good men the rich are few and the poor many; if stipends cannot sustain them, how can they avoid petty squeeze on the side?
translations.idiomatic: Worthy men are mostly poor—starve their pay and they moonlight.

sentences.zh: 從而罪之，是設機置稨以待天下之君子也。

translations.literal: Then to punish them is to set snares and springs for the realm's gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Punishing that squeeze lays traps for every honest clerk.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]《老子》云「為無為，事無事」也。

translations.literal: [six] Comment 1 The Laozi says, 'Act through non-action, govern through non-striving.'"
translations.idiomatic: [6] Note 1 Laozi counsels wu wei.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]摹，法也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Mo means model.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Mo means pattern or model.

sentences.zh: 三代皆用肉刑及井田之法，今不用，是不摹之也。

translations.literal: The Three Dynasties used corporal punishment and the well-field; not to use them now is not to take them as models.
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity used mutilation and well-fields; discarding both is to reject their pattern.

sentences.zh: 注[三]惡音烏。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Wu is read wu.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Wu here is read wu (the negative).

sentences.zh: 注[四]拘絜謂自拘束而絜其身者，即隱逸之人也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 Ju jie means those who restrain and purify themselves—that is, men in reclusion.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 'Ju jie' describes recluses who bind their conduct and keep themselves pure.

sentences.zh: 注[五]去音欺呂反。

translations.literal: Comment 5 Qu is read qilü fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Qu is read qilü.

sentences.zh: 注[六]稨，穿地陷獸也。

translations.literal: Comment 6 Bian means digging a pit in the ground to trap animals.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 A bian is a pitfall trap for game.

sentences.zh: 機，弩牙也。

translations.literal: Ji means the crossbow's sear or trigger.
translations.idiomatic: Ji names the crossbow's release mechanism.

sentences.zh: 盜賊凶荒，九州代作，饑饉暴至，軍旅捽髮，橫稅弱人，割奪吏祿，所恃者寡，所取者猥，[一]萬里懸乏，首尾不救，徭役並起，農桑失業，兆民呼嗟於昊天，貧窮轉死於溝壑矣。

translations.literal: When banditry and famine strike, the nine regions take turns in disaster; sudden dearth arrives, armies are pressed into the field, harsh taxes crush the weak, salaries of clerks are sliced away—what they rely on is scant, what they seize is excessive; [one] need stretches ten thousand li with head and tail unable to aid each other; corvée duties pile together, farming and sericulture lose their season, the million people cry out to August Heaven, and the destitute roll dying into ditches and gullies.
translations.idiomatic: Raiders, drought, and riot rotate through the provinces; famine and draft follow; predatory tax flays the poor and cuts official pay—revenue shrinks while exactions swell; [1] relief cannot reach from end to end of the realm; corvée ruins the crop cycle; the masses wail to Heaven and the hungry die in the mud.

sentences.zh: 今通肥饒之率，計稼穡之入，令畝收三斛，斛取一斗，未為甚多。

translations.literal: Now if we reckon by rich-soil yields and count the income from harvests, letting each mu yield three hu and taking one dou per hu, that is not very much.
translations.idiomatic: Assume fertile land yields three hu per mu and take one tenth of each hu—still a modest levy.

sentences.zh: 一歲之閒，則有數年之儲，雖興非法之役，恣奢侈之欲，廣愛幸之賜，猶未能盡也。

translations.literal: Within a single year there would then be several years' reserve; even if unlawful projects were launched, lavish desires indulged, and favorites showered with gifts, the store could not be exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: One good harvest would bank several years of grain; waste and favoritism could not empty the bins.

sentences.zh: 不循古法，規為輕稅，及至一方有警，一面被□，未逮三年，校計騫短，坐視戰士之蔬食，立望餓殍之滿道，如之何為君行此政也？

translations.literal: If instead you spurn ancient norms and aim at light taxes, then when one quarter sounds the alarm and one border is struck, before three years are out accounts fall short; you sit watching soldiers eat vegetables and stand hoping corpses of starvation fill the roads—what sort of ruler pursues such a policy?
translations.idiomatic: Light taxes without a cushion mean one alarm empties the treasury; you watch troops starve and corpses line the roads—what governance is that?

sentences.zh: [二]二十稅一，名之曰貊，況三十稅一乎？

translations.literal: [two] A tithe of twenty parts Mencius called the Mo barbarians' rate—how much more a tithe of thirty!
translations.idiomatic: [2] Mencius mocked a twenty-to-one tax as fit only for the Mo; a thirty-to-one rate is emptier still.

sentences.zh: [三]夫薄吏祿以豐軍用，緣於秦征諸侯，續以四夷，漢承其業，遂不改更，危國亂家，此之由也。

translations.literal: [three] To shave clerks' pay to fatten the army began with Qin's campaigns against the feudatories and continued against the four barbarians; Han inherited the practice and never reformed it—this is why states tottered and houses fell into chaos.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Starving the bureaucracy to fund armies began with Qin and continued under Han—root of ruined dynasties.

sentences.zh: 今田無常主，民無常居，吏食日稟，[四]*

translations.literal: Today fields have no fixed owner, people no fixed dwelling; clerks eat from daily doles, [four] *
translations.idiomatic: Land and tenants shift daily; officials live hand-to-mouth; [4] *

translations.literal: (salary)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss marks 'salary,'

sentences.zh: *班*[祿]*未定。

translations.literal: *rank and * salary * are unsettled.
translations.idiomatic: while rank and pay stay unsettled.

sentences.zh: 可為法制，畫一定科，租稅十一，更賦如舊。

translations.literal: One may frame law and set a fixed code: rent at one-tenth, the poll tax as of old.
translations.idiomatic: Fix the code: one-tenth field rent, poll tax unchanged from precedent.

sentences.zh: [五]今者土廣民稀，中地未墾；

translations.literal: [five] Today the soil is wide and the people sparse; middle-grade land is still unopened;
translations.idiomatic: [5] The empire has land to spare and people too few; middling soil lies fallow;

sentences.zh: [六]雖然，猶當限以大家，勿令過制。

translations.literal: [six] even so, holdings should be capped for great houses and not allowed to exceed the norm.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Still cap great estates so none outgrows the rule.

sentences.zh: 其地有草者，盡曰官田，力堪農事，乃聽受之。

translations.literal: Where the ground still bears scrub, call it all official land; only when strength suffices for farming may it be granted for cultivation.
translations.idiomatic: Mark brushland as state domain; lease it only to those who can farm it.

sentences.zh: 若聽其自取，後必為奸也。

translations.literal: If you let men seize it at will, later they will surely abuse it.
translations.idiomatic: Free grabbing invites fraud.

sentences.zh: 注[一]猥猶多也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 Wei means 'much' or 'numerous.'
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Wei means excessive or many.

sentences.zh: 注[二]《孟子》曰：「塗有餓莩而不知發。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Mencius says, 'Corpses of starvation lie on the road yet you will not open the granaries.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Mencius: 'Starved bodies litter the road while granaries stay shut.'

sentences.zh: 」趙岐注云：「餓死者曰莩」。

translations.literal: Zhao Qi's commentary says, 'Those who die of hunger are called piao.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi: the starved are called piao.'"

sentences.zh: 莩與殍通，音皮表反。

translations.literal: Piao is interchangeable with biao, read piaobiao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Piao reads like biao (corpse by the wayside).

sentences.zh: 注[三]《孟子》載白圭曰：「吾欲二十而取一何如？

translations.literal: Comment 3 The Mencius records Bai Gui saying, 'I wish to take one in twenty—how would that be?'
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Bai Gui asked Mencius about a one-twentieth tax.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰：「子之道貊*[道]*也。

translations.literal: Mencius said, 'Your way is the way of the Mo.
translations.idiomatic: Mencius replied, 'That is a northern barbarian's rate.

sentences.zh: 」趙岐注云：「貊，夷貊之人在荒者也。

translations.literal: Zhao Qi's commentary says, 'Mo means the Mo tribesmen who dwell in waste lands.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi: Mo are cold-country tribes without grain.

sentences.zh: 貊在北方，其氣寒，不生五穀，無中國之禮，故可二十取一而足也。

translations.literal: Mo lies in the north; its climate is cold and the five grains do not grow; they lack the rites of the Central States, so one in twenty can suffice for them.
translations.idiomatic: Their northland cannot support Chinese farming, so a light tithe suffices there.

sentences.zh: 」此言欲輕稅也。

translations.literal: This passage means he wished to lighten the tax.
translations.idiomatic: The point is he wanted lower taxes.

sentences.zh: 注[四]稟，給也。

translations.literal: Comment 4 Bin means to supply.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Bin means ration or issue.

sentences.zh: 注[五]更賦，已見

translations.literal: Comment 5 On the poll tax, see already
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 The poll tax is explained elsewhere—see

sentences.zh: 〈光武紀〉

translations.literal: the 'Annals of Guangwu'
translations.idiomatic: the Guangwu annals

sentences.zh: 也。

translations.literal: section.
translations.idiomatic: for details.

sentences.zh: 注[六]上田已耕，唯中地已下未也。

translations.literal: Comment 6 Upper fields are already plowed; only middle and lower grades remain unopened.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 Best land is under plow; middling and poor soils await.

sentences.zh: 法誡篇曰：

translations.literal: The chapter 'On Laws and Warnings' says:
translations.idiomatic: The essay "On Laws and Warnings" says:

sentences.zh: 周禮六典，頤宰貳王而理天下。

translations.literal: The six canons of the Zhou li—the grand steward assists the king as his deputy to order all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou li's six statutes have the grand steward as the king's deputy to govern the realm.

sentences.zh: [一]春秋之時，諸侯明德者，皆一卿為政。

translations.literal: [1] In Spring and Autumn times, feudal lords who had clear virtue all used a single minister to conduct government.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Virtuous marquises ruled through one chief minister.

sentences.zh: 爰及戰國，亦皆然也。

translations.literal: Down to the Warring States it was likewise.
translations.idiomatic: Warring kingdoms did the same.

sentences.zh: 秦兼天下，則置丞相，而貳之以御史大夫。

translations.literal: When Qin swallowed the realm it set up a chancellor and paired him with a grandee secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Qin paired chancellor with censor-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 自高帝逮於孝成，因而不改，多終其身。

translations.literal: From Emperor Gao through Emperor Cheng the pattern was unchanged; many ministers held office for life.
translations.idiomatic: From Gaodi to Chengdi the structure held; ministers often died in office.

sentences.zh: 漢之隆盛，是惟在焉。

translations.literal: Han at its height rested on this arrangement.
translations.idiomatic: Han zenith rode that balance.

sentences.zh: 夫任一人則政專，任數人則相倚。

translations.literal: Entrust one man and policy is unified; entrust several and they lean on one another.
translations.idiomatic: One chief focuses power; many chiefs lean on each other.

sentences.zh: 政專則和諧，相倚則違戾。

translations.literal: When policy is unified there is harmony; when they lean on one another there is friction.
translations.idiomatic: Unity breeds harmony; mutual obstruction breeds strife.

sentences.zh: 和諧則太平之所興也，違戾則荒亂之所起也。

translations.literal: Harmony is what raises great peace; friction is what breeds wasteland chaos.
translations.idiomatic: Harmony builds peace; strife spawns ruin.

sentences.zh: 光武皇帝慍數世之失權，忿強臣之竊命，[二]矯枉過直，政不任下，雖置三公，事歸台閣。

translations.literal: Emperor Guangwu resented generations of lost authority and powerful ministers' theft of the mandate; [two] overcorrecting the straight, he refused to delegate power downward; though he retained the three dukes, business went to the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Guangwu hated regents and strongmen; [2] he over-tightened reins—three titular dukes while the secretariat held real power.

sentences.zh: [三]自此以來，三公之職，備員而已，然政有不理，猶加譴責。

translations.literal: [three] Since then the three dukes have been mere placeholders, yet when government went awry they were still blamed and punished.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The three excellences became sinecures, yet still took the blame.

sentences.zh: 而權移外戚之家，寵被近習之豎，親其黨類，用其私人，內充京師，外布列郡，顛倒賢愚，貿易選舉，疲駑守境，貪殘牧民，撓擾百姓，忿怒四夷，[四]招致乖叛，亂離斯瘼。

translations.literal: Meanwhile power shifted to affinal houses and favor fell to palace attendants; they favored their cliques and employed private men, filling the capital within and spreading through commanderies without, inverting worthy and fool, trading offices for bribes, setting broken nags to guard the borders and greedy brutes to herd the people, harassing the commoners and enraging the four barbarians—[four] inviting rebellion and estrangement, so turmoil and separation became a wasting disease.
translations.idiomatic: Power slid to in-laws and eunuchs; cliques packed the capital and provinces, sold posts, set cowards on the frontier and thieves to govern, tormented the people and provoked tribes—[4] inviting revolt and collapse.

sentences.zh: [五]怨氣並作，陰陽失和，三光虧缺，怪異數至，蟲螟食稼，水旱為□，此皆戚宦之臣所致然也。

translations.literal: [five] Resentful vapors rose together, yin and yang lost harmony, the three luminaries were eclipsed, prodigies multiplied, insects devoured the grain, flood and drought alternated as scourge—all this was brought about by affinal and palace ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Ill omens—eclipses, locusts, flood and drought—stemmed from consort clans and eunuchs.

sentences.zh: 反以策讓三公，至於死免，乃足為叫呼蒼天，號咷泣血者也。

translations.literal: Yet the court shifted blame onto the three dukes with dismissals and death sentences—enough to make one cry to Heaven and weep blood.
translations.idiomatic: The throne scapegoated the three excellences—cause for tears of blood.

sentences.zh: 又中世之選三公也，務於清□謹慎，循常習故者。

translations.literal: Moreover, in mid-era the choice of three dukes sought men who were pure [lacuna], cautious, and stuck to routine.
translations.idiomatic: Mid-Han picked dukes for punctilio and timidity.

sentences.zh: 是婦女之檢柙，鄉曲之常人耳，惡足以居斯位邪？

translations.literal: These are the shackles fit for women, common fellows of the lane—how can they occupy such posts?
translations.idiomatic: Such men are village pedants, not architects of state.

sentences.zh: [六]埶既如彼，選又如此，而慾望三公勳立於國家，績加於生民，不亦遠乎？

translations.literal: [six] Power being thus, selection being thus, yet hoping the three dukes will build merit for the state and blessing for the people—is that not far off the mark?
translations.idiomatic: [6] With power and recruitment so broken, expecting the three excellences to save the realm is fantasy.

sentences.zh: 昔文帝之於鄧通，可謂至愛，而猶展申徒嘉之志。

translations.literal: In the past Emperor Wen's favor for Deng Tong was utmost, yet he still extended Shen Tu Jia's resolve.
translations.idiomatic: Even Wen's doting on Deng Tong yielded to Shen Tu Jia's steel.

sentences.zh: [七]夫見任如此，則何患於左右小臣哉？

translations.literal: [seven] When trust runs so, what worry is there of petty men at the ruler's side?
translations.idiomatic: [7] With such oversight, petty favorites cannot ruin the court.

sentences.zh: 至如近世，外戚臣豎請托不行，意氣不滿，立能陷人於不測之禍，惡可得彈正者哉！

translations.literal: In recent times, when affines and eunuchs find a request denied or their pride wounded, they can at once trap a man in unforeseeable calamity—how may such men be impeached and corrected?
translations.idiomatic: Today a snubbed consort or eunuch invents a capital crime—who dares correct them?

sentences.zh: 曩者任之重而責之輕，今者任之輕而責之重。

translations.literal: Formerly responsibility was heavy and blame light; now responsibility is light and blame heavy.
translations.idiomatic: Old Han loaded office and spared blame; now blame crushes figureheads.

sentences.zh: 昔賈誼感絳侯之困辱，因陳大臣廉恥之分，開引自裁之端。

translations.literal: Once Jia Yi, moved by the Marquis of Jiang's humiliation, set forth the boundary between a great minister's integrity and shame and opened the way to self-execution.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Yi, pitying Zhou Bo's jailhouse shame, urged ministers to fall on their swords.

sentences.zh: [八]自此以來，遂以成俗。

translations.literal: [eight] From that time it became custom.
translations.idiomatic: [8] Suicide for disgraced ministers became habit.

sentences.zh: 繼世之主，生而見之，習其所常，曾莫之悟。

translations.literal: Successive rulers have seen it from birth, grown used to the norm, and never awakened.
translations.idiomatic: Sons of Heaven inherit the custom and never question it.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼，可悲夫！

translations.literal: Alas, how pitiful!
translations.idiomatic: Alas—how lamentable!

sentences.zh: 左手據天下之圖，右手刎其喉，愚者猶知難之，況明哲君子哉！

translations.literal: The left hand grips the map of the empire while the right hand cuts the throat—even a fool knows that is hard; how much more a wise and enlightened gentleman!
translations.idiomatic: To clutch the realm and slit your own throat—fools shrink from it, let alone sages!

sentences.zh: [九]光武奪三公之重，至今而加甚，不假後黨以權，數世而不行，蓋親疏之埶異也。

translations.literal: [nine] Guangwu stripped the three dukes of weight, and today the stripping is worse; he did not lend power to the empress's kin, yet for generations it has not been practiced—because the configuration of kinship and distance differs.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Guangwu weakened the excellences; later courts weakened them further; he refused consort power, yet later ages ignored the lesson—kinship politics changed the board.

sentences.zh: [一０]母后之黨，左右之人，有此至親之埶，故其貴任萬世。

translations.literal: [ten] The cliques of empress dowagers and men at the ruler's elbow have such intimate leverage that their honor and burden last ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: [10] Consort kin and bedchamber favorites ride intimacy to perpetual power.

sentences.zh: 常然之敗，無世而無之，莫之斯鑒，亦可痛矣。

translations.literal: The failure that always follows has afflicted every age, yet none take it as a mirror—how painful!
translations.idiomatic: The crash always comes, yet no reign learns—agonizing to watch.

sentences.zh: 未若置丞相自總之。

translations.literal: Better to set a chancellor and let him hold the whole.
translations.idiomatic: Better restore a chancellor with full portfolio.

sentences.zh: 若委三公，則宜分任責成。

translations.literal: If you insist on the three dukes, then divide tasks and demand named results.
translations.idiomatic: If you keep three heads, split portfolios and audit outcomes.

sentences.zh: 夫使為政者，不當與之婚姻；

translations.literal: Let those who govern not be joined to the ruler by marriage;
translations.idiomatic: Office-holders should not marry into the throne;

sentences.zh: 婚姻者，不當使之為政也。

translations.literal: let those joined by marriage not be set to govern.
translations.idiomatic: royal in-laws should not hold the levers.

sentences.zh: 如此，在位病人，[一一]舉用失賢，百姓不安，爭訟不息，天地多變，人物多妖，然後可以分此罪矣。

translations.literal: Only then, when the throne sickens the people, [eleven] promotion misses the worthy, the hundred surnames are restless and lawsuits unceasing, Heaven and earth multiply portents and prodigies swarm among men, may you parcel out the guilt accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Only when the people sicken, talent is missed, suits choke the courts, omens multiply, [11] may you assign blame where it belongs.

sentences.zh: 注[一]《爾雅》曰：「頤，大也。

translations.literal: Comment 1 The Erya says, 'Yi means great.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1 Erya: yi means great.

sentences.zh: 」貳謂副貳也。

translations.literal: Er means 'second' or deputy.
translations.idiomatic: Er is deputy or assistant.

sentences.zh: 《周禮》天官頤宰「掌建邦之六典，以佐王理邦國。

translations.literal: The Zhou li, office of Heaven's grand steward, says, 'He establishes the six canons of the state to aid the king in governing the regional states.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou li grand steward of heaven: six canons to aid the king.

sentences.zh: 一曰理典，以理官府；

translations.literal: First, the canon of governance to order the offices;
translations.idiomatic: First canon orders government offices;

sentences.zh: 二曰教典，以擾萬姓；

translations.literal: second, the canon of education to harmonize the hundred surnames;
translations.idiomatic: second harmonizes the people through teaching;

sentences.zh: 三曰禮典，以諧萬姓；

translations.literal: third, the canon of rites to tune the hundred surnames;
translations.idiomatic: third aligns them with rites;

sentences.zh: 四曰政典，以均萬姓；

translations.literal: fourth, the canon of administration to equalize the hundred surnames;
translations.idiomatic: fourth balances them through policy;

sentences.zh: 五曰刑典，以愨萬姓；

translations.literal: fifth, the canon of punishments to awe the hundred surnames;
translations.idiomatic: fifth awes them with penal law;

sentences.zh: 六曰事典，以生萬姓」也。

translations.literal: sixth, the canon of works to give livelihood to the hundred surnames.'"
translations.idiomatic: sixth secures their livelihood through public works.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]慍猶恨也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Yun means to resent.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 Yun means bitter resentment.

sentences.zh: 數代謂元、成、哀、平。

translations.literal: Several generations means Yuandi, Chengdi, Aidi, and Pingdi.
translations.idiomatic: The lost generations are Yuan, Cheng, Ai, and Ping.

sentences.zh: 強臣謂王莽。

translations.literal: The strong minister means Wang Mang.
translations.idiomatic: The usurper is Wang Mang.

sentences.zh: 注[三]台閣謂尚書也。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Tai ge means the secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Tai ge denotes the imperial secretariat.

sentences.zh: 注[四]撓音火高反。

translations.literal: Comment 4 Rao is read huogao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Rao is read huogao.

sentences.zh: 注[五]瘼，病也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 Mo means illness or wasting.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Mo means wasting sickness.

sentences.zh: 注[六]檢柙猶規矩也。

translations.literal: Comment 6 Jian xia means rules and measures.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 Jian xia means compass and square—rules.

sentences.zh: 注[七]展猶申也。

translations.literal: Comment 7 Zhan means to extend or assert.
translations.idiomatic: Note 7 Zhan means to press or assert.

sentences.zh: 文帝時，太中大夫鄧通居上傍，有怠慢禮，丞相申屠嘉奏事見之，罷朝，召通責之曰：「通小臣，戲殿上，大不敬，當斬。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen was on the throne, Palace Counselor Deng Tong sat beside the emperor with slack ritual; Chancellor Shen Tu Jia, having finished his memorial, saw it; after court he summoned Tong and rebuked him, saying, 'You, a petty clerk, sport on the hall—grave irreverence; you should be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Wen let Deng Tong lounge by the throne; Shen Tu Jia, leaving court, seized him: 'You play on the dais—death offense.

sentences.zh: 」通頓首，首盡出血。

translations.literal: Tong knocked his head until blood ran.
translations.idiomatic: Deng beat his head bloody.

sentences.zh: 文帝使人召通，謝丞相曰：「此吾弄臣，君其釋之。」

translations.literal: Emperor Wen sent for Tong and apologized to the chancellor, saying, 'This is my jester—release him, my lord.'"
translations.idiomatic: Wen intervened: 'He is my fool—let him go.'"

sentences.zh: 注[八]文帝時賈誼上書曰：「大臣有罪，不執縛係引而行也。

translations.literal: Comment 8 Jia Yi's memorial under Emperor Wen said, 'When great ministers are guilty, they are not bound and dragged away.
translations.idiomatic: Note 8 Jia Yi: disgraced ministers should not be hauled in ropes.

sentences.zh: 其有大罪者，聞命則北面再拜，跪而自裁，*

translations.literal: For the greatest crimes, on receiving the command they turn north, bow twice, kneel, and cut their own throats *
translations.idiomatic: For capital guilt they kneel north and open their own throats*

translations.literal: (themselves)
translations.idiomatic: the gloss '(themselves)' marks an editorial reading,

sentences.zh: **[上]*不使人捽抑而刑之也。

translations.literal: **[while]* the sovereign does not send men to seize and mutilate them.
translations.idiomatic: **so the throne need not send bailiffs to torture them.

sentences.zh: 」是時丞相絳侯周勃免就國，人有告勃謀反，系長安獄，卒無事，復爵邑，故誼以此譏上。

translations.literal: At that time Chancellor Marquis of Jiang Zhou Bo had been dismissed to his fief; someone accused him of plotting rebellion; he was bound in Chang'an prison, yet in the end nothing was proved and his title and towns were restored—therefore Yi used this to reproach the throne.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Bo was jailed on a false treason charge, then cleared—Jia Yi cited it to shame the court.

sentences.zh: 上深納其言，是後大臣有罪，皆自殺，不受刑也。

translations.literal: The emperor deeply accepted his words; thereafter great ministers who were guilty all killed themselves and did not suffer mutilation.
translations.idiomatic: Wen adopted the advice; later disgraced ministers fell on their swords.

sentences.zh: 注[九]言不以重利害其生。

translations.literal: Comment 9 It means not letting weighty harm endanger their lives.
translations.idiomatic: Note 9 Honor life over crushing humiliation.

sentences.zh: 事見莊子。

translations.literal: The story appears in the Zhuangzi.
translations.idiomatic: The parable appears in Zhuangzi.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]言光武奪三公重任，今奪更甚。

translations.literal: Comment 10 It means Guangwu took weight from the three dukes and today the taking is greater.
translations.idiomatic: Note 10 Guangwu hollowed the excellences; later courts hollowed them further.

sentences.zh: 光武不假後黨威權，數代遂不遵行。

translations.literal: Guangwu did not lend awesome power to empress kin, yet for generations it was not observed.
translations.idiomatic: He refused consort power, yet successors ignored him.

sentences.zh: 此為三公疏，後族親故也。

translations.literal: This is because the three dukes were kept distant while the empress clan was intimate.
translations.idiomatic: Excellences were remote; in-laws were close.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]病人謂萬姓困敝也。

translations.literal: Comment 11 'Sicken the people' means the myriad people are exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Note 11 'Sicken' means the masses are worn out.

sentences.zh: 或曰：政在一人，權甚重也。

translations.literal: Someone says: When government rests in one man, his power is very heavy.
translations.idiomatic: Critics say: one-man rule concentrates terrifying power.

sentences.zh: 曰：人實難得，何重之嫌？

translations.literal: The answer: Worthy men are truly hard to find—why grudge them weighty charge?
translations.idiomatic: He replies: true talent is scarce—why fear entrusting it with real power?

sentences.zh: 昔者霍禹、竇憲、鄧騭、梁冀之徒，籍外戚之權，管國家之柄；

translations.literal: In the past Huo Yu, Dou Xian, Deng Zhi, Liang Ji, and their kind, riding the power of affinal kin, held the state's handle;
translations.idiomatic: Huo Yu, Dou Xian, Deng Zhi, and Liang Ji used in-law clout to seize the levers of state;

sentences.zh: 及其伏誅，以一言之詔，詰朝而決，何重之畏乎？

translations.literal: when they were executed to the last man, a single edict settled it by the next dawn—what fear is there of 'too much weight'?
translations.idiomatic: when the axe fell, one edict cleared them by dawn—where was the danger of 'too much authority'?

sentences.zh: 今夫國家漏神明於媟近，輸權重於婦黨，筭十世而為之者八九焉。

translations.literal: Today the state drips its divine awesomeness into lewd proximity and pours heavy power into women's factions—eight or nine times out of ten this has gone on for ten generations.
translations.idiomatic: Today sovereignty leaks into bedchamber favorites and weight piles on consort clans—nine rulers in ten across ten reigns.

sentences.zh: 不此之罪而彼之疑，何其詭邪！

translations.literal: You blame not this crime yet doubt that one—how perverse!
translations.idiomatic: You scapegoat ministers yet spare the harem—how perverse!

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]此謂後黨，彼謂三公也。

translations.literal: [one] Comment 1 'This' means the empress faction; 'that' means the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note 1 'This' is the consort clan; 'that' the three excellences.

sentences.zh: 詭猶違也。

translations.literal: Gui means contrary or perverse.
translations.idiomatic: Gui means backwards or perverse.

sentences.zh: 論曰：百家之言政者尚矣。

translations.literal: The disquisition says: The hundred schools' talk of government reaches far back.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ye's judgment: political debate is ancient as the hundred schools.

sentences.zh: [一]大略歸乎寧固根柢，革易時敝也。

translations.literal: [one] In the main it returns to securing the root and trunk and mending the ills of the age.
translations.idiomatic: [1] At heart it means firming the foundation and curing each era's sickness.

sentences.zh: 夫遭運無恆，意見偏雜，故是非之論，紛然相乖。

translations.literal: For fate's turn has no constancy and opinions run partial and mixed, so judgments of right and wrong tangle and clash.
translations.idiomatic: Times change; views splinter; verdicts on policy cross and quarrel.

sentences.zh: 嘗試妄論之，[二]以為世非胥、庭，人乖鷇飲，化多萬肇，情故萌生。

translations.literal: I have ventured a rash discussion: [two] I hold that the age is not that of Xu and Ting, men stray from the quail's roost and the nestling's drink, transformation has ten thousand beginnings, and circumstance and habit breed fresh motives.
translations.idiomatic: I risk a thesis: [2] we are not in the age of Herxu's simplicity; men have left the quail-roost, nestling-drink purity; change spawns endlessly and habit breeds new motives.

sentences.zh: [三]雖周物之智，不能研其推變；

translations.literal: [three] Though one had the wisdom to know all things, he could not exhaust their shifting permutations;
translations.idiomatic: [3] Even omniscience cannot chart every permutation;

sentences.zh: 山川之奧，未足況其紆險。

translations.literal: the mystery of mountains and streams is not enough to compare to their deviousness.
translations.idiomatic: mountain secrets cannot match the maze of the human heart.

sentences.zh: [四]則應俗適事，難以常條。

translations.literal: [four] Thus answering the vulgar and suiting events cannot follow a single constant rule.
translations.idiomatic: [4] So policy must track the moment—no one rule fits all ages.

sentences.zh: 如使用審其道，則殊塗同會；

translations.literal: If employment matches the Way, many roads meet at one hub;
translations.idiomatic: Fit the man to the Way and divergent paths converge;

sentences.zh: 才爽其分，則一豪以乖。

translations.literal: if talent misses its station, a single hair's breadth throws all awry.
translations.idiomatic: misplace talent by a hair and the whole machine jams.

sentences.zh: [五] 何以言之？

translations.literal: [five] How shall I say it?
translations.idiomatic: [5] Why is this so?

sentences.zh: 若夫玄聖御世，則天同極，施捨之道，宜無殊典。

translations.literal: When the dark sage rules the age, he shares Heaven's utmost limit; in the path of setting up and casting down there ought to be no different canon.
translations.idiomatic: Under a hidden sage the pole aligns with Heaven; founding and ending dynasties should follow one principle.

sentences.zh: [六]而損益異運，文樸遞行。

translations.literal: [six] Yet loss and gain ride changing fortune, culture and plainness succeed each other.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Still each age adds or strips rite; refinement and plainness alternate.

sentences.zh: [七]用明居晦，回泬於曩時；

translations.literal: [seven] To use light while dwelling in darkness twists and plunges in former times;
translations.idiomatic: [7] Clever rule in dark times zigzagged in the past;

sentences.zh: 興戈陳俎，參差於上世。

translations.literal: to raise spears and set sacrificial stands varied in high antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: arms and altars took different shapes in high antiquity.

sentences.zh: [八]及至戴黃屋，服絺衣，豐薄不齊，而致化則一；

translations.literal: [eight] Then again, some don the yellow canopy and wear fine ramie—plenty and thrift differ, yet the teaching they reach is one;
translations.idiomatic: [8] Some ride the yellow-canopy carriage in ramie robes—rich or spare, their moral lesson converges;

sentences.zh: [九]亦有宥公族，黥國儲，寬慘巨隔，而防非必同。

translations.literal: [nine] Some pardon ducal kin and tattoo the heir apparent—lenience and cruelty stand poles apart, yet the guard against wrong is the same.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Some spare nobles, brand the crown prince—wide or harsh, the fence against evil matches.

sentences.zh: 此其分波而共源，百慮而一致者也。

translations.literal: This is their branching streams from one source, many thoughts with one outcome.
translations.idiomatic: They are tributaries of one stream, many plans with one end.

sentences.zh: [一０]若乃偏情矯用，則枉直必過。

translations.literal: [ten] Yet when partial feeling warps application, straight and crooked overshoot.
translations.idiomatic: [10] When bias twists practice, correction overshoots the mark.

sentences.zh: [一一]故葛屨履霜，敝由崇儉；

translations.literal: [eleven] Hence hemp sandals treading frost—ruin came from exalting thrift too far;
translations.idiomatic: [11] Hemp shoes on frost—misery from excessive thrift;

sentences.zh: [一二]楚楚衣服，戒在窮賒；

translations.literal: [twelve] bright 'chu-chu' robes—warning lay in endless credit on luxury;
translations.idiomatic: [12] Showy mayfly robes—danger from bottomless debt of splendor;

sentences.zh: [一三] □禁厚下，以尾大陵弱；

translations.literal: [thirteen] [lacuna] forbidding generosity below—through tails too large the weak were lorded over;
translations.idiomatic: [13] Checking inferiors while fattening vassals—tail-heavy lords crushed the throne;

sentences.zh: [一四]斂威峻罰，以苛薄分崩。

translations.literal: [fourteen] gathering awe and harsh punishments—through cruelty and meanness the realm split apart.
translations.idiomatic: [14] Hoarded terror and cruel law—Qin-style harshness shattered the state.

sentences.zh: [一五]斯曹、魏之刺，所以明乎國風；

translations.literal: [fifteen] Hence the barbs aimed at Cao and Wei clarify the 'Airs of the States';
translations.idiomatic: [15] Wei satires expose local manners;

sentences.zh: 周、秦末軌，所以彰於微滅。

translations.literal: the last tracks of Zhou and Qin display themselves in subtle extinction.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou and Qin's death-spasms show in their dying flicker.

sentences.zh: 故用捨之端，興敗資焉。

translations.literal: Therefore the hinge of use and refusal is where rise and ruin draw their capital.
translations.idiomatic: Use or discard talent—that is where fortune turns.

sentences.zh: 是以繁簡唯時，寬猛相濟。

translations.literal: Thus complexity and simplicity follow the times; lenience and severity must aid each other.
translations.idiomatic: Detail or simplicity must fit the hour; soft and hard must temper one another.

sentences.zh: 刑書鐫鼎，事有可詳；

translations.literal: When penal texts were cast on tripods, some matters deserved full detail;
translations.idiomatic: Tripod statutes spelled out what needed spelling;

sentences.zh: 三章在令，取貴能約。

translations.literal: when the three chapters stood in the command, value lay in the power to be brief.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bang's three-article code prized brevity.

sentences.zh: [一六]太叔致猛政之□，國子流遺愛之涕，[一七]宣孟改冬日之和，平陽循畫一之法。

translations.literal: [sixteen] Taishu's harsh rule brought [lacuna]; Guozi shed tears of lingering love; [seventeen] Xuan Meng altered the winter day's mildness; Pingyang kept the 'drawn-line' law.
translations.idiomatic: [16] Taishu ran harsh justice and paid for it; the people wept Zichan's kindness; [17] Zhao Dun changed winter's warmth; Cao Can kept one clear line for all.

sentences.zh: 斯實□張之弘致，可以征其統乎！

translations.literal: Such were the great reaches of tightening and loosening—may they verify the thread that binds!
translations.idiomatic: These are the grand rhythms of slack and taut—[lacuna]—do they not map the whole?

sentences.zh: [一八]數子之言當世失得皆究矣，然多謬通方之訓，好申一隅之說。

translations.literal: [eighteen] The several men's words exhaust the gains and losses of their age, yet they often mistake the teaching that fits every quarter and love to press a doctrine of one corner.
translations.idiomatic: [18] Wang Fu and friends read their times well yet often miss universal principle and preach one-sided cures.

sentences.zh: [一九]貴清靜者，以席上為腐議；

translations.literal: [nineteen] Those who prize stillness treat table talk as rotten argument;
translations.idiomatic: [19] Quietists dismiss policy talk as cant;

sentences.zh: 束名實者，以柱下為誕辭。

translations.literal: those who bind name to substance treat archive lore as empty chatter.
translations.idiomatic: Legalists mock archive subtlety as hollow.

sentences.zh: [二０]或推前王之風，可行於當年，有引救敝之規，宜流於長世。

translations.literal: [twenty] Some praise former kings' customs as feasible today; some cite rules to save present ills as fit to flow forever.
translations.idiomatic: [20] Some say ancient wind can be copied now; some say crisis remedies should bind all later ages.

sentences.zh: 稽之篤論，將為敝矣。

translations.literal: Checked against sober judgment, both turn stale.
translations.idiomatic: Tested by steady sense, both go wrong.

sentences.zh: 如以舟無推陸之分，瑟非常調之音，[二一]不陽局以疑遠，不拘玄以妨素，則化樞各管其極，理略可得而言與？

translations.literal: As a boat has no business on dry land nor a zither the tuning of a foreign mode—[twenty-one] if you do not bind the far with a foot-measure nor hobble the plain with dark mystery, then each pivot of transformation may hold its pole and the outline of principle can be spoken, can it not?
translations.idiomatic: [21] Boats belong on water, zithers to their key—do not measure the horizon with a foot-rule nor block plain dealing with occult theory; then each age's hinge keeps its balance and we may outline the Way—may we not?

sentences.zh: [二二] 注[一]尚猶遠也。

translations.literal: [twenty-two] Comment 1 Shang means 'far' or 'high.'
translations.idiomatic: [22] Note 1 Shang here means lofty or remote.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謙不敢正言也。

translations.literal: Comment 2 Modesty—dare not speak outright.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2 He speaks humbly.

sentences.zh: 注[三]赫胥氏、大庭氏並古之帝號。

translations.literal: Comment 3 Herxu and Greatting are both ancient imperial titles.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3 Herxu and Dating are archaic sage-kings.

sentences.zh: 莊子曰：「夫聖人鶉居而鷇飲。

translations.literal: The Zhuangzi says, 'The sage dwells like a quail and drinks like a nestling.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi: the sage nests like quail, sips like a fledgling.

sentences.zh: 」言鶉鳥無常居，鷇飲不假物，並淳樸時也。

translations.literal: It means quail have no fixed roost and nestlings drink without vessels—both images of unsophisticated times.
translations.idiomatic: Quail shift roosts; nestlings need no cup—images of primal simplicity.

sentences.zh: 肇，始也。

translations.literal: Zhao means beginning.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao means 'origin' or 'start.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]易系辭曰：「知周乎萬物而道濟天下。

translations.literal: Comment 4 The Changes, Appended Remarks, says, 'Knowledge extends to all things and the Way aids all under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4 Zhouyi Xici: knowledge circles the myriad things and the Way saves the world.

sentences.zh: 」推，遷也。

translations.literal: Tui means shift or change.
translations.idiomatic: Tui means transformation.

sentences.zh: 莊子曰「凡人心險於山川，難知於天」也。

translations.literal: The Zhuangzi says, 'The human heart is more precipitous than mountains and streams, harder to know than Heaven.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi: hearts hide more than mountains.'"

sentences.zh: 注[五]用得其人，審其道也。

translations.literal: Comment 5 When employment hits the right man, the Way is examined.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5 Right man, right Way.

sentences.zh: 授非其才，爽其分也。

translations.literal: When office meets the wrong talent, the allotment is missed.
translations.idiomatic: Wrong talent misses the fit.

sentences.zh: 易系辭曰：「天下同歸而殊塗，一致而百慮。

translations.literal: The Changes, Appended Remarks, says, 'All under Heaven share one return though the roads differ; they reach one goal though the cares are many.
translations.idiomatic: Xici: many roads, one return; many cares, one end.

sentences.zh: 」易緯曰：「差以毫釐，失之千里。」

translations.literal: The Changes weft says, 'Miss by a hair's width, err by a thousand li.'"
translations.idiomatic: Yi wei: a hair's miss loses a thousand li.'"

sentences.zh: 注[六]莊子曰：「玄聖，素王道也。

translations.literal: Comment 6 The Zhuangzi says, 'The dark sage is the way of the unadorned king.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6 Zhuangzi: 'dark sage' is the plain king's path.

sentences.zh: 」極猶致也。

translations.literal: Ji means utmost or arrival.
translations.idiomatic: Ji means the utmost reach.

sentences.zh: 言法天之道，同其致也。

translations.literal: It means modeling Heaven's Way and sharing its arrival.
translations.idiomatic: It means matching Heaven's pole.

sentences.zh: 施捨猶興廢也。

translations.literal: She she means establishing and casting down.
translations.idiomatic: She she means founding and ending policies.

sentences.zh: 注[七]論語孔子曰：「殷因於夏禮，所損益可知也。

translations.literal: Comment 7 The Analects, Confucius says, 'Yin took over Xia ritual—the losses and gains can be known.
translations.idiomatic: Note 7 Lunyu: Yin revised Xia ritual.

sentences.zh: 」樸，質也。

translations.literal: Pu means substance.
translations.idiomatic: Pu means plain substance.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰「文質再而復」也。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says, 'Culture and substance alternate twice and return.'"
translations.idiomatic: Liji: refinement and plainness cycle.'"

sentences.zh: 注[八]回泬猶攜互不齊一也。

translations.literal: Comment 8 Hui xue means carrying and mixing, uneven and not uniform.
translations.idiomatic: Note 8 Hui xue means jumbled, uneven practice.

sentences.zh: 泬音穴。

translations.literal: Xue is read xue.
translations.idiomatic: Xue is read xue (the graph).

sentences.zh: 注[九]前書音義曰：「天子車以黃繒為蓋裡，故曰黃屋。

translations.literal: Comment 9 The Former Han glossary says, 'The Son of Heaven's carriage uses yellow silk for the canopy lining—hence yellow canopy.
translations.idiomatic: Note 9 Yellow canopy: yellow silk lining the imperial roof.

sentences.zh: 」韓子曰：「堯之王天下也，冬日鹿裘，夏日葛衣。

translations.literal: Han Feizi says, 'When Yao ruled all under Heaven, in winter he wore deerskin, in summer ramie.
translations.idiomatic: Han Feizi: Yao wore fur in winter, ramie in summer.

sentences.zh: 」絺，葛也。

translations.literal: Chi is ramie.
translations.idiomatic: Chi is fine ramie cloth.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]禮記曰：「公族有死罪，獄成，有司讞於公曰『某之罪在大辟』，公曰『宥之』。

translations.literal: Comment 10 The Record of Rites says, 'When a ducal kinsman is guilty of a capital crime and the trial is complete, the officer reports to the duke, "So-and-so's crime is great execution." The duke says, "Pardon him."
translations.idiomatic: Note 10 Liji: thrice the duke pardons a kinsman's death sentence.

sentences.zh: 有司又曰『在大辟』，公又曰『宥之』。

translations.literal: The officer again says, 'It is great execution,' and the duke again says, 'Pardon him.'"
translations.idiomatic: The officer repeats; the duke repeats mercy.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰，秦孝公太子犯法，□鞅曰「太子君嗣也，不可施刑，刑其傅公子虔，黥其師公孫賈」也。

translations.literal: The Records say that when Duke Xiao of Qin's heir broke the law, Shang Yang said, 'The heir is the lord's successor and cannot be mutilated—punish his tutor Prince Qian and tattoo his instructor Gongsun Jia.'"
translations.idiomatic: Shiji: Shang Yang flogged the tutor and tattooed the instructor when the crown prince sinned.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]孟子曰：「矯枉過直。

translations.literal: Comment 11 Mencius says, 'Straightening the crooked overshoots the straight.
translations.idiomatic: Note 11 Mencius: correcting bend overshoots straight.

sentences.zh: 」矯，正也。

translations.literal: Jiao means to correct.
translations.idiomatic: Jiao means set straight.

sentences.zh: 枉，曲也。

translations.literal: Wang means bent.
translations.idiomatic: Wang means crooked.

sentences.zh: 言正曲者過於直，以喻為政者懲奢則太儉，患寬則傷猛，不能折衷也。

translations.literal: It means that he who straightens the crooked goes past the straight line—an analogy for rulers who punish luxury by extreme thrift or fear lenience by savage harshness and cannot strike the mean.
translations.idiomatic: The image: zealots overcorrect—too spare after luxury, too cruel after laxity—missing the middle.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]詩魏風序曰：「葛屨，刺褊也。

translations.literal: Comment 12 The preface to the Wei Airs in the Odes says, 'Ge ju satirizes meanness.
translations.idiomatic: Note 12 Wei feng preface: Ge ju attacks stinginess.

sentences.zh: 其君儉嗇褊急，而無德以將之。

translations.literal: Its lord was miserly, cramped, and urgent, lacking virtue to lead them.
translations.idiomatic: The marquis was tight-fisted and harsh.

sentences.zh: 」詩曰：「糾糾葛屨，可以履霜。

translations.literal: The poem says, 'Twisted twisted the hemp sandals—they may tread the frost.
translations.idiomatic: The ode: twisted hemp shoes on frost.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「葛屨賤，皮屨貴，魏俗至冬猶葛屨，可用履霜，利其賤也。」

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary says, 'Hemp sandals are base, leather shoes noble; Wei custom wore hemp even in winter—they could tread frost, prizing what was cheap.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan: Wei prized cheap hemp over warm leather.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一三]詩曹風序曰：「蜉蝣，刺奢也。

translations.literal: Comment 13 The preface to the Cao Airs says, 'Mayfly satirizes extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: Note 13 Cao feng: Mayfly attacks luxury.

sentences.zh: 」詩曰：「蜉蝣之羽，衣裳楚楚。

translations.literal: The poem says, 'Mayfly's wings—bright bright the robes and skirts.
translations.idiomatic: The poem: mayfly wings, gorgeous dress.

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「蜉蝣，渠略也。

translations.literal: Mao Chang's commentary says, 'Mayfly is the qu lü insect.
translations.idiomatic: Mao: mayfly is qu lü.

sentences.zh: 朝生夕死，猶有羽翼以自飾。

translations.literal: It is born in the morning and dies at evening, yet still has wings to adorn itself.
translations.idiomatic: It lives one day yet preens feathers.

sentences.zh: 楚楚，鮮魍也。

translations.literal: Chu chu means fresh and bright.
translations.idiomatic: Chu chu means glittering finery.

sentences.zh: 喻曹朝腢臣皆小人也。

translations.literal: It likens the courtiers of Cao—all petty men.
translations.idiomatic: It satirizes Cao's petty courtiers.

sentences.zh: 徒飾其衣裳，不知死亡之無日。

translations.literal: They only dress their robes and do not know death is near that very day.
translations.idiomatic: They dress up though sunset is hours away.

sentences.zh: 」賒奢同。

translations.literal: She is the same as she 'extravagance.'
translations.idiomatic: She reads as she, 'extravagance.'

sentences.zh: 注[一四]□禁謂防制太寬，厚下謂封建太廣。

translations.literal: Comment 14 [Lacuna] forbidding means guardrails too loose; generosity below means enfeoffment too wide.
translations.idiomatic: Note 14 Loose control plus fat vassals—Zhou's error.

sentences.zh: 言周室微弱而諸侯強盛，如尾大然。

translations.literal: It means the Zhou house was feeble while the feudatories grew strong—as when a tail grows too large.
translations.idiomatic: Weak Zhou, strong barons—tail too big to wag.

sentences.zh: 左傳楚申無宇曰「末大必折，尾大不掉」也。

translations.literal: Duke Shen Wuyu of Chu in the Zuo Commentary said, 'When the tip grows large it must snap; when the tail grows large it cannot wag.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zuo: big branches break; big tails cannot wag.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一五]斂，聚也。

translations.literal: Comment 15 Lian means to gather.
translations.idiomatic: Note 15 Lian means amass.

sentences.zh: 言秦酷法，以至分崩也。

translations.literal: It means Qin's cruel laws led to partition and collapse.
translations.idiomatic: Qin's cruelty split the realm.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]左傳曰：「鄭人鑄刑書。

translations.literal: Comment 16 The Zuo Commentary says, 'The people of Zheng cast penal writings.
translations.idiomatic: Note 16 Zuo: Zheng cast penal tripods.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云「鑄刑書於鼎，以為國之常法」也。

translations.literal: Du Yu's commentary says, 'They cast the penal text on a tripod to be the state's constant law.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu: bronze law code.'"

sentences.zh: 高祖初入關，除秦苛法，約法三章，言其詳約不同。

translations.literal: When Emperor Gao first entered the passes he abolished Qin's harsh laws and contracted the law to three chapters—showing how detail and brevity differ.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu replaced Qin detail with three articles—full versus spare code.

sentences.zh: 注[一七]左傳曰：「鄭子產有疾，謂子大叔曰：『我死，子必為政。

translations.literal: Comment 17 The Zuo Commentary says, 'Zichan of Zheng fell ill and told Grandee Taishu, "When I die you must govern.
translations.idiomatic: Note 17 Zuo: dying Zichan tells Taishu to take office.

sentences.zh: 唯有德者能以寬服人，其次莫如猛。

translations.literal: Only the virtuous can win men with lenience; the next best is severity.
translations.idiomatic: Only the virtuous govern with lenience; the next best is severity.

sentences.zh: 』」又曰：「子產卒，仲尼聞之，出涕曰：『古之遺愛也。

translations.literal: It also says, 'When Zichan died, Confucius heard it, went out weeping, and said, "He was the lingering love of antiquity."'
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo adds that Confucius wept Zichan was antiquity's lingering kindness.

sentences.zh: 』」國子即子產也，鄭穆公子國之子，因以為姓也。

translations.literal: Guozi is Zichan—son of Guo, a son of Duke Mu of Zheng—hence he took Guo as surname.
translations.idiomatic: Guozi is Zichan, scion of Zheng's ducal house.

sentences.zh: 注[一八]宣孟，晉大夫趙盾也。

translations.literal: Comment 18 Xuan Meng is Jin grandee Zhao Dun.
translations.idiomatic: Note 18 Xuan Meng is Zhao Dun of Jin.

sentences.zh: 左傳賈季對酆舒曰：「趙衰，冬日之日也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary: Jia Ji said to Feng Shu, 'Zhao Cui is like the sun in winter.
translations.idiomatic: In the Zuo, Jia Ji tells Feng Shu that Zhao Cui is the winter sun—

sentences.zh: 趙盾，夏日之日也。

translations.literal: Zhao Dun is like the sun in summer.'
translations.idiomatic: —and Zhao Dun the summer sun.'

sentences.zh: 」注云：「冬日可愛，夏日可畏。

translations.literal: The commentary says, 'The winter sun is lovable; the summer sun is dreadful.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss adds: winter's warmth invites love; summer's blaze inspires fear.

sentences.zh: 」前書平陽侯曹參為相國，百姓歌之曰：「蕭何為法，講若畫一。

translations.literal: The Former Han records that when Marquis of Pingyang Cao Can served as chancellor, the people sang, 'Xiao He made the laws, bright as a single drawn line.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Can was chief minister the folk sang that Xiao He's code was clear as one ruled line;

sentences.zh: 曹參代之，守而勿失。

translations.literal: Cao Can replaced him and kept them without losing them.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Can succeeded him and changed nothing;

sentences.zh: 載其清靜，人以寧一。」

translations.literal: He carried on his quiet rule and the people were unified in peace.'"
translations.idiomatic: he carried on the quiet style until the realm rested easy.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一九]一隅謂一方偏見也。

translations.literal: Comment 19 'One corner' means a one-sided bias.
translations.idiomatic: Note 19 'One corner' is partisan dogma.

sentences.zh: 注[二０]清靜謂道家也。

translations.literal: Comment 20 'Quiet stillness' refers to the Daoist school.
translations.idiomatic: Note 20 'Quiet stillness' denotes Daoist teaching.

sentences.zh: 席上謂儒也。

translations.literal: Xishang refers to Confucians.
translations.idiomatic: Xishang means the scholar at the mat—the Confucian.

sentences.zh: 腐，朽也。

translations.literal: Fu means rotten.
translations.idiomatic: Fu means rotten or stale.

sentences.zh: 禮記儒行曰：「儒有席上之珍。

translations.literal: The Record of Rites, 'Conduct of the Scholar,' says, 'The ru has treasures on the mat.
translations.idiomatic: The Liji Ruxing says the scholar brings treasures to the feast of learning.

sentences.zh: 」高祖折隨何曰：「安用腐儒哉。

translations.literal: Emperor Gao snapped at Sui He, 'What use have I for rotten ru?'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Gao sneered at Sui He, 'Why need I stale pedants?'

sentences.zh: 」名實，名家也。

translations.literal: Ming shi means the school of names.
translations.idiomatic: Ming shi is the Legalist logician school.

sentences.zh: 柱下，老子也。

translations.literal: Zhuxia means Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuxia names Laozi as archivist.

sentences.zh: 誕，虛也。

translations.literal: Dan means empty.
translations.idiomatic: Dan means hollow or inflated.

sentences.zh: 言志各不同也。

translations.literal: It means their aims each differed.
translations.idiomatic: Each school pursued a different end.

sentences.zh: 注[二一]古法不施於今，猶舟不可行之於陸也。

translations.literal: Comment 21 Ancient methods cannot be applied to the present, just as boats cannot be pushed on dry land.
translations.idiomatic: Note 21 Antique codes do not fit modern soil—like barging a boat overland.

sentences.zh: 今法有合於時，如瑟可移柱而調也。

translations.literal: Present law that fits the times is like moving the zither's bridges to tune it.
translations.idiomatic: Timely statute is like shifting bridges on a zither to find the key.

sentences.zh: 莊子曰「是推舟於陸，勞而無功」也。

translations.literal: The Zhuangzi says, 'This is pushing a boat on land—toil without gain.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi: hauling a hull across fields—labor without profit.

sentences.zh: 前書董仲舒曰：「琴瑟不調，甚者必解而更張之，乃可鼓也。

translations.literal: The Former Han records Dong Zhongshu as saying, 'When zither and se are badly out of tune, you must loosen strings and retune before they can be played.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu wrote that when lute and zither jar, you slack and retune before playing;

sentences.zh: 為政不行，甚者必變而更化之，乃可理也。」

translations.literal: When government will not go, the worst cases must be changed and transformed before they can be ordered.'"
translations.idiomatic: when policy fails, the worst knots demand reform before order returns.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二二]音余。

translations.literal: Comment 22 It is read yu.
translations.idiomatic: Note 22 The graph is read yu.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：管視好偏，腢言難一。

translations.literal: Encomium: The bamboo tube's view loves bias; many voices are hard to unify.
translations.idiomatic: The eulogy: narrow sight breeds partiality; many tongues rarely agree;

sentences.zh: 救樸雖文，矯□必疾。

translations.literal: Saving plainness with pattern is swift; correcting [lacuna] must be keen.
translations.idiomatic: plain rescue needs polish; correcting excess must be swift;

sentences.zh: 舉端自理，滯隅則失。

translations.literal: Raise the main thread and order follows; stick to a corner and you miss.
translations.idiomatic: grasp the root and things align; cling to one corner and you stumble;

sentences.zh: 詳觀時蠹，成昭政術。

translations.literal: Study closely the age's corruption and the arts of rule stand clear.
translations.idiomatic: read the rot of the times and governance shows plain.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]滯隅謂偏執一隅也。

translations.literal: [one] Comment 1 Zhiyu means stubbornly holding one corner.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Note 1 Zhiyu is obstinate one-sidedness.

sentences.zh: 淮南子曰：「非循一跡之路，守一隅之指，而不與俗推移也。」
